7

High Country Home  Health,  Inc.  v.  Thompson,  359 F.3d
1307,  1311  (10th  Cir.  2004).°

2.  Respondents  repeatedly  describe  the  regulatory
regime,  first  adopted  in 1974,  as  a “one-way  ratchet”  or
a “one-way  tolling  rule.”  Br.  34, 50, 51, 55.  Contrary  to
respondents’  contention,  however,  the limited  opportuni-
ties  for  additional  administrative  review  after  the  expi-
ration  of 180  days  are  largely  neutral.

The  good-cause  regulation,  allowing  limited  exten-
sions  of the appeal  deadline,  inures  only to the benefit  of
providers.  The  reopening  regulation,  for  its  part,  is
largely neutral.  42 C.F.R.  405.1885(a)  (allowing reopen-
ing “on  motion”  of the intermediary,  the  Board,  the  Sec-
retary,  or  the provider).  Reopening  may remedy under-
payments  or  result  in  the  recovery  of  overpayments.
Your  Home  Visiting  Nurse  Servs.,  Inc.  v.  Shalala,  525
U.S.  449,  455-456  (1999)  (Your  Home).  And  while  pro-
viders  cannot  seek  discretionary  reopening  after  three
years,  neither  can  the  intermediary,  the  Board,  or  the
Secretary.  42 C.F.R.  405.1885(a)  (“No  such  determina-
tion  or  decision  may be reopened  after  such  3-year  peri-
od”);  42 C.F.R.  405.1885(b)  (requiring  reopening  in cer-
tain  circumstances  if “within  the 3-year  period”).  Thus,
an  intermediary  determination  or  Board  decision  could
not be reopened  under  the regulations  after  three  years
if the  Secretary  discovered  that errors  in calculating  the
SSI  fraction  had  overstated  hospitals’  DSH  payments.
The  opportunity  for  a  hospital  to  seek  an  extension  for
taking  an  appeal  for  good  cause,  or  to  seek  reopening,
within  three  years  is a  manifestation  of the  Secretary’s

*  Approximately  80%  of the  hospitals  paid under  the  Prospective
Payment  System  (PPS)  receive  DSH  payments.  See  77  Fed.  Reg.
53,721-53,724  (2012)  (3423  PPS  hospitals,  including  1903  urban  and
820  rural  DSH  hospitals).

8

“grace,”  Your  Home,  525  U.S.  at  454,  not  a  one-way
ratchet—particularly  not one  that favors  the  Secretary.
The  only example  of the  purported  inequity  that  re-
spondents  identify  is  42  C.F.R.  405.1885(d),  which  re-
quires  reopening  “at  any  time  if it is  established  that
[the] determination  or decision  was  procured  by fraud  or
similar  fault  of any  party  to the  determination  or  deci-
sion.”  The  so-called  “fraud”  exception  to  the  absolute
three-year  deadline  for reopening  is limited  in scope  and
rarely  used.  CMS  has  taken  the  position  that  the  term
“established”  requires  an  independent  administrative  or
judicial  finding  of fraud  or  similar  fault  separate  from
any  reopening,  such  as  in a False  Claims  Act  case.  See,
e.g.,  Gov’t  Mot.  to  Dismiss  or  Summ.  J.  at  22,  Bradley
Mem 'l Hosp.  v.  Thompson,  1:04-cv-416  Docket  entry
(D.D.C.  July 30, 2004).  A regulatory  regime  that  allows
the  agency  to  recover  overpayments  in  those  circum-
stances  based  on  fraud  should  not  “shock[]”  this  Court,
which  was  willing to countenance  “a system  in which  un-
derpayments  could  never  be  the  basis  for  reopening.”
Your  Home,  525  U.S.  at 455.’

3.  Respondents’  insistence  that  somehow  Congress
promised  them  the payments  they seek  in this  litigation,
no  matter  how  long after  the fact,  is equally  unfounded.
E.g.,  Br.  57-58.  When  a provider  chooses  to participate
in  the  Medicare  program,  its  payments  depend  on  the
Medicare  Act and  the Secretary’s  implementing  regula-
tions,  which  require  providers  to comply  with  specified
procedures  in order  to receive  payment.  A provider  that

”  Contrary  to respondents’  contention  (Br.  10, 50), the fraud  excep-
tion  applies  equally  to a fiscal  intermediary  if it “procured”  a Board
decision  by  “fraud  or  similar  fault.”  A  fiscal  intermediary  is  a
“part[y]”  to the Board  hearing,  42  C.F .R. 405.1843(a),  even  though  it
is not  a “part{[y]”  to  its own  determination,  42  C.F.R.  405.1805.

9

fails  to  comply  with  those  procedures  is not  entitled  to
payment  any  more  than  a  taxpayer  who  fails  to  seek  a
refund  within  a specified  period  of time.  Respondents’
characterization  of the payments  allegedly  due  as  “fully
funded”  and  “set aside”  by Congress,  id.  at 57, when  the
suit  is based  on  cost  reports  that  have  been  closed  for  a
decade  or  more,  blinks  reality.  Congress  did  not  prom-
ise or  set aside  money  to pay  Medicare  providers  in 2012
for services  rendered  in 1987  in a case  not  appealed  until
2006.

B.  Respondents’  Arguments  For  Ignoring  Well-Established
Principles  of Administrative  Law  Are  Unpersuasive
Respondents  do not dispute  that,  through  notice-and-
comment  rulemaking  contemporaneous  with  the  enact-
ment  of  Section  139500(a)(3)  nearly  40  years  ago,  the
Secretary  determined  that the  Board  has no  authority  to
hear  appeals  filed  beyond  the  180-day  deadline,  except
as  provided  by regulation—.e.,  for good  cause  shown  if
the  request  is filed  within  three  years  of the  NPR.  Re-
spondents  also  do  not  dispute  that  an  equitable-tolling
regime  would  conflict  with  the  limitations  in the  Secre-
tary’s  regulations.  Respondents  nevertheless  contend
that  settled  principles  of administrative  law  precluding
courts  from  imposing  extra-statutory  procedural  re-
quirements  on  an  administrative  tribunal,  see  Vermont
Yankee  Nuclear  Power  Corp.  v.  NRDC,  4385  U.S.  519
(1978)  (Vermont  Yankee),  and  from  setting  aside  a duly
promulgated  regulation  that  is neither  arbitrary  nor  ca-
pricious,  should  not apply here.  Respondents  fail to con-
front  the  force  of this  Court’s  precedents.

1.  The  government’s  opening  brief  argues  at length
(Br.  17-29)  that  Vermont  Yankee  prohibits  courts  from
unilaterally  imposing  on  agencies  additional  procedural
rules  not  required  by statute,  and  that  the  equitable-

10

tolling  regime  adopted  by  the  court  of  appeals  here
would  do precisely  that.  Respondents  devote  three  par-
agraphs  to arguing that  Vermont  Yankee  is “inapt.”  Br.
54-55.  Contrary  to  respondents’  suggestion,  Vermont
Yankee  is not  limited  to  an  agency’s  rulemaking  proce-
dures,  nor  to  an  analysis  of the  procedures  imposed  by
the  Administrative  Procedure  Act  (APA).  See Pension
Benefit  Guar.  Corp.  v.  LTV Corp.,  496  U.S.  633, 653-656
(1990)  (applying  Vermont  Yankee  to informal  agency  ad-
judication  where  no  provision  in  governing  statute  or
APA  afforded  asserted  procedural  rights).  Rather,  as
this  Court  explained  in Vermont  Yankee,  “[e]ven  apart
from  the  [APA]  this  Court  has  for  more  than  four  dec-
ades  emphasized  that the formulation  of procedures  was
basically  to be left within  the  discretion  of the  agencies
to  which  Congress  had  confided  the  responsibility  for
substantive  judgments.”  435  U.S.  at  524;  see  FCC  v.
Pottsville  Broad.  Co.,  309  U.S.  134,  143  (1940).

2.  Respondents  make  no  attempt  to  argue  that  the
Secretary’s  good-cause  regulation  for  an  extension  of
the appeal  deadline,  including  the three-year  outer  limit,
is arbitrary  or  capricious.  Gov’t  Br.  29-31.  Neither  do
respondents  dispute  that the good-cause  regulation  falls
squarely  within  the  Secretary’s  broad  rulemaking  au-
thority.  Jd. at 18.  Respondents  do contend  (Br.  54) that
Congress  would  not  have  delegated  authority  to  the
Secretary  to avoid  performing  administrative  review  re-
quired  by Congress,  but  the  substance  of respondents’
objection  is  not  clear.  If they  are  suggesting  that  the
Secretary  has  no  authority  to interpret  the  Board’s  ad-
judicatory  jurisdiction,  the Court  held  otherwise  in Your
Home.  Gov't  Br.  50-51;  id.  at  49-50  (citing  cases).  If
they  are  merely  suggesting  that  the  Secretary  has  no
authority  to reject open-ended  equitable  tolling because

11

See  Part  C,  infra.

in their view Congress  mandated  it, their  nondelegation
argument  collapses  into  a question  of statutory  interpre-
tation.
On  its  face,  Section
139500(a)(3)  provides  for Board  review  only “if,” among
other  things,  the provider  files  a hearing  request within
180  days.  42  U.S.C.  139500(a)(3)  (emphasis  added).  If
there  were  any  conflict  between  the  good-cause  regula-
tion  and  the  plain  language  of the  statute,  it would  be
with  the  Secretary’s  allowance  of any  extension  of the
180-day  period,  not  her  failure  to  adopt  an  amorphous
equitable-tolling  regime  that  has  had  no  place  in  the
Medicare  provider  payment  program  for the  entire  his-
tory  of its  existence.  See  Court-Appointed  Amicus  Br.
41-47.

C.  Congress  Did Not  Mandate  Open-Ended  Equitable  Toll-

ing Of The  180-Day  Appeal  Period

Respondents  argue  that  Chevron  deference  is  not
warranted  because  Section  139500(a)(8)  is not  ambigu-
ous.  Br.  51-52.  That  argument  rests  not  on  the  statuto-
ry text,  which  says  nothing about  equitable  tolling,  or  on
generally  applicable  tools  of  statutory  interpretation.
Instead,  respondents  rely  exclusively  on  the  presump-
tion  in favor  of equitable  tolling  that  this  Court  adopted
in Irwin  v.  Department  of Veterans  Affairs,  498  U.S.  89,
95-96  (1990),  for  a  statute  of  limitations  applicable  to
suits  filed  in  court.  See  Br.  52  (“[A]pplication  of  the
presumption  eliminates  any  ambiguity  for the agency  to
interpret.”).  No  such  presumption  applies  here.  But
even  if a  presumption  applied  in some  form,  the  Secre-
tary’s  implementation  of Section  139500(a)(3)  is sound
and  respondents  cannot  prevail.

1.  a.  This  Court  has  never  applied  the  Jrwin  pre-
sumption  to impose  equitable  tolling  on  internal  agency
review  procedures.  Gov’t  Br.  32-36.  Neither  case  cited

12

by respondents  (Br.  44) applied  a presumption  and  each
is inapposite  for other  reasons.  The government’s  open-
ing  brief  (Br.  33-34)  explains  in  detail  why  Zipes  v.
Trans  World Airlines,  Inc.,  455  U.S.  385 (1982),  is readi-
ly distinguishable.  Union  Pacific  R.R.  v.  Sheehan,  439
U.S.  89, 92 (1978)  (per curiam),  is even  further  removed
because  the  Court  did  nothing  more  than  explain  that,
as  a  matter  of fact,  the  National  Railroad  Adjustment
Board  had considered  the railroad  employee’s  claim  that
the  applicable  time  deadline  (in a  collective-bargaining
agreement)  should  be  equitably  tolled.

Respondents  contend  that  it  would  “make[]  little
sense”  to  forgo  a  “statutory  analysis”  where  “the  time
period  at issue”  is “for  administrative,  rather  than judi-
cial,  review.”  Br.  42.  That  misapprehends  the  govern-
ment’s  argument.  If Congress  intends  a statutory  time
limit  to  be  subject  to  equitable  tolling,  that  intent  of
course  controls.  Before  its  decision  in Jrwin,  the  Court
used  traditional  tools  of statutory  interpretation  to de-
termine  whether  equitable  tolling  of a  suit-filing  time
limit  implicating  the  Federal  Treasury  would  be  “con-
sistent  with  Congress’  intent,”  Bowen  v.  City  of N.Y.,
476  U.S.  467,  480  (1986).  Gov’t  Br.  40-41.  In the admin-
istrative  context,  traditional  tools  of statutory  construc-
tion  entail  application  of the rules  governing judicial  re-
view  of  an  agency  action  or  interpretation,  which  are
drawn  from  the  APA,  Chevron,  and  Vermont  Yankee.
Where  Congress  has  expressly  granted  authority  to an
agency  to  promulgate  rules  and  procedures  to  govern
the  administrative  appeals  process,  it  makes  “little
sense”  (Resp.  Br.  42) to presume  that  Congress  intend-
ed the  Judiciary  to adopt  freestanding  equitable  excep-
tions  to  statutory  and  regulatory  deadlines  and  other
Far
prerequisites  to  internal  administrative  review.

13

from  “a realistic  assessment  of legislative  intent,”  id.  at
42 (quoting  /rwin,  498  U.S.  at 95),  incantation  of a pre-
sumption  would  redistribute  the  authority  granted  by
Congress  from  the  expert  agency  to  the  courts.  See
Pottsville  Broad.,  309  U.S.  at  144  (cautioning  against
reading  “the  laws  of  Congress  through  the  distorting
lenses  of inapplicable  legal  doctrine”);  id.  at  141  (“[T]o
the extent  that  a federal  court  is authorized  to review  an
administrative  act,  there  is superimposed  upon  the  en-
forcement  of  legislative  policy  through  administrative
control  a  different  process  from  that  out  of which  the
administrative  action  under  review  ensued.”).

For these  reasons,  respondents’  assertion  that  “equi-
table  tolling  by an  agency  adjudicator”  is “the  rule,  not
the  exception,”  Br.  43,  proves  very  little.  There  are
many  instances  in  which  the  administering  agency
agrees  that equitable  tolling should  be available,  or  that
some  other  exception  to  finality  should  be  allowed,  at
least  in certain  circumstances.  Indeed,  here,  the  Secre-
tary  has  done  so  by adopting  the  regulations  allowing
reopening  and  extension  of the appeal  deadline,  subject
to the three-year  limitation.  There  may  also  be instanc-
es  in which  the  administering  agency  has declined  to al-
low equitable  tolling,  but a contrary  congressional  intent
is clear.  A presumption  in  favor  of equitable  tolling  is
not  needed  to  identify  those  cases.*

*  The courts  of appeals  cases  cited by respondents  (Br. 43 n.16)  are
distinguishable.  Jackson  v.  Astrue,  506  F.3d  1349,  1352-1353  (11th
Cir.  2007),  did not  involve  an  administrative  deadline,  but rather  the
same judicial  review  deadline  at issue  in City of New  York.  In several
other  cases,  the  administering  agency  agreed  that  equitable  tolling
was  available.  Hill  v.  Merit  Sys.  Prot.  Bd.,  No.  2012-3024,  2012  WL
1663002,  at *3 (Fed.  Cir.  May  14, 2012)  (discussing  equitable  tolling
of administrative  complaint deadline which  government  conceded was
available  in prior  case,  see  Kirkendall  v.  Department  of Army,  479

14

b.  To  the  extent  respondents  argue  that  this  Court
ean  apply  /rwin’s  presumption  without  identifying  a
“precise  private  equivalent,”  Resp.  Br.  45-46,  the  gov-
ernment  does  not  disagree,  Gov’t  Br.  38.  But  respond-
ents’  reliance  on  Holland  v.  Florida,  130  S.  Ct.  2549,
2560-2562  (2010),  to suggest  that  they need  not  identify
any  private  analogue  is misplaced.  As  the  government
explains  in its opening  brief  (Br.  39 & n.18),  Holland,  a
state  habeas  corpus  case,  did  not  require  the  Court  to
identify  any  private  analogue  because  habeas  (quite  un-
like  statutory  payments  under  Medicare)  has  historical-
ly been  governed  by equitable  principles,  and  because
Holland  was  not  a  suit  against  the  United  States,
let  alone  one  implicating  the  heightened  sovereign-
immunity  principles  that  govern  when  a  party  seeks
money  from  the  Federal  Treasury.  Respondents  also
cite  City  of New  York,  476  U.S.  at  480,  and  Honda  v.
Clark,  386  U.S.  484,  500-501  (1967),  but  Honda  did  not

F.3d 830, 837 (Fed.  Cir.) (en banc), cert.  denied,  552  U.S.  948 (2007));
Ajalla  v.  White,  81  Fed.  Appx.  715,  719  (10th  Cir.  2003)  (29 C.F.R.
1614.604(c)  expressly  provides  for  equitable  tolling  of regulatory
deadline);  Von Eye v.  United  States,  92 F.3d 681, 684 (8th Cir.  1996);
White  v.  Bentsen,  31  F.3d  474,  475  (7th  Cir.  1994),  cert.  denied,  514
U.S.  1050  (1995);  School  Dist.  of Allentown  v. Marshall,  657 F.2d  16,
18-19  (3d  Cir.  1981);  cf.  Ramirez  v.  Secretary,  686  F.3d  1239,  1243
(11th Cir.  2012) (regulation  provides for mandatory extension  in spec-
ified circumstances).  In one  case  where  the government  argued that
equitable  tolling was  unavailable,  the  defendant  agency  did  not ask
for deference  in the court of appeals.  Kirkendall,  479 F.3d at 836-844
(veterans  discrimination  appeal).  The other two  cases  involved  time
limits  for  motions  to  reopen  immigration  removal  proceedings.
Albillo-De  Leon v. Gonzales,  410 F.3d  1090,  1095-1098  (9th Cir. 2005);
lavorski  v. INS, 232 F.3d  124, 129-134  (2d Cir. 2000) (Sotomayor,  J.).
The  reopening  statutes  and  regulations  there,  however,  have  been
read by some  courts  to suggest flexibility,  e.g.,  id.  at 130-132,  and re-
moval  proceedings  do not involve  sophisticated  institutional  litigants.

15

involve  payments  from  the  general  Treasury,  and  both
decisions  predated  /rwin  and  therefore  looked  to  con-
gressional  intent  without  applying  any  presumption  in
favor  of equitable  tolling.

ce.  If a presumption  in favor  of equitable  tolling were
to  apply  at all  in this  context,  it would  be  substantially
weakened  because  equitable  principles  have  not  tradi-
tionally  governed  Medicare  provider  payments,  and  be-
cause  the  180-day  administrative  appeal  period  was  en-
acted  18  years  before  this  Court’s  decision  in  /rwin.
Gov’t  Br. 39-40.  Respondents’  answer  is to describe  the
Board’s  powers  as  “flexible.”  Br.  46.  But the only “flex-
ibility”  respondents  point  to  is  exprevaly  set  forth  by
statute;  the  Board  does  not  have  any  inherent  equitable
powers  outside  those  prescribed  by the  Medicare  Act
and  the  Secretary’s  implementing  regulations.  Gov't  Br.
20-22.

2.  Even  if the  Jrwin  presumption  had  some  applica-
tion,  there  are  “good  reason(s]  to believe  that  Congress
did  not  want  the  equitable  tolling  doctrine  to  apply[.]”
Brockamp,  519  U.S.  at 350.

a.  For  nearly  40 years,  the  Secretary  has  prohibited
the  Board  from  extending  the  180-day  deadline,  except
as  provided  by regulation.  Until  the  decision  below,  no
court  had ever  superimposed  equitable-tolling  principles
onto  that statutory  and regulatory  scheme.  Against that
backdrop,  Congress  amended  Section  139500  on  six sep-
arate  occasions  and  never  expressed  any disapproval  of
the  Secretary’s  interpretation.  Gov’t  Br.  20 & n.8.  “It is
well  established  that  when  Congress  revisits  a  statute
giving rise  to a longstanding  administrative  interpreta-
tion without  pertinent  change,  the ‘congressional  failure
to revise  or  repeal  the agency’s  interpretation  is persua-
sive  evidence  that the interpretation  is the  one  intended

16

by Congress.’”  CFTC  v.  Schor,  478  U.S.  833,  845-846
(1986)  (citation  omitted).

the

has

treated

Secretary

Respondents  do  not  dispute  any  of this  history.  In-
deed,  they rely on  it to argue  that  the  180-day  adminis-
trative  appeal  deadline  is not  jurisdictional.  Resp.  Br.
17-18,  34.  According  to  respondents,  “for  nearly  four
decades,
Section
139500(a)(3)’s  time  limit as  nonjurisdictional”  and  “Con-
gress’s  longstanding  acceptance  of  (a)(3)’s  nonjuris-
dictional  status  merits  *  *  *  respect.”  Br.  34.°  It is
true  that  the  Secretary  has  not  treated  Section
139500(a)(3)’s  time  limit  itself  as  “jurisdictional”  in the
sense  that she could  not  allow for any extension  by regu-
lation.  But  from  the  outset  the  Secretary  has  imposed
strict  limitations  on  such  extensions.  Congress’s  long-
standing  acceptance  of the Secretary’s  treatment  of Sec-
tion  139500(a)(3)  thus  necessarily includes  acceptance  of
those  strict  limitations,  which  are  incompatible  with  the
equitable  tolling  respondents  seek.

*  Respondents  contend  that  “for  nearly  forty  years,  most  courts
and the Executive  Branch’s  published  regulations  have  treated”  the
180-day  limit  “as  nonjurisdictional  and  subject  to  equitable  excep-
tions.”  Br. 23 n.6.  That is incorrect.  First,  two of the three  courts  of
appeals  to consider  the issue concluded  that the time limit is jurisdic-
tional  in the strictest  sense  and that even  the Secretary’s  regulation
allowing  extensions  for good cause  is invalid.  Compare  St.  Joseph's
Hosp.  v. Heckler,  786  F.2d 848, 851-853  (8th Cir.  1986),  and Alacare
Home  Health  Servs.,  Inc.  v.  Sullivan,  891  F.2d  850,  856  (11th  Cir.
1990),  with  Western  Med.  Enters.  v.  Heckler,  783  F.2d  1376,  1379-
1380  (9th  Cir.  1986).  Second,  during  that  40-year  time  frame,  no
court  ever  recognized  an  “equitable”  exception  to the statutory  and
regulatory  appeal deadline.  Third, the Secretary’s  “published  regula-
tions”  likewise  never  recognized  any “equitable”  exceptions.  See pp.
19-20,  infra.

17

b.  Nothing  about  the  60-day  deadline  set  forth  in 42
U.S.C.  139500(f)(1)  for seeking judicial  review  suggests
otherwise.  Resp.  Br.  41-42.  Even  assuming  that  time
limit  is subject  to  equitable  tolling,"  it does  not  follow
that  the  administrative  time  limit  for seeking  Board  re-
view  must  also  be subject  to tolling that  the  Secretary’s
regulations  do not  allow.  A provider  is entitled  to judi-
cial  review  only if it has satisfied  the statutory  precondi-
tions,  which  include  securing  a  “final  decision  of  the
Board.”  42  U.S.C.  139500(f)(1).  As  relevant  here,  a
provider  may  obtain  a  hearing  before  the  Board  (and,
thus,  a final  decision)  only if “such  provider”  is dissatis-
fied with the intermediary’s  payment  determination;  the
amount  in  controversy  is  $10,000  or  more;  and  the  re-
quest is filed  within  180 days.  42 U.S.C.  139500(a).  Pur-
suant  to  that  statutory  scheme,  if the  provider  is  not
“dissatisfied,”  or  if the  amount  in  controversy  is  less
than  $10,000,  the  provider  is not  entitled  to any judicial
review  of the intermediary’s  determination.  The same  is
true  of  a  provider  who  fails  to  file  a  hearing  request
within  the  period  specified  by Section  139500(a)  and  the
Secretary’s  regulations.

There  is another,  more  practical  reason  to distinguish
the  judicial  review  deadline  in  Section  139500(f)(1)
from  the  administrative  appeal  deadline  in  Section

©  The Secretary  has argued  that the 60-day judicial  review  period
in  Section  139500(f)(1)  is not  subject  to  equitable  exceptions.  The
courts  have  reached  different  conclusions.  Compare  Lloyd Noland
Hosp.  & Clinic  v.  Heckler,  762  F.2d  1561,  1564-1565  (11th  Cir.  1985)
(subject  to waiver  and  equitable  tolling),  and  Bedford  Cnty.  Mem’l
Hosp.  v.  Heckler,  583  F. Supp.  367, 370-371  (W.D.  Va.  1984)  (subject
to waiver),  aff’d,  769  F.2d  1017  (4th  Cir.  1985),  with  Wilmac  Corp.  v.
Thompson,  No. 03-5936,  2007 WL 2599238,  at *2-*3  (E.D.  Pa. Sept. 4,
2007)  (not subject to equitable  tolling),  and Kidney Ctr.  v. Shalala,  63
F.  Supp.  2d 51, 55-57  (D.D.C.  1999)  (not subject  to waiver).

18

139500(a)(3).  The universe  of reasons  for invoking  equi-
table  tolling of the 60-day judicial  review  deadline  would
be  quite  limited.  Once  a  provider  has  “notice”  that  a
final  administrative  decision  has  issued,  42  U.S.C.
139500(f)(1),  it would  be exceedingly  difficult  to assert  a
justification  for  missing  the  filing  deadline,  especially
more  than  a  few  days  or  weeks.  The  limited  case  law
touching  on  equitable  tolling of the 60-day  deadline,  see
note  10,  supra,  bears  that  out.  By contrast,  as  amply
demonstrated  by this  case  and  the  other  related  cases
pending  in  district  court,  Gov’t  Br.  App.  34a-37a,  the
same  cannot  be  said  of the  180-day  administrative  ap-
peal  deadline.  Superimposing  an  open-ended  equitable
tolling regime  onto  the carefully  structured  administra-
tive  review  process  would  deprive  closed  payment  de-
terminations  from  many years  earlier  of the finality they
have  long enjoyed,  and would  place substantial  adminis-
trative  burdens  on  the  agency,  its  contractors,  and  the
Medicare  Trust  Fund,  Gov’t  Br.  23-28,  in a way  that eq-
uitable  tolling of the judicial  review  deadline  would  (and
has)  not.  Even  if Congress  intended  the  latter,  it would
not  have  envisioned  (let  alone  mandated)  the  former.
Simply  put, the  program  could  not function  with  any de-
gree  of efficiency without  the order  and  finality achieved
through  the  statutory  and  regulatory  regime  that  has
long governed  the  administrative  review  process.

c.  Respondents’  attempts  to minimize  the importance
of administrative  finality  and  the  practical  effects  of an
equitable-tolling  regime  are  unavailing.

Kespondents  first  contend  (Br.  47-48)  that the  Board
is perfectly  capable  of engaging  in a fact-intensive  equi-
table  tolling  inquiry  and,  indeed,  has  been  “regularly”
engaging in “equitable  inquiries.”  Respondents  conflate
the  Secretary’s  focused  good-cause  regulation  with  the

19

open-ended  equitable  tolling  regime  they  urge."'  The
good-cause  regulation  is substantially  more  limited  than
judicially  imposed  equitable  tolling.  Most  significantly,
like  the reopening  regulation,  it has  a mandatory  three-
year  outer  limit.  Respondents  barely  acknowledge  that
critical  distinction.  There  is  a  world  of difference  be-
tween  the  possibility  that  a past cost  year  may  be revis-
ited within  three  years  of the intermediary’s  determina-
tion,  but  no  later,  and  the  possibility  that  a  past  cost
year  may be revisited  at any  time.  Moreover,  while  eq-
uitable  tolling  is  itself  a  narrow  doctrine,  it  is  still
broader  than  the  Secretary’s  limited  good-cause  excep-
tion  to  the  180-day  appeal  deadline—particularly  after
the  2008  regulatory  amendments.  73  Fed.  Reg.  30,206
(2008);  42  C.F .R. 405.1836(b)  (2011)  (limiting good cause
to “extraordinary  circumstances  beyond  [the hospital’s]
control  (such  as  a  natural  or  other  catastrophe,  fire,  or

'  Only four of the cases  respondents  cite discuss  equitable  tolling.
Two  involved  the same  Baystate  issue  presented  here.  In those  cas-
es,  the  Board,  after  holding that  equitable  tolling  is unavailable  as  a
matter  of law, briefly explained  that, even  if tolling were  available,  it
would  not  be warranted  based  on  the  “undisputed  facts.”  Medical
Coll.  of Ga.  Hosp.,  2010  WL  4214223,  at  *3;  SKI  1987-1994  DSH
SSI%  Group,  PRRB  Dec.  No. 2009-D12,  2009  WL 981317,  at *4 (Mar.
5, 2009).  The  other  two  cases  did  not  involve  the  180-day  appeal
deadline  at issue  here,  and  any  analysis  of equitable  principles  was
cursory  at best.  See  Bradford  Reg'l  Med.  Ctr.,  PRRB  Dec.  No.  99-
D19,  1999  WL  10149,  at *8 (Jan.  7, 1999)  (90-day  period  for submit-
ting  supporting  documentation  relating  to  capital-related  costs),
rev'd,  1999 WL 398034  (HCFA Adm’r  Mar.  12, 1999), rev’d, Bradford
Hosp.  v.  Shalala,  136 F. Supp. 2d 428 (W.D.  Pa. 2001); New Hanover
Reg't Med.  Ctr.  & Psychiatric  Unit,  PRRB  Dec.  No.  2003-D37,  2003
WL  21804275,  at  *6  (July  17, 2003)  (180-day  period  for  requesting
adjustment to rate-of-increase  ceiling).  The equitable-tolling  inquiry
respondents  advocate  would  in fact  be a drastic  departure  from  the
Board’s  “regular{]”  practice  that  has  endured  for  nearly  40 years.

20

strike)”).  Accordingly,  application  of the good-cause  ex-
ception  does  not require the agency  to devote  significant
resources  to  adjudicating  untimely  appeals.  Gov’t  Br.
24.

Respondents  also  contend  that no  “floodgates”  would
open  because  equitable-tolling  claims  would  be  limited
to  “the  agency’s  own  misfeasance,”  which  is  “entirely
within  the  Secretary’s  own  control.”  Br.  55-56.  That
equitable-tolling  relief should  rarely be granted,  howev-
er,  does  not  mean  that  the  doctrine  would  rarely  be  in-
voked.  Recent history shows  that what constitutes  “mis-
feasance”  is in the  eye  of the  beholder.  Gov’t  Br.  25-27.
As this Court  recognized  in Brockamp,  the  mere  availa-
bility of equitable  tolling  “could  create  serious  adminis-
trative  problems  by forcing  the  [agency]  to respond  to,
and  perhaps  litigate,  large  numbers  of late  claims,  ac-
companied  by requests  for ‘equitable  tolling’  which,  up-
on  close  inspection,  might  turn  out  to lack  sufficient  eq-
uitable  justification.”  519  U.S.  at 352;  see  High  Coun-
try,  359  F.3d  at  1311  (“[F  Jor every  plaintiff  whose  sub-
stantive  claim  or  reason  for default  leads  an  adjudicator
to excuse  the default,  ten less sympathetic  plaintiffs  are
likely  to demand  similar  treatment.”).

The  Medicare  Act’s  “complex  and  highly  technical
regulatory  program”  heightens  the  need  for  finality.
Thomas  Jefferson  Univ.  v.  Shalala,  512  U.S.  504,  512
(1994)  (citation  omitted);  see  Methodist  Hosp.  v.  Shala-
la, 38  F.3d  1225,  1235  (D.C.  Cir.  1994)  (“policy  of retro-
active  corrections  would  erode  the  predictability  and  fi-
nality that  underlie  the  [Medicare  Prospective  Payment
System]  scheme”).  This Court  recognized  in Your
Home
that  “the  statutory  purpose  of imposing  a  180-day  limit
on  the right to seek  Board  review  of NPRs  *  *  *  would
be frustrated  by permitting  requests  to reopen  to be re-

21

viewed  indefinitely.”  525  U.S.  at  454.  Engrafting  an
open-ended  equitable  tolling  regime  onto  Section
139500(a)(3)  would  have  an  even  more  dramatic  effect,
because  it would  not  be  cabined  by a  three-year  outer
limit.  Contrary  to  respondents’  assertion  (Br.  39 n.13),
the  need  for order  and  finality  under  Medicare  is espe-
cially  critical  in light  of the  backlog  of cases  before  the
Board.  Gov’t  Br.  30-31.  “[{T]he  Board  is burdened  by an
immense  caseload,”  and  “procedural  rules  requiring
timely  filings  are  indispensible  devices  for  keeping  the
machinery  of the  reimbursement  appeals  process  run-
ning smoothly.”  High  Country,  359  F.3d  at  1310.”

D.  The  Board  Has  No  Authority  To  Adjudicate  Appeals
Filed  Outside  The  Statutory  And  Regulatory  Deadlines
Respondents  contend  (Br.  34)  that  the  government
agrees  that  Section  139500(a)(3)  is  not  jurisdictional.
What  the government  explains  in its opening  brief,  how-
ever,  is that although  Section  139500(a)(3)  does  limit the
Board’s  jurisdiction,  it does  not  preclude  the  Secretary
from  adopting  a narrow  and  time-limited  good cause  ex-
ception  to the  180-day  filing  deadline,  in the  exercise  of
her  broad  authority  under  the  Medicare  Act.  Section

*  Respondents  compare  the  size  of the Medicare  program  to that
of Social  Security,  Br.  49 n.19,  but there  are  at least  two  critical  dis-
tinctions.  First,  the disability  benefits  scheme  at issue  in City of New
York  was  “designed  to  be  ‘unusually  protective’  of claimants,”  476
U.S.  at 480, whereas  the Medicare  payment  scheme  at iesue  here  in-
volves  “sophisticated”  institutional  providers,  Your Home,  525  U.S.
at 456.  Second,  a determination  of individual  entitlement  to disability
benefits  does not have  the breadth  and technical  complexity  involved
in a determination  of Medicare  provider  payments.  In that  respect,
Medicare  provider  payments  are  more  analogous  to the subject mat-
ter in Brockamp  (tax collection)  than to the subject matter  in City of
New  York  (Social  Security  disability  benefits).

22

139500(a)(3)  and  the  regulations  implementing  it then
together establish  mandatory  time  limits  on  the  Board’s
adjudicative  authority.  The  court  of appeals  erred  in
overriding  those  mandatory  limits  by imposing  an  un-
precedented  open-ended  equitable  tolling regime  on  the
Medicare  provider  appeals  process  that Congress  enact-
ed 40 years  ago.

*-_  es  +

*  ©

For  the  foregoing  reasons  and  those  stated  in  our
opening  brief,  the  judgment  of  the  court  of  appeals
should  be  reversed.

Respectfully  submitted.

2A  NORMAN  J.  SINGER,  STATUTES  AND  STATU-

TORY  CONSTRUCTION  (6th ed.  2000)  .........0:c000000  40

INTEREST  OF  AMICUS  CURIAE

On  July  23,  2012,  this  Court  invited  John  F.  Man-
ning  “to  brief  and  argue  this  case,  as  amicus  curiae,
in  support  of the  position  that  the  180-day  statutory
time  limit  for  filing  an  appeal  with  the  Provider
Reimbursement  Review  Board  from  a  final  Medicare
payment  determination  made  by a  fiscal  interme-
diary,  42  U.S.C.  §139500(a)(3),  may  not  be  extended
for any  period.”  This  brief responds  to that  -nvitation.!

STATEMENT

A.  Statutory  and  Regulatory  Background

1.

The  Medicare  Act,  Title  XVIII  of  the  Social
Security  Act,  as  amended,  42  U.S.C.  § 1395  et  seq.,
provides  medical  services  for  elderly  and  disabled
patients.
Part  A  of  the  program  prescribes  that,
when  certain  classes  of health  care  providers  supply
cove’ed  health  services  to  Medicare  beneficiaries,
they  receive  reimbursements  for  certain  costs  as  de-
termined  by statute  and  by regulations  promulgated
by  the  Secretary  of  Health  and  Human  Services
(“HHS”).
Since  1983,  the
Medicare  statute  has  provided  that,  for  in-patient
hospital  services,  providers  will  receive  reimburse-
ment  under  a  “prospective  payment  system”  that  sets
a  standardized  rate  for  each  discharge  based  on
the  patient’s  diagnosis-related  group  (“DRG”),  which
is  calculated  on  the  basis  of,  among  other  things,
See  id.
the  patient’s  diagnosis,  age,  and  sex.
§ 1395ww(b),  (d);  see  also  Good  Samaritan  Hosp.  v.
Shalala,  508  U.S.  402,  406  n.3  (1993).  Because  the

See  id.  §1395c  et  seq.

!  No  counsel  for  a  party  authored  any  part  of this  brief,  and
no  person  other  than  amicus,  his  co-counsel,  and  his  employer
(Harvard  University)  made  any  financial  or  other  material
contribution  to  the  preparation  of this  brief.

2

amount  of  reimbursement  reflects  a  prospectively
determined  average  that  does  not  necessarily  capture
actual  costs,  the  statute  directs  the  Secretary  to
make  certain  adjustments  based  on  hospital-specific
factors.  See  42  U.S.C.  § 1395ww(d)(5).

2.

To  obtain  Medicare  reimbursements,  health
care  providers  must  submit  “cost  reports”  annually  to
fiscal  intermediaries  (typically,  insurance  companies)
that  the  Secretary  has  designated  to  calculate  reim-
bursements  under  applicable  statutory  and  regula-
See  42  U.S.C.  §1395h;  42  C.F.R.
tory  criteria.
§§ 405.1801(b),  413.20(b),  413.24(f),  421.3;2  see  also
Regions  Hosp.  v.  Shalala,  522  U.S.  448,  452  (1998).
The  intermediary  then  analyzes  the  cost  report,
audits  it if necessary,  and  issues  a  “notice  of amount
of  program  reimbursement”  (“NPR”)  specifying  the
provider’s  reimbursement  for  the  fiscal  year  covered
by  the  report.
42  C.F.R.  § 405.1803;  see  Regions
Hosp.,  522  U.S.  at  452-53.

3.

In  the  early  years  of  the  Medicare  program,
the  statute  provided  no  mechanism  for  further  review
of  an  intermediary’s  reimbursement  determination,
and,  while  the  Department  of  Health,  Education,
and  Welfare  had  sought  to  develop  administrative
procedures  to  settle  disputes  between  providers  and
intermediaries,  the  House  Ways  and  Means  Commit-

2  Unless  otherwise  indicated,  this  brief  will  refer  to  the  2007
edition  of 42  C.F.R.  Although  Part  405  of 42  C.F.R.—including
some  of  the  sections  at  issue  in  this  litigation—was  subse-
quently  amended,  the  amendments  themselves  specify  that,
with  exceptions  not  applicable  here,  the  changes  apply  only  to
“appeals  pending  as  of,  or  filed  on  or  after{,]  August  21,  2008.”
73  Fed.  Reg.  30,190,  30,190  (May  23,  2008).  The  amended  regu-
See  Pet.  App.  15a
lations,  therefore,  do  not  govern  this  case.
n.3.

3

tee  thought  it  necessary  to  establish  “a  specific  pro-
cedure  for  settling  disputed  final  determinations
applying  to  the  amount  of program  reimbursement.”
H.R.  Rep.  No.  92-231,  at  108  (1971),  reprinted  in
To  that  end,  the
1972  U.S.C.C.A.N.  4989,  5094.
Social  Security  Amendments  of  1972,  Pub.  L.  No.
92-603,  § 243(a),  86  Stat.  1329,  1420,  established  the
Provider  Reimbursement  Review  Board  (“PRRB”  or
“Board”)  and  put  in  place  a  detailed  scheme  of  ad-
ministrative  and  judicial  review  for  intermediaries’
final  determinations  of cost  reimbursements.

The  statute—42  U.S.C.  § 1395003—establishes  the
Board,  prescribes  its  powers,  and  sets  forth  the  mech-
anism  for  judicial  review  of  the  Board’s  decisions.
Subsection  (a),  captioned  “Establishment,”  delineates
what  cases  the  newly  established  Board  may  hear.
First,  as  to  subject  matter,  a  provider  that  has  filed
timely  cost  reports  may  seek  review  if it is  “dissatis-
fied”  with  an  intermediary’s  determination  of  cost
reimbursements  or  with  the  Secretary’s  determina-
tion  of  the  “amount  of  the  payment”  to  be  received
during  a  fiscal  year  under  the  prospective  payment
system.  Id.  § 139500(a)(1)(A)(i),  (ii).4  The  Board  may
also  hear  the  case  if  the  provider  has  not  received
an  amount  determination  from  the  Secretary  or  an
NPR  from  the  intermediary  on  “a  timely  basis”  after
having  filed  the  necessary  materials  in  conformity
with  applicable  regulations.  Jd.  § 139500(a)(1)(B),  (C).
Second,  the  right  to  a  hearing  accrues  only  if  “the
Id.
amount  in  controversy  is  $10,000  or  more.”

3  The  text  of  § 139500  is  set  forth  in  the  Addendum  to  the

brief.

4  Because  respondents  challenge  calculations  embodied  in
their  NPRs,  see  Pet.  App.  17a,  their  challenge  arises  under
subsection  (a)(1)(A)(i).

4

§ 139500(a)(2).  Third,  a  provider  may  seek  a  hearing
only  if “such  provider  files  a  request  for  a  hearing
within  180  days”  after  notice  of the  intermediary’s  or
the  Secretary’s  “final  determination”  or,  for  claims  of
unlawfully  delayed  determination,  “within  180  days
after  notice  of  such  determination  would  have  been
received  if such  determination  had  been  made  on  a
timely  basis.”  Id.  § 139500(a)(3).

The  balance  of  § 139500  fleshes  out  the  review
scheme.
Providers,  for  example,  “have  the  right  to
be represented  by counsel,  to  introduce  evidence,  and
to  examine  and  cross-examine  witnesses,”  and  Board
decisions  must  be  “upon  the  record.”  Id.  § 139500(c),
(d).  The  Board  has  the  power  “to  affirm,  modify,  or
reverse”  the  fiscal  intermediary’s  “final  determina-
tion”  and  “to  make  any  other  revisions  on  matters
covered  by  [the  provider’s]  cost  report,”  whether  or
not  the  intermediary  considered  those  matters  in
rendering  an  NPR.
The  Board’s
decision  becomes  final  within  60  days  unless  the  Sec-
retary,  “on  [her]  own  motion,”  decides  to  “reverse[],
affirm[],  or  modif[y}”  it.  Id.  § 139500(f)(1).5  A  pro-
vider  may  then  file  a  civil  action  in  district  court
within  60  days  of receiving  notice  of the  Board’s  final
decision  or,  if the  Secretary  acts,  of her  final  disposi-
tion  of the  matter.  Jd.

Id.  § 139500(d).

5 The  Secretary  delegated  her  authority  to  review  the  Board’s
decisions  to  the  Administrator  of  the  Health  Care  Financing
Administration  (“HCFA”).
See  49  Fed.  Reg.  35,247,  35,248
(Sept.  6,  1984).  In  2001,  the  Secretary  changed  HCFA’s  name
to  the  Center  for  Medicare  &  Medicaid  Services  (“CMS”)  and
redelegated  to  the  CMS  Administrator  all  authority  previously
delegated  to  the  HCFA  Administrator.  See  66  Fed.  Reg.  35,437
(July  5, 2001).  For  simplicity,  this  brief  will  refer  to  HCFA  and
CMS  as  CMS.

5

4.

In  regulations  promulgated  pursuant  to  ex-
press  authority  to  implement  the  Medicare  program,
see  42  U.S.C.  §§ 1302(a),  1395/(a),  1395hh(a)(1),  the
Secretary  has  added  further  detail  to  the  review
scheme  established  by the  statute.  See,  e.g.,  42  C.F.R.
§§ 405.1835  (“Right  to  Board  hearing.”),  405.1837
(“Group  appeal.”),  405.1839  (“Amount  in controversy.”).
At  all  times  relevant  here,  HHS  regulations  gave
the  Board  discretion  to  extend  the  180-day  time  limit
“for  good  cause  shown,”  provided  that  the  request  for
extension  was  filed  not  “more  than  3 years  after  the
date  the  notice  of  the  intermediary’s  determination
[was]  mailed  to  the  provider.”  Id.  § 405.1841(b).®

B.  Statement  of the  Case

1.

This  case  arises  from  respondents’  claims  for
additional  reimbursements  for  fiscal  years  1987
through  1994.  See  Pet.  App.  2a.  The  Medicare  Act
provides  for  additional  payments  to  “disproportionate
share  hospitals”  (“DSH”}—those  which  “serve[]  a
significantly  disproportionate  number  of  low-income
The
patients.”.

42  U.S.C.  § 1395ww(d)(5)(F)G)(D.

6  The  2008  amendments  to  Part  405  of  42  C.F.R.  tightened
up  the  requirements  for  extension  of time.  Under  the  current
regulations,  “(t]he  Board  may  find  good  cause  ...  only  if the
provider  demonstrates  ...  [that]  it  could  not  reasonably  be
expected  to  file  timely  due  to  extraordinary  circumstances
beyond  its  control  (such  as  a  natural  or  other  catastrophe,  fire,
42  C.F.R.  § 405.1836(b)  (2011).  The  Board  must
or  strike).”
receive  the  request  for  extension  “within  a  reasonable  time”
after  the  180-day  limit  expires,  but  no  “later  than  3 years  after”
the  NPR  issues.  Id.  § 405.1836(b),  (c)(2).  Finally,  the  amended
regulation  precludes  the  Board  from  granting  an  extension
where  the  request  is based  on  “a change  in the  law,  regulations,
CMS  Rulings,  or  general  CMS  instructions  (whether  based  on  a
court  decision  or  otherwise)  or  a CMS  administrative  ruling  or
policy.”  Id.  § 405.1836(c)(1).

6

DSH  provision  at  issue  here  states  that,  for  any
given  hospital,  the  DSH  adjustment  for  a  fiscal
year  depends  on  the  number  of  patient-days  spent
by  patients  entitled
to  both  Medicare  Part  A
and  Supplemental  Security  Income  (“SSI’)  benefits,
divided  by  the  number  of  patient-days  spent  by
those  entitled  to  Medicare  Part  A  benefits.
See
The  Center  for  Medi-
id.  § 1395ww(d)(5)(F)(vi)().7
care  &  Medicaid  Services  (“CMS”)  calculates  the
so-called  “Medicare/SSI  fraction”  for  each  hospital
each  fiscal  year  and  supplies  that  number  to  the
fiscal  intermediary,  which  then  calculates  the  dis-
proportionate  patient  percentage  and  makes  an
appropriate  DSH  adjustment  to  the  reimbursement.
See  42  C.F.R.  § 412.106(b).

2.

After  receiving  NPRs  in  the  years  at  issue,
respondents  did  not  appeal  their  DSH  adjustments
to  the  PRRB  within  the  180-day  limit  prescribed  by
42  U.S.C.  § 139500(a)(3).  See  Pet.  App.  17a.  Rather,
respondents’  lawsuit  grew  out  of information  revealed
in  unrelated  litigation  by  Baystate  Medical  Center
(“Baystate”).
See  Baystate  Med.  Ctr.  v.  Leavitt,  545
F.  Supp.  2d  20,  amended  in  part,  587  F.  Supp.  2d
37  (D.D.C.  2008).  In  a  timely  appeal  by Baystate  of
its  DSH  adjustments  for  fiscal  years  1993  through
1996,  the  Board  found  that  CMS  had  omitted  several
categories  of  SSI  eligibility  data  and  used  a  flawed
method  of identifying  SSI-eligible  Medicare  patients.
See  Pet.  App.  17a-18a.  Although  CMS  then  reversed
the  Board’s  determination,  the  district  court  in  Bay-

7 A second  DSH  adjustment  reflects  the  proportion  of patient-
days  spent  by patients  entitled  to  Medicaid  divided  by the  total
number  of patient-days.  See  42  U.S.C.  § 1395ww/(d)(5)(F)(vi)(ID).
The  Medicaid-based  adjustment  is not  at  issue  in this  case.

7

state  reversed  CMS  and  largely  affirmed  the  Board’s
determinations.  See  id.  at  18a  n.5.

3.  On  September  12,  2006,  approximately  three
months  after  the  Board’s  Baystate  decision,  respon-
dents  sought  Board  review  of their  DSH  adjustments
for  fiscal  years  1987  to  1994.  See  Pet.  App.  2a,  18a.
They  conceded  that  their  appeals  were  filed  outside
both  the  180-day  limit  set  by 42  U.S.C.  § 139500(a)(3)
and  the  three-year  period  within  which  they  could
have  sought  a  “good  cause”  exception  to  that  limit
See  Pet.  App.  18a.
under  42  C.F.R.  § 405.1841(b).
Respondents  argued,  however,  that,  in  light  of “CMS’
alleged  knowing  and  unlawful  refusal  to  inform  hos-
pitals  that  the  SSI  percentage  was  incorrectly  stated
for the  fiscal  years  under  appeal,”  their  case  qualified
for equitable  tolling.  Jd.  at  53a.

The  Board  held  that  it  “lack[ed]  jurisdiction”  over
respondents’  appeal  for  the  years  in question  because
it “was  not  timely  filed.”  Jd.  at  55a.  Citing  Anaheim
Memorial  Hospital  v.  Blue  Cross  of California,  PRRB
Dec.  2000-D72  (Medicare  and  Medicaid  Guide  (CCH))
{| 80,527  (PRRB  July  3,  2000),  the  Board  reasoned
that  “it  does  not  have  general  equitable  powers
and  cannot  grant  equitable  relief  such  as  equitable
tolling.”
Pet.  App.  55a.  Rather,  as  “an  administra-
tive  forum,”  it possesses  “only  the  powers  granted  to
it  by  statute  and  regulation.”  Jd.  Accordingly,  the
Board  concluded  that  it  had  no  power  to  hear  an
appeal  not  filed  within  either  the  statutory  180-day
limit  or  the  three-year  regulatory  window  in which  to
seek  a “good  cause”  exception.  Jd.

4.

Respondents  filed  the  present  action  challeng-
ing  the  Board’s  decision  in  the  U.S.  District  Court
for  the  District  of Columbia.  See  Pet.  App.  11a-50a.

8

Invoking  Jrwin  v.  Department  of  Veterans  Affairs,
498  U.S.  89  (1990),  respondents  argued  that  the  time
limit  prescribed  by § 139500(a)(3)  was  presumptively
subject  to  equitable  tolling.
The
court,  however,  found  that  the  Jrwin  presumption
applies  only  when  a  suit  against  the  federal  govern-
ment  is  analogous  to  a  private  lawsuit  for  which
Jd.  at  31a.
equitable  tolling  would  be  available.
Reasoning  that  “the  programmatic  reimbursement  at
issue  is  not  familiar  to  private  litigation,”  the  court
found  the Jrwin  presumption  inapplicable.  Jd.  at 32a.

Pet.  App.  30a.

The  district  court  further  relied  on  United  States  v.
Brockamp,  519  U.S.  347  (1997),  which  had  held  that
the  emphatic  language  and  complexity  of the  limita-
tions  period  for  tax-refund  claims,  see  26  U.S.C.
§ 6511,  showed  that  Congress  did  not  intend  to
supplement  the  statute  with  judge-made  equitable
tolling.
The  court  reasoned  that  the  Medicare
Act  also  expressed  the  180-day  limit  in  “‘emphatic’
language.”  Pet.  App.  34a.  And,  while  acknowledging
that  the  PRRB  review  scheme  was  not  as  intricate
as  the  tax  scheme  at  issue  in  Brockamp,  the  court
found  it  significant  that  the  Secretary  had  used  her
delegated  rulemaking  authority  to  supplement  the
statute’s  time  limit  with  rules  specifying  the  circum-
stances  in  which a  late  filing  would  be  excused  or  a
final  Board  determination  reopened.  Id.  at  36a-37a.
The  court  held  that,  if it were  to  apply  a  judge-made
equitable  tolling  doctrine,  it  would  circumvent  the
Secretary's  careful  regulatory  scheme.
ld.  at  39a-
40a.
Finally,  while  resting  on  the  previously  dis-
cussed  grounds,  the  court  added  that  “[t]he  volume
and  complexity  of  the  provider  reimbursement  pro-
gram”  and  the  sophistication  of  Medicare  providers

9

at  least  “buttressed”  its  conclusion  that  Congress  did
not  intend  to  permit  equitable  tolling.  Jd.  at  40a.

5.

Jd.  at  7a.

The  D.C.  Circuit  reversed.

Pet.  App.  la-10a.
The  court  of appeals  reasoned  that  the  presumption
in  favor  of  equitable  tolling  applies  with  full  force
to  the  PRRB  review  scheme.  The  court  noted  that
the  language  of  § 139500(a)(3)  is  no  more  emphatic
than  that  of  other  time  limits  that  the  courts  had
subjected  to  equitable  tolling.
Further,
distinguishing  the  PRRB  review  scheme  from  the  tax
statute  at  issue  in  Brockamp,  the  court  emphasized
that  the  latter  scheme  not  only  speaks  in  “‘highly
detailed’”  and  “‘technical’”  terms,  but  also  “‘sets
forth  explicit  exceptions  to  its  basic  time  limits.’”  Jd.
at  7a-8a  (quoting  Brockamp,  519  U.S.  at  350,  351).
In  contrast,  § 139500(a)(3)’s  time  limit  is  stated  in
See  id.  at  9a.  In  addition,
relatively  simple  terms.
because  the  elaborate  exceptions  to  § 139500(a)(3)’s
180-day  time  limit  appear  in  agency  regulations
rather  than  the  statute,  the  court  concluded  that
they  shed  no  light  on  congressional  intent.
Jd.  In
short,  the  court  of appeals  concluded  that,  while  the
provider  review  scheme  is  complex,  “unlike  the  tax
code,  [it]  does  not  create  a  detailed  Jenga  tower  of
deadlines  and  exceptions  that  equitable  tolling  might
topple.”  Id.  at  10a.

6.

The  court  of  appeals’  decision  brought  the
D.C.  Circuit  into  conflict  with  the  Eleventh  Circuit’s
decision  in  Alacare  Home  Health  Services,  Inc.  v.
Sullivan,  891  F.2d  850  (11th  Cir.  1990),  and  the
Eighth  Circuit’s  decision  in  St.  Joseph’s  Hospital  v.
Heckler,  786  F.2d  848  (8th  Cir.  1986).  Both  circuits
held  that  § 139500(a)(3)’s  180-day  limit  is  jurisdic-
tional  and  therefore  not  subject  to  any  unspecified
exception.  Those  courts  reasoned  that  the  statutory

10

text  set  forth  clear  and  unqualified  limits  on  the
Board’s  authority  and  that  the  legislative  history
reinforced  the  mandatory  character  of  that  limita-
tion.  See  Alacare,  891  F.2d  at  856;  St.  Joseph’s,  786
F.2d  at  851-52.  Under  that  reasoning,  § 139500(a)(3)’s
time  limit  is  subject  neither  to  equitable  tolling  by
the  judiciary  nor  to  the  Secretary’s  regulatory  “good
cause”  exception.  See  Alacare,  891  F.2d  at  856.  The
Ninth  Circuit,  in  contrast,  held  that  the  Secretary’s
“good  cause”  exception  represented  a  permissible
interpretation  of  § 139500(a)(3)  and  was  therefore
entitled  to  deference  under  Chevron  U.S.A.  Inc.  v.
Natural  Resources  Defense  Council,  Inc.,  467  U.S.
837  (1984).  See  Western  Med.  Enters.,  Inc.  v.  Heckler,
783  F.2d  1376,  1379-80  (9th  Cir.  1986).

SUMMARY  OF ARGUMENT

Congress  prescribed  an  unqualified  180-day  limit
for  sophisticated  Medicare  providers  to  challenge  a
reimbursement  decision  before  the  Provider  Reim-
bursement  Review  Board.  See  42  U.S.C.  § 139500(a).
Like  other  procedural  requirements,  time  limits  can
qualify  as  jurisdictional  rules  or  as  claims-processing
rules.  Because  a jurisdictional  limitation  defines  the
authority  of  a  tribunal  to  act,  this  Court  has  tradi-
tionally  held  that  such  limitations  are  not  subject  to
waiver,  forfeiture,  or  (in  the  case  of time  limitations)
equitable  tolling.
See,  e.g.,  Gonzalez  v.  Thaler,  132
S.  Ct.  641,  648  (2012);  Dolan  v.  United  States,  130
S.  Ct.  2533,  2538  (2010).  Because  claims-processing
rules  merely  facilitate  the  orderly  progress  of litiga-
tion,  they  permit  greater  flexibility  on  all  of  those
dimensions.  See,  e.g.,  Henderson  v.  Shinseki,  131  S.
Ct.  1197,  1203  (2011).  Congress  alone  has  the  power
to  determine  whether  a  procedural  requirement  con-
stitutes  a  strict  “jurisdictional”  rule  or  a  more  flexible

ll

“claims-processing  rule.”
See,  e.g.,  id.;  Arbaugh  v.
Y &  H  Corp.,  546  U.S.  500,  514-15  (2006).  Because
jurisdictional  rules  and  claims-processing  rules  may
possess  similar  qualities,  identifying  the  distinction
between  them  can  be  “confusing  in  practice.”  Reed
Elsevier,  Inc.  v.  Muchnick,  130  S.  Ct.  1237,  1243
(2010).  Accordingly,  this  Court  has  sought  to  clarify
the  inquiry  by  treating  procedural  requirements  as
presumptively  nonjurisdictional  in  the  absence  of  a
“clear”  legislative  indication  to  the  contrary.  Hender-
In  this  case,  the  text  and
son,  131  S. Ct.  at  1203.
structure  of § 139500(a)  and  that  provision’s  relation-
ship  to  the  Medicare  Act  as  a  whole  clearly  indicate
that  subsection  (a)(3)’s  timely  filing  requirement  is
jurisdictional.

A.

This  Court  has  explained  that  a  jurisdictional
provision  “delineat[es]  the  class  of  cases”  that  a  tri-
bunal  has  the  authority  to  hear  and  “the  persons...
falling  within  [its]  adjudicatory  authority.”  Kontrick
v.  Ryan,  540  U.S.  443,  455  (2004).  Section  139500(a)
—which  provides  for  the  “Establishment”  of  the
PRRB—defines  the  Board’s  power  to  act  by  specify-
ing both  the  parties  who  are  eligible  to  seek  a  hear-
ing  and  the  class  of cases  that  the  Board  may  hear.
As  to  the  latter,  subsection  (a)  expressly  conditions
the  right  to  a  hearing  on  the  satisfaction  of  three
parallel  and  identically  situated  conditions  relating
to  (1)  the  appropriate  subject  matter  of  a  provider
appeal;  (2)  the  minimum  amount  in  controversy  for
such  an  appeal;  and  (3)  the  time  within  which  such
an  appeal  must  be  filed.  See  42  U.S.C.  § 139500(a)(1)-
Because  it  explicitly  provides  for  the  Board’s
(3).
“Establishment”  and  defines  the  classes  of  cases
falling  within  the  Board’s  adjudicative  authority,
the  text  of  § 139500(a)—including  the  timely  filing

12

requirement  in  subsection  (a)(3)—represents  a  classic
jurisdictional  provision.  See,  e.g.,  Steel  Co.  v.  Citizens
for  a  Better  Env't,  523  U.S.  83,  89  (1998).
Indeed,
if subsection  (a)  does  not  define  the  Board’s  jurisdic-
tion,  then  no  provision  in  the  statute  does.  The  fact
that  § 139500(a)(3)  is  an  integral  component  of  a
provision  that  speaks  in  jurisdictional  terms  distin-
guishes  this  case  from  others  in which  the  Court  has
held  a  procedural  requirement  to  be nonjurisdictional.
See,  e.g.,  Henderson,  131  S.  Ct.  at  1202-03;  Reed
Elsevier,  130  S.  Ct.  at  1245-46;  Union  Pacific  R.R.
Co.  v.  Brotherhood  of Locomotive  Eng’rs  &  Trainmen
Gen.  Comm.  of  Adjustment,  Central  Region,  130
S. Ct.  584,  599  (2009);  Arbaugh,  546  U.S.  at  514-15;
Zipes  v.  Trans  World  Airlines,  Inc.,  455  U.S.  385,
393-94  (1982).

B.  The  structure  of § 139500  and  its  relationship
to  other  parts  of  the  Medicare  Act  confirm  that
subsection  (a)(3)’s  timeliness  requirement  is jurisdic-
tional.  In  its  most  recent  case  to  address  the  status
of a  timely  filing requirement—Henderson  v.  Shinseki,
supra—this  Court  found  that  the  procedural  infor-
mality  of  the  veterans’  benefits  review  statute  and
the  statute’s  overall  solicitude  for  the  procedural
rights  of veterans  precluded  attributing  to  Congress
the  intention  to  treat  a  particular  statutory  filing
deadline  as a strict  jurisdictional  rule.  See  Henderson,
131  S.  Ct.  at  1205-06.  The  Social  Security  Amend-
ments  of  1972,  Pub.  L.  No.  92-603,  § 243(a),  86  Stat.
1329,  1420—of  which  § 139500  was  a  part—convey
the  opposite  message.
Section  139500  itself  adopts
crisp  and  unqualified  procedural  preconditions  that
ask  Medicare  providers  to  turn  square  corners  when
seeking  to  appeal  an  intermediary’s  reimbursement
The  Medicare  Act  as  a  whole,  moreover,
decision.
draws  an  explicit  and  telling  contrast  between  the

13

timely  filing  requirements  that  apply  to  sophisticated
Medicare  providers  and  those  that  apply  to  individual
Medicare  beneficiaries.
The  timely  filing  require-
ments  for  sophisticated  Medicare  providers  who  wish
to  pursue  either  administrative  or  judicial  review
speak  in  strict  and  unqualified  terms.  See  42  U.S.C.
§ 139500(a)(3),  (f)(1).
At  the  same  time,  the  Act
prescribes  for  Medicare  beneficiaries  virtually  the
same  timely  filing  periods  as  it  does  for  providers—
but  then  expressly  confers  upon  the  Secretary,  in
addition,  administrative  discretion  to  extend  those
deadlines.  See  id.  § 1395ff(b).  This  disparate  inclu-
sion  and  omission  of administrative  discretion  makes
clear  that  Congress  struck  a  deliberate  balance  when
it  enacted  § 139500(a)(3)  in  sharp  and  unqualified
terms.
See,  e.g.,  Sosa  v.  Alvarez-Machain,  542  U.S.
692,  711  n.9  (2004)  (using  similar  structural  reason-
ing);  Russello  v.  United  States,  464  U.S.  16,  23  (1983)
(same).

C.  The  Secretary’s  longstanding  embrace  of  a
“good  cause”  exception  to  § 139500(a)(3)’s  180-day
limit  does  not  negate  the  conclusion  that  the  timely
It  is  true  that
filing  requirement  is  jurisdictional.
the  regulatory  “good  cause”  exception  is at  odds  with
the  premise  that  the  180-day  limit  is  jurisdictional.
The  Secretary,  moreover,  adopted  the  “good  cause”
regulation  in  a  format  that  makes  the  resulting
interpretation  at  least  Cheuvron-eligible.
See  United
States  v.  Mead  Corp.,  533  U.S.  218,  229-30  (2001).
Nonetheless,  the  Secretary’s  position  is  not  entitled
to  deference  under  the  circumstances  in  this  case.
Because  the  text  and  structure  of the  statute  make
clear  that  § 139500(a)(3)’s  timely  filing  requirement
is jurisdictional,  Congress  has  addressed  the  precise
question  at  issue,  requiring  both  the  agency  and  the

14

Def.  Council,

v.  Natural  Res.

Chevron
judiciary  to  “give  effect  to”  its  decision.
Inc.,
U.S.A.  Inc.
467  U.S.  837,  842-43  (1984).  Nor  should  the  Court
treat  the  agency’s  longstanding  position  about  “good
cause”  as  persuasive  indirect  evidence  of  statutory
meaning.  See,  e.g.,  Andrus  v.  Shell  Oil  Co.,  446  U.S.
657,  667-68  (1980);  Norwegian  Nitrogen  Prods.  Co.
v.  United  States,  288  U.S.  294,  314-15  (1933).  The
position  of  the  responsible  agencies  on  the  status
of  subsection  (a)(3)  has  been,  at  best,  inconsistent.
Putting  the  “good  cause”  exception  to  one  side,  the
Secretary,  CMS,  and  the  PRRB  have  repeatedly
treated  § 139500(a)’s  requirements—including  sub-
section  (a)(3)’s  timely  filing  requirement—as  “juris-
dictional.”  The  Secretary,  moreover,  has  yet  to  recon-
cile  her  “good  cause”  exception  with  that  position.
Accordingly,  were  this  Court  to  hold  that  the  timely
filing  requirement  is jurisdictional,  that  ruling  would
not  contradict  any  clearly  articulated  or  consistently
held  agency  interpretation  of subsection  (a)(3).

ARGUMENT

THE  180-DAY  DEADLINE  FOR  FILING  AN  AP-
PEAL  WITH  THE  BOARD  IS JURISDICTIONAL
AND  THUS  MAY  NOT  BE  EXTENDED

Congress  has  the  power  to  distinguish  procedural
requirements  that  serve  as  strict  “jurisdictional”  lim-
its  from  those  that  function  as  more  flexible  “claims-
See,  e.g.,  Henderson  v.  Shinseki,
processing  rules.”
131  S.  Ct.  1197,  1202-03  (2011);  Reed  Elsevier,  Inc.  v.
Muchnick,  130  S.  Ct.  1237,  1243-44  (2010).  The  con-
cept  of “jurisdiction,”  properly  understood,  describes
a  tribunal’s  very  authority  to  decide  a  case,  and  a
jurisdictional  rule  is  therefore  one  that  defines  a
See,  e.g.,  Kontrick  v.
tribunal’s  adjudicative  power.

15

Ryan,  540  U.S.  443,  455  (2004);  Steel  Co.  v.  Citizens
for a  Better  Env't,  523  U.S.  83,  89  (1998).8  Because
a  jurisdictional  rule  serves  a  strict  power-delimiting
function,  this  Court  has  traditionally  held  that  such
rules  are  not  subject  to  waiver,  forfeiture,  or—in  the
case  of jurisdictional  time  limits—equitable  tolling.
See,  e.g.,  Gonzalez  v.  Thaler,  132  S.  Ct.  641,  648
(2012);  Dolan  v.  United  States,  130  S.  Ct.  2533,  2538
(2010);  John  R.  Sand  &  Gravel  Co.  v.  United  States,
552  U.S.  130,  133-34  (2008);  United  States  v.  Cotton,
In  contrast,  because  a
535  U.S.  625,  630  (2002).
claims-processing  rule  merely  “seek[s]  to  promote  the
orderly  progress  of  litigation  by  requiring  that  the
parties  take  certain  procedural  steps  at  certain  speci-
fied  times,”  Henderson,  131  S.  Ct.  at  1203,  such  rules
are  traditionally  subject  to  waiver  or  estoppel.
See
United  States  v.  Locke,  471  U.S.  84,  94  n.10  (1985).
Nonjurisdictional  timely  filing  requirements,  more-
over,  are  (at least  presumptively)  subject  to  equitable
tolling.  See  Irwin  v.  Department  of Veterans  Affairs,
498  U.S.  89,  95-96  (1990).

Because

rule  and  a  claims-
a  jurisdictional
types  of
processing  rule  may  embody  identical
requirements  (such  as  time  limitations),  this  Court
has  made  clear  that  the  difference  between  the  two
depends  on  the  intention  Congress  has  expressed

8 Though  these  jurisdictional  principles  are  often  articulated
in  the  context  of Article  III  courts,  they  apply  no  less  to  non-
Article  IIT  tribunals  whose  powers  are  established  and  defined
by Congress  pursuant  to  its  authority  under  the  Necessary  and
Proper  Clause,  U.S.  Const.  art.  I, § 8, cl.  18.  See  United  States
vu.  Denedo,  556  U.S.  904,  912  (2009)  (“[I]t  is  for  Congress
to  determine  the  subject-matter  jurisdiction  of  federal  courts.
...  This  rule  applies  with  added  force  to  Article  I tribunals,  ..
.
which  owe  their  existence  to  Congress’  authority  to  enact  legis-
lation  pursuant  to Art.  I, § 8 of the  Constitution.”).

16

about  the  “nature”  of the  particular  rule.  Henderson,
131  S. Ct.  at  1203.  Because  the  resulting  inquiry  can
be “confusing  in practice,”  Reed  Elsevier,  130  S. Ct.  at
1243,  moreover,  this  Court  has  developed  a  “readily
administrable  bright  line”  rule  for  sorting  jurisdic-
tional  rules  from  claims-processing  rules.  Arbaugh  v.
Y &  H Corp.,  546  U.S.  500,  516  (2006).  Accordingly,
as  a  way  both  “to  capture  Congress’  likely  intent  and
...  [to]  provide[]  helpful  guidance  for  courts  and  liti-
gants,”  this  Court  now  “look[s]  to  see  if there  is  any
‘clear’  indication  that  Congress  wanted  [a procedural]
rule  to  be  ‘jurisdictional.’”  Henderson,  131  S.  Ct.  at
1203.  Although  framed  as  a  “clear-statement  prin-
ciple,”  Gonzalez,  132  S.  Ct.  at  648-49,  this  approach
does  not  require  Congress  to  “use  magic  words  in
order  to  speak  clearly.”
Henderson,  131  S.  Ct.  at
1203.  Rather,  the  Court  uses  all  the  traditional  tools
of  statutory  construction—including  the  language,
context,  structure,  and  purpose  of  a  statute—to
determine  whether  Congress  has  clearly  indicated
that  a  procedural  rule  is jurisdictional.  See,  e.g.,  id.
at  1204-06;  Dolan,  130  S.  Ct.  at  2538;  Reed  Elsevier,
130  S.  Ct.  at  1248;  Zipes  v.  Trans  World  Airlines,
Inc.,  455  U.S.  385,  393  (1982).9  As  is  true  of  any

8  In  other  words,  this  Court  has  not  adopted  the  kind  of
strong  clear-statement  rule  that  it has,  at  times,  used  to  protect
a  constitutional  norm  such  as  federalism.  See,  e.g.,  Gregory  v.
Ashcroft,  501  U.S.  452,  460  (1991)  (“[I]f  Congress  intends  to
alter  the  ‘usual  constitutional  balance  between  the  States  and
the  Federal  Government,’  it  must  make  its  intention  to  do  so
‘unmistakably  clear  in  the  language  of  the  statute.’”)  (quoting
Atascadero  State  Hosp.  v.  Scanlon,  473  U.S.  234,  242  (1985));
Dellmuth  v.  Muth,  491  U.S.  223,  228  (1989)  (applying  the  same
clear-statement  rule  to  the  question  whether  a  federal  statute
abrogated  state  sovereign  immunity).
Whereas  those  strong
clear-statement  rules  find  their  justification  directly  in  the
protection  of a  constitutionally  derived  value,  the  jurisdictional

17

question  of  statutory  interpretation,  this  approach
“leave[s]  the  ball  in  Congress’  court.”  Arbaugh,  546
U.S.  at  515-16.

Under  this  framework,  this  Court  has  frequently
found  timely  filing  requirements  to  be  claims-
processing  rules.  See  Henderson,  131  S.  Ct.  at  1203
(so  noting).  But  it has  also  recognized  that  Congress
may  instead  choose  to  treat  such  requirements  as
strictly  jurisdictional  in  order  to  achieve  “a  broader
system-related  goal,  such  as  facilitating  the  adminis-
tration  of claims,  limiting  the  scope  of a  governmen-
tal  waiver  of sovereign  immunity,  or  promoting  judi-
cial  efficiency.”  John  R.  Sand  &  Gravel,  552  U.S.  at
133  (citations  omitted).  In  particular,  when  “serious
administrative  problems”  might  ensue  from  apply-
ing  a  flexible  limitations  period  to  a  massive  and
complex  regulatory  scheme,  Congress  has  at  times
adopted  uncompromising  time  requirements  that
protect  administrative  workability  at  “the  price  of
United
occasional  unfairness  in  individual  cases.”
States  v.  Brockamp,  519  U.S.  347,  352-53  (1997)
(construing  a  statute  of  limitations  governing  tax
refunds).  Given  the  scope  and  scale  of the  Medicare
reimbursement  system,!9  as  well  as  the  generally

rule  of  thumb  at  issue  in  this  case  is  a  policy-based  canon,
rooted  in concerns  about  the  systemic  costs  that  a  (nonwaivable)
jurisdictional  rule  imposes  on  courts  and  litigants  by  altering
the  usual  operation  of the  adversary  system.  See,  e.g.,  Hender-
son,  131  S.  Ct.  at  1202.  Thus,  this  Court  seems  to  have  treated
its  jurisdiction  canon  more  as  a  presumption  against  jurisdic-
tional  status  than  as  a  rule  that  requires  Congress  to  express
itself  with  extraordinary  clarity  on  the  face  of the  text.

10  Even  passing  familiarity  with  the  Medicare  provider  reim-
bursement  system  confirms  that  the  system  is  highly  complex.
See  Good  Samaritan  Hosp.  v.  Shalala,  508  U.S.  402,  404  (1993).
The  Medicare  scheme  also  operates  on  a  massive  scale;  as

18

high  level  of legal  sophistication  of Medicare  provid-
ers,!!  Congress  could  surely  have  chosen  to  strike
such  a  balance  here.
The  language  and  structure
of  42  U.S.C.  § 139500(a)  and  its  relationship  to
the  Medicare  Act  as  a  whole  clearly  indicate  that
Congress  did,  in  fact,  do  so  by  casting  § 139500(a)
in  jurisdictional  terms  and  by  omitting  from  it  the
same  flexibility  over  timing  that  the  Medicare  Act
conferred  upon  the  Secretary,  in  express  terms,  in
other  relevant  contexts.

A.  The  Language  And  Context  Of  § 139500(a)
Make  Clear  That  The  180-Day  Time  Limit  Is
A  Crucial  Element  In  The  Delineation  Of
The  Board’s  Jurisdiction  To  Hear  Cases

“Statutory  construction,”  of  course,  “‘must  begin
with  the  language  employed  by Congress.’”  Milner  v.
Department  of the  Navy,  131  S.  Ct.  1259,  1264  (2011)
(quoting  Park  ’N Fly,  Inc.  v.  Dollar  Park  &  Fly,  Inc.,
469  U.S.  189,  194  (1985)).  Although  § 139500(a)  does
not  use  the  “magic  word”  (jurisdiction),  the  text  of
that  provision  unmistakably  defines  the  jurisdiction
of  the  PRRB.  Jurisdictional  provisions  “delineat([e]
the  classes  of cases  (subject-matter  jurisdiction)  and
the  persons  (personal  jurisdiction)  falling  within  a

of  last  year,  the  system  processed  approximately  250  billion
dollars  of  Part  A  claims  filed  by  more  than  25,000  providers.
See  CMS,  2011  CMS  Statistics,  Tbls.  II.3,  III.5  (June  2011),
http://www.cms.gov/Research-Statistics-Data-and-Systems/
at
Research/ResearchGenInfo/CMSStatistics.htm]  (last  visited  Aug.
27,  2012).

11  See  Your  Home  Visiting  Nurse  Servs.,  Inc.  v.  Shalala,  525
U.S.  449,  456  (1999)  (“[E]ach  of the  tens  of thousands  of sophis-
ticated  [Medicare  providers]  .
.  is generally  capable  of identify-
ing  an  underpayment  in  its  own  NPR  within  the  180-day  time
period  specified  in 42 U.S.C.  § 139500(a)(3).”).

.

19

court’s  adjudicatory  authority.”  Kontrick,  540  U.S.  at
455.  Accord,  e.g.,  Reed  Elsevier,  130  S.  Ct.  at  1243;
Eberhart  v.  United  States,  546  U.S.  12,  16  (2005)
(per  curiam).  A timely  filing  requirement,  therefore,
qualifies  as  “‘jurisdictional’  when  Congress  prohibits
federal  courts  from  adjudicating  an  otherwise  legiti-
mate  ‘class  of cases’  after  a certain  period  has  elapsed”
from  a  specified  event.  Bowles  v.  Russell,  551  U.S.
213 (2007).
205,

By this  measure,  § 139500(a)—including  the  timely
filing  requirement  at  issue  here—is  a  classic  jurisdic-
tional  provision.  Section  139500  is entitled  “Provider
Reimbursement  Review  Board,”  and  subsection  (a)
specifically  addresses,  as  its  caption  states,  the
Board’s  “Establishment.”  42  U.S.C.  § 139500(a);  see,
e.g.,  Florida  Dep’t  of Revenue  v.  Piccadilly  Cafeterias,
Inc.,  554  U.S.  33,  47  (2008)  (title  or  caption  may
provide  context  for  understanding  the  text);  Porter  v.
Nussle,  534  U.S.  516,  527-28  (2002)  (same).  Consis-
tent  with  that  descriptor,  subsection  (a)  establishes
the  Board’s  authority  by  carefully  defining  what
cases  it may  hear.  Subsection  (a)  begins  by defining
the  class  of parties  eligible  for  a  hearing  before  the
Board.  See  42  U.S.C.  § 139500(a)  (authorizing  hear-
ings  for  providers  that  “ha[ve]  filed  a  required  cost
report  [with  an  intermediary]  within  the  time  speci-
fied  in regulations”  or  “submitted  such  reports  within
such  time  as  the  Secretary  may  require”  for  purposes
of the  prospective  payment  system).  The  statute  then
delineates  the  appropriate  class  of cases  the  Board
may  hear,  dictating  that  providers  “may  obtain  a
hearing  ...  if” the  provider  satisfies  the  three  num-
bered  requirements.  Jd.  (emphasis  added).

The  first  such  requirement—codified  in  subsection
(a)(1)—specifies  the  subject  matter  of the  claims  that

20

otherwise  eligible  providers  may  bring  to  the  Board.
Specifically,  a  provider  may  obtain  review  in  three
carefully  defined  circumstances.
First,  review  is
available  if the  provider  is  “dissatisfied  with  a  final
determination  of ...  its  fiscal  intermediary  ...  as  to
the  amount  of total  program  reimbursement  due  the
provider  for  the  items  and  services  furnished  to  indi-
viduals  for  which  payment  may  be  made  under  this
subchapter  for  the  period  covered  by  such  report.”
Id.  § 139500(a)(1)(A)(@i).
Second,  a  provider  may
invoke  the  Board’s  authority  if  “dissatisfied  with  a
final  determination  of the  Secretary  as  to  the  amount
of  the  payment  under”  the  prospective  payment
system  (prescribed  by  42  U.S.C.  § 1395ww/(b),  (d)).
Id.  § 139500(a)(1)(A)(ii).
Finally,  the  provider  may
seek  redress  before  the  Board  if  the  provider  “has
not  received  [a]  final  determination  [NPR]  from  [an]
intermediary  on  a  timely  basis  after  filing  [a  cost]
report,  where  such  report  complied  with  the  rules  and
regulations  of the  Secretary  relating  to  such  report.”
Id.  § 139500(a)(1)(B);  see  also  id.  § 139500(a)(1)(C)
(authorizing  review  of  untimely  responses  to  a
“supplementary  cost  report”).  These  provisions  serve
precisely  the  same  function  as  numerous  provisions
of the  Judicial  Code  that  define  subject-matter  juris-
diction  by reference  to  the  type  of claim  that  may  be
asserted.  See  Arbaugh,  546  U.S.  at  515  n.11  (giving
examples);  see  also,  e.g.,  28  U.S.C.  § 1339  (“any  civil
action  arising  under  any  Act  of  Congress  relating
to  the  postal  service”);  id.  § 1347  (“any  civil  action
commenced  by any  tenant  in  common  or  joint  tenant
for  the  partition  of lands  where  the  United  States  is
one  of the  tenants  in  common  or  joint  tenants”).  To
the  extent  that  they  have  considered  the  question,
moreover,  the  federal  courts  and  the  responsible

21

agencies  have  consistently  described  subsection  (a)(1)
as  a jurisdictional  element. '2

The  second  condition  for  obtaining  a  hearing—
codified  in the  parallel  subsection  (a)(2)—also  reflects
a  familiar  jurisdictional  requirement:
A  provider
the  amount
may  obtain  a  hearing  only  “if  ...
42  U.S.C.
in  controversy  is  $10,000  or  more.”
§ 139500(a)(2).  Perhaps  in  part  because  of its  salient
role  in  defining  diversity  jurisdiction  and,  until
1980,  federal-question  jurisdiction,  the  amount-in-
controversy  requirement  is  commonly  thought  of  as
a  “threshold  ingredient”  of  a  tribunal’s  jurisdiction.
Arbaugh,  546  U.S.  at  515;  see  28  U.S.C.  § 1331  (1976)
(former  federal-question  statute);  28  U.S.C.  § 1332(a)
(diversity  jurisdiction);  see  also,  e.g.,  16  U.S.C.  § 814;
22  U.S.C.  §6713(a)(1)(B);  28  U.S.C.  § 1346(a)(2).

12  This  Court,  for  example,  has  written  that  “a  provider's
dissatisfaction  with  the  amount  of its  total  reimbursement  is  a
condition  to  the  Board’s  jurisdiction.”  Bethesda  Hosp.  Ass'n  v.
Bowen,  485  U.S.  399,  404  (1988).  The  relevant  agencies  have
also  treated  the  provision  as  jurisdictional.
See,  e.g.,  Baystate
Med.  Ctr.  v.  Mutual  of Omaha  Ins.  Co.,  No.  2006-D20,  2006  WL
1684639,  at  *35  n.107  (CMS  Adm’r  May  11,  2006)  (observing
that,  with  respect  to  subsection  (a)(1)'s  “dissatisfaction”  require-
ment,  “[jjurisdiction  can  never  be  waived”);  Select  Specialty
Hosp.  v.  Mutual  of Omaha  Ins.  Co.,  No.  2005-D38,  2005  WL
3741460,  at  *3-4  (CMS  Adm’r  July  11,  2005)  (noting  that,  “[b]y
failing  to  present  a  claim  for  reimbursement,”  the  provider
“failed  to  meet  the  threshold  test  for Board  jurisdiction”).  Thus,
for  example,  where  the  provider  is  not  “dissatisfied”  with  the
amount  of reimbursement  but  merely  asks  the  Board  to  deter-
mine  which  competing  claimant  is entitled  to  receive  the  reim-
bursement,  the  Board  has  disclaimed  jurisdiction  to  hear  the
case.
See,  e.g.,  Home  Care  PRN  v.  Blue  Cross  &  Blue  Shield
Ass'n,  No.  2004-D1,  2003  WL  22490094,  at  *2  (PRRB  Oct.  14,
2004);  Florida  Convalescent  Ctrs.  97 Therapy  Mgmt.  Fee  Group  v.
BlueCross  BlueShield  Ass’n,  No.  2004-D45,  2004  WL  2584861,

Again,  the  federal  courts  and  the  relevant  agencies
have  described  subsection  (a)(2)  as  an  element  of the

Finally,  the  third  condition—and  the  one  at  issue
In

here—prescribes  a  timely  filing  requirement.
particular,  a  provider  may  obtain  a  hearing

if  ...  such  provider  files  a  request  for  a  hearing
within  180  days  after  notice  of the  intermediary’s

13  Though  this  Court  has  never  had  occasion  to  speak  to  the
question,  lower  federal  courts  have  routinely  characterized  sub-
section  (a)(2)  as  a  jurisdictional  requirement.  See,  e.g.,  Beacon
Healthcare  Servs.,  Inc.  v.  Leavitt,  629  F.3d  981,  984  (9th  Cir.
