934  F.2d  1280,  1284-85  &  nn.6-7  (D.C.  Cir.  1991)  (describing
subsection  (a)(2)’s  amount-in-controversy  provision  as  a  “juris-
dictiona]  requirement”).

In  its  cases,  moreover,  the  PRRB  has  similarly  conceived
of  subsection  (a)(2)  as  a  jurisdictional  requirement.
See,  e.g.,
Interim  Health  Care  v.  BlueCross  BlueShield  Ass’n,  No.  2010-
D49,  2010  WL  4211383,  at  *4  (PRRB  Sept.  24,  2010)  (analogizing
subsection  (a)(2)’s  amount-in-controversy  requirement  to  that
of  28  U.S.C.  § 1332  and  using  criteria  developed  under  § 1332
to  calculate  the  amount  in  controversy);  Salt  Lake  Reg?  Med.
Ctr.  v.  BlueCross  BlueShield  Ass'n,  No.  2010-D39,  2010  WL
4214208,  at  *9  (PRRB  June  30,  2010)  (explaining  that  “the
amount  in controversy  is a  fundamental  requirement  for  Board
jurisdiction”).  Consistent  with  this  position,  current  HHS  regu-
lations  provide  that,  “[w]hen  ...  the  amount  in  controversy
changes  to  an  amount  less  than  the  minimum  for  a  Board
appeal  due  to...  .  [a] more  accurate  assessment  of the  amount  in
controversy,  the  Board  does  not  retain  jurisdiction.”  42  C.F.R.
§ 405.1839(c)(5)(B)  (2011).

23

final  determination  under  paragraph  (1)(A)(i),
or  with  respect  to  appeals  under  paragraph
(1)(A)Gi),  180  days  after  notice  of the  Secretary’s
final  determination,  or  with  respect  to  appeals
pursuant  to  paragraph  (1)(B)  or  (C),  within  180
days  after  notice  of  such  determination  would
have  been  received  if  such  determination  had
been  made  on  a  timely  basis.

42  U.S.C.  § 139500(a)(3).  This  timely  filing  require-
ment  directly  parallels
the  subject-matter  and
amount-in-controversy  requirements,  and  all  three
follow  conjunctively  after  the  phrase  indicating  that
an  otherwise  eligible  provider  may  obtain  review
only  “if”  all  three  requirements  are  met.  Given  their
identical  placement  in the  statute  as  conditions  upon
the  invocation  of the  Board’s  hearing  authority,  there
is simply  no  basis  for  treating  some  of these  require-
ments  as  jurisdictional  and  others  as  nonjurisdic-
tional.
All  three  operate  in  an  integrated  way  to
define  and  delimit  the  Board’s  authority  to  hear
provider  appeals.

Because  the  three  identically  situated  subsections
operate  in  conjunction  to  define  and  condition
the  Board’s  exercise  of  authority,  the  Board  or  a
reviewing  court  can  no  more  alter  the  timeliness
requirement  than  it  could  alter  the  subject-matter
or  amount-in-controversy  requirements.  If the  Board
adopted  a  regulation  stating  that  it  would  hear
appeals  “having  an  amount  in  controversy  of $5,000
for  good  cause  if  the  matter  involves  a  recurring
issue  central  to  the  administration  of Medicare  Part
A,”  that  regulation  would  undoubtedly  exceed  the
Board’s  authority.  It would  bring  within  the  Board’s
jurisdiction  cases  that  Congress  specified  it could  not
See  Lyng  v.  Payne,  476  U.S.  926,  937  (1986)
hear.

24

(“[A]n  agency’s  power  is  no  greater  than  that  dele-
An  agency  regulation
gated  to  it  by  Congress.”).
extending  the  time  for  filing  beyond  the  180  days
specified  by the  statute—or  a  judicial  decree  equita-
bly tolling  that  statutory  limit  on  the  agency’s  power
—has  the  same  effect.

The  fact  that  the  timely  filing  requirement  is  an
element  of a  statutory  provision  that  speaks  in juris-
dictional  terms  and  establishes  the  Board’s  powers
distinguishes  this  case  from  other  recent  cases  hold-
ing a  procedural  requirement  to  be  nonjurisdictional.
In  Arbaugh,  for  example,  this  Court  held  that

15  employees,  42  U.S.C.  § 2000e(b),  was  nonjurisdic-
tional,  reasoning  that  the  numerosity  requirement
did  not  appear  in “Title  VII’s  jurisdictional  provision,
42  U.S.C.  § 2000e-5(f)(3),”  but  rather  “in  a  separate
provision  that  ‘does  not  speak  in jurisdictional  terms
or  refer  in  any  way  to  the  jurisdiction  of the  district
546  U.S.  at  515  (quoting  Zipes,  455  U.S.
courts.”
at  394);  see  also  Zipes,  455  U.S.  at  393-94  (similarly
noting  that  the  Title  VII  provision  “specifying  the

§ 139500(a)  speaks  in jurisdictional  terms  by defining

14  Other  recent  cases  rest  on  a  similar  distinction.

For
example,  in Kontrick  v.  Ryan,  supra,  this  Court  declined  to  treat
a  timely  objection  requirement  in  bankruptcy  proceedings  as
jurisdictional,  despite  the  requirement’s  emphatic  wording.  In
reaching  that  conclusion,  the  Court  reasoned  that  the  require-
ment  in  question  did  not  originate  in  any  of the  statutes  defin-
ing  the  bankruptcy  court’s  subject-matter  jurisdiction,  but
rather  appeared  in  a  Federal  Rule  of  Bankruptcy  Procedure
promulgated  by this  Court  pursuant  to its authority  to  prescribe
“the  practice  and  procedure  in  cases  under  title  11,”  28  U.S.C.
§ 2075.  See  540  U.S.  at  453-54.  In  Eberhart  v.  United  States,
supra,  this  Court  relied  on  Kontrick’s  reasoning  to  hold  that
a  “closely  parallel”  set  of  requirements  governing  new  trial
motions  under  the  Federal  Rules  of  Criminal  Procedure  was
See  546  U.S.  at  15-16;  see  also  Owen
also  nonjurisdictional.
Equip.  &  Erection  Co.  v.  Kroger,  437  U.S.  365,  370  (1978)
(deeming  it  “axiomatic”  that  Court-prescribed  rules  of practice
and  procedure  “do  not  create  or  withdraw  federal!  jurisdiction”).
And  in  Scarborough  v.  Principi,  541  U.S.  401  (2004),  this  Court
held  that  the  statutory  deadline  for  filing  an  attorney’s  fee
application  under  the  Equal  Access  to  Justice  Act,  28  U.S.C.
§ 2412(d)(1)(B),  was  nonjurisdictional  because  it involved  a  form
of relief  ancillary  to  the  judgment  of a  court  that  already  had
jurisdiction  over  the  case.
In  contrast
with  all of those  cases,  the  timely  filing  requirement  at  issue  in
this  case  appears  in the  very  provision  that  defines  the  Board’s
jurisdiction.

See  541  U.S.  at  413.

the  Board’s  power  to  act,  this  Court  should  construe
its  component  requirements—including  the  180-day

This  Court’s  decision  in  Gonzalez  v.  Thaler,  supra,
does  not  require  a  different  result.
At  issue  in
Gonzalez  was  the  status  of a  procedural  requirement
for  appealing  a  district  court’s  final  order  in a  habeas
proceeding—namely,  the  requirement  that  the  “certi-

15  It  is  true  that  § 139500(a)  is  phrased  in  terms  of the  pro-
vider’s  right  to  seek  review  rather  than  the  Board’s  power  to
hear  an  appeal.  But  that  factor  does  not  negate  the  conclusion
that  § 139500(a)  speaks  in jurisdictional  terms.  In fact,  because
the  right  to  invoke  a  tribunal’s  power  is the  mirror  image  of the
tribunal's  power  to  hear  the  case,  jurisdictional  statutes  often
speak  in  terms  of a  litigant’s  conditional  right  to  invoke  the
tribunal’s  authority.  See,  e.g.,  16 U.S.C.  § 814  (establishing  the
conditions  pursuant  to  which a licensee  of the  Federal  Energy
Regulatory  Commission  may  acquire  certain  property  rights  “by
the  exercise  of the  right  of eminent  domain  in  the  district  court
of the  United  States  for  the  district  in  which  such  land  or  other
property  may  be  located,  or  in  the  State  courts”);  28  U.S.C.
§ 2101(c)  (“Any  other  appeal  or  any  writ  of certiorari  intended
to  bring  any  judgment  or  decree  in a civil  action,  suit  or  pro-
ceeding  before  the  Supreme  Court  for  review  shall  be  taken  or
applied  for  within  ninety  days  after  the  entry  of such  judgment
or  decree.”);  id.  § 2107(a)  (“[NJo  appeal  shall  bring  any  judg-
ment,  order  or  decree  in an  action,  suit  or  proceeding  of a civil
nature  before  a  court  of  appeals  for  review  unless  notice  of
appeal  is  filed,  within  thirty  days  after  the  entry  of such  judg-
ment,  order  or  decree.”);  id.  § 2253(c)(1)  (“Unless  a  circuit  jus-
tice  or  judge  issues a certificate  of appealability,  an  appeal  may
not  be  taken  to  the  court  of appeals  from  ...
the  final  order  in
a  habeas  corpus  proceeding  in  which  the  detention  complained
.  .”); see  also,
of arises  out  of process  issued  by a  State  court  ..

§ 2253(c)(1)  as  jurisdictional);  FEC  v.  NRA  Political  Victory
Fund,  513  U.S.  88,  90  (1994)  (holding  that  § 2101(c)  is jurisdic-

ficate  of  appealability”  needed  for  such  an  appeal

28  U.S.C.  § 2253(c)(3);
see  also  id.  § 2253(c)(2).  While  acknowledging  that
§ 2253(c)(1)—the  related  provision  conditioning  the
right  to  appeal  on  issuance  of a  certificate  of appeal-
ability—was  jurisdictional,  this  Court  found  that
§ 2253(c)(3)’s  specification  of  the  content  of  such  a

In  so  holding,  this  Court  rejected  the  government’s
argument  that  “the  placement  of  § 2253(c)(3)  in  a
section  containing  jurisdictional  provisions  signals
Rather,
that  it  too  is  jurisdictional.”
as  the  Court  noted,  “[mJere  proximity  will  not  turn
a  rule  that  speaks  in  nonjurisdictional  terms  into  a
jurisdictional  hurdle.”  Jd.  Indeed,  subsection  (c)(3)’s
“proximity  to”  provisions  that  spoke  in  terms  of  the
court  of appeals’  power  to  act  merely  served  to  “high-
light[]  the  absence  of  clear  jurisdictional  terms  in
§ 2253(c)(3).”  Id.'6

Jd.  at  651.

In contrast  with  the  provision  at  issue  in  Gonzalez,
§ 139500(a)(3)’s  180-day  filing  requirement  is  not
jurisdictional  merely  because  it is proximate  to  other

16  The  Court  in  Gonzalez  gave  particular  emphasis  to  the
disparate  framing  of subsections  (c)(1)  and  (c)(3).  See  132  S.  Ct.
at  649.  Subsection  (c)(1)  provides  that,  “[u)nless  a circuit  justice
or  judge  issues  a  certificate  of appealability,  an  appeal  may  not
be  taken  to  the  court  of appeals.”  28  U.S.C.  § 2253(c)(1).
In
contrast,  subsection  (c)(3)  states  merely  that  “[t]he  certificate  of
appealability  under  paragraph  (1)  shall  indicate  which  specific
issue  or  issues  satisfy  the  [substantia]  showing  of constitutional
deprivation]  required  by  paragraph  (2).”  Id.  § 2253(c)(3).  Un-
like  subsection  (c)(1),  therefore,  subsection  (c)(3)  is not  explicitly
phrased  in terms  that  delineate  the  power  of the  court  of appeals
to  act.

28

plainly  jurisdictional  provisions.
Rather,  it  is  part
and  parcel  of a  set  of three  integrated  provisions  that
together  define  the  Board’s  very  authority  to  grant
a  provider  a  hearing.  On  its  face,  subsection  (a)  as
a  whole  provides  for  the  Board’s  “Establishment”
and  expressly  “delineat[es]  the  classes  of  cases  ...
and  the  persons  ...  falling  within  [the  tribunal's]
adjudicatory  authority.”  Kontrick,  540  U.S.  at  455.
Indeed,  if  § 139500(a)  does  not  define  the  Board’s
jurisdiction,  then  no  provision  of  the  statute  does.
And,  in  contrast  with  the  habeas  statute  at  issue  in
Gonzalez,  nothing  about  the  placement  or  wording
of § 139500(a)’s  three  numbered  subsections  provides
any  basis  for  distinguishing  among  them  in  the  per-
formance  of that  classic  jurisdictional  function.

Nor  does  the  fact  that  subsection  (a)(3)  involves
a  timely  filing  requirement  negate  the  jurisdictional
function  that  it serves  in  subsection  (a).  To  be  sure,
this  Court  in  Henderson  v.  Shinseki,  supra,  recently
described  timely  filing  requirements  as  “quintessen-
tial  claim-processing  rules.”  131  S.  Ct.  at  1203.  But
it  also  made  clear  that,  even  where  a  timely  filing
requirement  is concerned,  Congress  is “free  to  attach
the  conditions  that  go  with  the  jurisdictional  label  to
a  rule  that  we  would  prefer  to  call  a  claim-processing
That  is,  even  if  such  time  limits  are
rule.”
typically  claims-processing  rules,  Congress  may  at
times  choose  to  treat  them  as  jurisdictional  in  order
to  achieve  “a  broader  system-related  goal,  such  as
facilitating  the  administration  of claims,  limiting  the
scope  of  a  governmental  waiver  of  sovereign  immu-
nity,  or  promoting  judicial  efficiency.”  John  R.  Sand
& Gravel,  552  U.S.  at  133  (citations  omitted);  see  also
Bowles,  551  U.S.  at  213  (“[T]he  notion  of  subject-
matter  jurisdiction  obviously  extends  to  classes  of

Jd.

29

cases  ...  falling  within  a  court’s  adjudicatory  author-
ity,  but  it  is  no  less  jurisdictional  when  Congress
prohibits  federal  courts  from  adjudicating  an  other-
wise  legitimate  class  of cases  after  a  certain  period
has  elapsed  from  final  judgment.”)  (citations  and  in-
ternal  quotation  marks  omitted;  ellipsis  in  original).
Accordingly,  as  it  does  with  any  procedural  require-
ment,  this  Court  must  consult  all  the  applicable  tools
of statutory  construction  to  determine  whether  Con-
gress  clearly  intended  or  understood  a  particular
See
timely  filing  requirement  to  be  jurisdictional.
Henderson,  131  S. Ct.  at  1203,  1204-06.

It  is  true  that,  in  recent  years,  this  Court  has
tended  to  find  timely  filing  requirements  jurisdic-
tional  largely  in  those  contexts  in which  well-settled
precedents  had  long  treated  a  particular  category
of  timeliness  provisions  as  such.
See,  e.g.,  John  R.
Sand  &  Gravel,  552  U.S.  at  134-36  (periods  for  filing
claims  against  the  United  States  in various  iterations
of courts  of claims);  Bowles,  551  U.S.  at  209-10  (time
limits  for  filing  notices  of  appeal).
But  Henderson
itself  makes  plain  that  time  limits  may  be  jurisdic-
tional  even  when  they  do not  fall  into  such  a  category
and  that  all  the  traditional  tools  of  statutory  inter-
pretation  still  apply.  The  Court  in  Henderson  held
that  the  time  limit  there  at  issue—the  filing  period
for  a  notice  of appeal  from  one  non-Article  III  adju-
dicative  body  (the  Board  of  Veterans’  Appeals)  to
another  (the  U.S.  Court  of  Appeals  for  Veterans
Claims)—did  not  fall  within  any  category  whose
jurisdictional  status  had  been  established  by  long-
See  131  S.  Ct.  at  1203  (distin-
standing  precedent.
guishing  Bowles  on  the  ground  that  it  dealt  with
court-to-court  notices  of appeal).  Yet  the  Court  found
it  necessary  to  go  on  to  scrutinize  with  care  the  text,

30

context,  and  structure  of  the  legislation  governing
veterans  claims  in  order  to  determine  whether  Con-
gress  meant  that  particular  time  limit  to  be jurisdic-
tional.  See  id.  at  1204-06.

Jd.  at  1205.

Accordingly,  this  Court  found  the  timely  filing  re-
quirement  in  Henderson  to  be  nonjurisdictional  only
after  assuring  itself  that  the  text  of that  requirement
did  not  speak  in  jurisdictional  terms  or  in  any  way
indicate  that  Congress  “wanted  [it]  to  be  treated  as
in
having  jurisdictional  attributes.”
addition,  taking  a  page  from  other  recent  cases  (see
pp.  24-26  &  n.14,  supra),  the  Court  found  it  signifi-
cant  that  the  timely  filing  requirement  appeared  “in
a  subchapter  fof the  Veterans’  Judicial  Review  Act,
Pub.  L.  No.  100-687,  Div.  A,  102  Stat.  4105  (1988),]
entitled  ‘Procedure,”  and  not  in  the  “subchapter
entitled  ‘Organization  and  Jurisdiction.”  Jd.  at  1205.
Finally,  with  respect  to  the  statute’s  overall  struc-
ture,  the  Court  emphasized  that  the  “most  telling”
evidence  of legislative  intent  derived  from  “the  singu-
lar  characteristics  of  the  review  scheme  that  Con-
gress  created  for  the  adjudication  of veterans’  bene-
fits  claims.”
Because  the  scheme  as  a  whole
reflected  procedural  informality,  flexibility,  and  solli-
citude  for  veterans,  see  id.  at  1205-06,  treating  the
time  limit  at  issue  as  jurisdictional  “would  clash
sharply  with  [the overall]  scheme,”  id.  at  1206.17

Jd.

As  discussed  above,  in  sharp  con-
opposite  result.

trast  with  the  scheme  at  issue  in Henderson,  the  text

Even  more

make  plain  that  Congress  intended  the  180-day  filing

B.  The  Structure  Of  § 139500(a)  And  That  Of
The  Medicare  Act  As  A Whole  Clearly  Indi-
cate  That  Congress  Intended  Subsection
(a)(3)’s  180-Day  Time  Limit  To  Be  Strictly
Jurisdictional

This  Court  has  explained  that,  “‘[iJn  expounding  a
statute,  we  must  not  be  guided  by  a  single  sentence
or  member  of a  sentence,  but  look  to  the  provisions  of
the  whole  law,  and  to  its  object  and  policy.’”  United
States  Natl  Bank  of  Oregon  v.  Independent  Ins.
Agents  of Am.,  Inc.,  508  U.S.  439,  455  (1993)  (quoting
United  States  v.  Heirs  of Boisdore,  49  U.S.  (8  How.)
Indeed,  as  the  Court  has  noted,
113,  122  (1849)).
reading  a  particular  procedura)  requirement  in  the
context  of the  overall  statutory  scheme  may  provide
the  “most  telling”  evidence  of  whether  Congress
intended  that  requirement  to  be  jurisdictional.
Henderson,  131  S.  Ct.  at  1205.  The  Medicare  Act’s
structure  makes  clear  that  Congress  in  fact  meant
to  draw  the  strict  and  unyielding  limitation  that
§ 139500(a)(3)’s  unqualified  terms  suggest.

For  example,  a  provider
may  obtain  a  hearing  before  the  PRRB  only  if it has
first  filed  required  cost,  or  other,  reports  with  the
intermediary  or  the  Secretary  in  a  timely  fashion.
See  42  U.S.C.  § 139500(a).1®  In  addition,  the  statute
specifies  in precise  detail  which  claims  providers  may
bring  to  the  Board—and  which  claims  fall  outside  its
powers.  Subsection  (a)(1),  as  noted,  requires  that  a
provider  must  be  “dissatisfied  with  a  final  determi-
nation”  either  “of...  its  fiscal  intermediary  ...  as  to
the  amount  of total  program  reimbursement”  or  “of
the  Secretary  as  to  the  amount  of the  payment”  the
provider  is to  receive  under  the  prospective  payment
system.  Id.  § 139500(a)(1)(A)(j),  (ii).  Aside  from  that
class  of cases,  a  provider  may  appeal  only  the  inter-
mediary’s  failure  to  file  an  NPR  “on  a  timely  basis”—
and  then  only  if the  cost  report  or  supplemental  cost
report  filed  with  the  intermediary  “complied  with  the
rules  and  regulations  of  the  Secretary  relating  to
such  report.”  Jd.  § 139500(a)(1)(B),  (C).  The  statute
also  restricts  group  appeals  to  cases  in  which  “the
matters  in  controversy  involve  a common  question  of
fact  or  interpretation  of  law  or  regulations  and  the
amount  in  controversy  is,  in  the  aggregate,  $50,000
or  more.”  Id.  § 139500(b).

18  For  further  discussion  of the  underlying  deadline,  see  note

21,  infra.

33

Even  within  the  class  of  otherwise  allowable
claims,  moreover,  the  statute  expressly  excludes  from
review  certain  findings—such  as  the  intermediary’s
denial  of  reimbursement,  under  42  U.S.C.  § 1395y,
for  various  procedures  or  tests  that  are  “not  reason-
able  and  necessary”  or  are  “performed  more  frequent-
ly”  than  is  covered  by  the  statute,  id.  § 1395y(a)(1).
See  id.  § 139500(g){1).
Finally,  the  provision  autho-
rizing  expedited  judicial  review  makes  clear  that
neither  the  Board  nor  CMS  has  authority  to  enter-
tain  a  provider’s  challenges  to  the  validity  of  the
governing  regulations;  a  provider  must  submit  its
claims  for reimbursement  on  the  assumption  that  the
regulations  are  valid  and  contest  their  validity,  if at
all,  only  upon  judicial  review.  See  id.  § 139500(f)(1);
see  also  Bethesda  Hosp.  Ass'n  v.  Bowen,  485  U.S.
399,  406  &  n.4  (1988)  (drawing  that  inference  from
The  apparent  strictness  of  the
§ 139500(f)(1))."9
timely  filing  requirements  thus  fits  neatly  with,
rather  than  contradicts,  the  overall  tenor  of a  statute

19  The  Secretary  has  by regulation  denied  the  Board  authority
to  invalidate  regulations  issued  pursuant  to  the  Medicare  Act  or
CMS  Rulings  interpreting  the  statute  and  regulations.  See  42
C.F.R.  § 405.1867  (2011).  Accordingly,  to  avoid  needless  Board
proceedings  where  a  provider's  challenge  to  an  intermediary's
decision  turns  on  the  validity  of a  regulation  or  a CMS  Ruling,
see  H.R.  Rep.  No.  96-1167,  at  394  (1980),  reprinted  in  1980
U.S.C.C.A.N.  5526,  5757,  the  statute  gives  providers  “the  right
to  obtain  judicial  review  of any  action  of the  fiscal  intermediary
which  involves  a  question  of  law  or  regulations  relevant  to
the  matters  in  controversy  whenever  the  Board  determines...
that  it is  without  authority  to  decide  the  question.”  42  U.S.C.
A  provider  may  seek  such  a  determination
§ 139500(f)(1).
of  eligibility,  however,  only  if  that  provider  could  otherwise
“obtain  a  hearing  under  subsection  (a)  of this  section  and  has

34

that  generally  asks  providers  to  turn  square  corners
in pursuing  their  appellate  rights.?°

More  importantly,  the  Medicare  Act’s  disparate
treatment  of timely  filing  requirements  for  Medicare
providers,  on  the  one  hand,  and  for  individual  Medi-
care  beneficiaries,  on  the  other,  makes  clear  that  the
timeliness  requirements  applicable  to  the  former  are
jurisdictional.
Where  providers  are  concerned,  the
Act  adopts  a  set  of  strict  timely  filing  requirements
that  are  consistent  with  the  assumption  that  “each
of  the  tens  of  thousands  of  sophisticated  [Medicare
providers]  ...  is  generally  capable  of identifying  an
underpayment  in  its  own  NPR  within  the  180-day
time  period  specified  in  42  U.S.C.  § 139500(a)(3).”
Your  Home  Visiting  Nurse  Servs.,  525  U.S.  at  456.
For  individual  Medicare  beneficiaries,  however,  the
Act  borrowed  explicitly  flexible  timeliness  require-
ments  from  Social  Security  procedures  “that  Con-
gress  designed  to  be  ‘unusually  protective’  of  [indi-
vidual]  claimants.”  Bowen  v.  City  of New  York,  476
U.S  467,  480  (1986)  (quoting  Heckler  v.  Day,  467  U.S.
104  106-07  (1984)).

On  their  face,  the  time  requirements  prescribed
for  Medicare  provider  appeals  under  § 139500  are
framed  in  unqualified  terms.21_  The  particular  limit

received  at  any  such  hearing  even  though  inadmissible  under

21,  infra.

Board,  a  provider  must  have  “filed  a  required  cost  report  [with

at  issue  here  straightforwardly  insists  that  the

is notified  of the  Board’s  decision.”  Id.  § 139500(f)(1).
“(Wjithin  60  days  of  the  date  on  which  notice  of

42  U.S.C.  § 139500(a).  That  provision  simply  makes  compliance

condition  of appealing  that  NPR  to  the  Board.  The  administra-

offers  a  marked  contrast  to the  stricter  articulation  of the  appel-
late  time  limits  in subsection  (a)(3).

22  In  addition,  at  least  one  other  key  provision  is  expressly

judicia]  review  of the  Board’s  or  the  Secretary's  decisions,  the
statute  provides  for  prejudgment  interest  accruing  from  “the
first  day  of the  first  month  beginning  after  the  180-day  period
as  determined  pursuant  to  subsection  (a)(3)  of this  section.”  42
U.S.C.  § 139500(f)(2).  That  provision  has  the  obvious  purpose
of timing  prejudgment  interest  from  the  conclusion  of the  period
for seeking  Board  review.  Significantly,  the  statute  defines  that
period  in  the  unqualified  numerical  terms  of subsection  (a)(3)
rather  than  qualitatively  from,  for  example,  “the  close  of  the
period  for  obtaining  a  hearing  with  the  Board”  (as  one  might
expect  if some  flexibility  were  contemplated).

24  None  of this  analysis  is  meant  to  suggest  that  the  review

For  example,  much  like  the
provider  review  statute,  the  beneficiary  review  statute  includes
amount-in-controversy  requirements  for  at  least  some  appeals.
See  42  U.S.C.  § 1395ff(b)(1)(E).
The  analysis  here,  however,
focuses  on  the  statutes’  contrasting  approaches  to  building
administrative  flexibility  into  the timely  filing  requirements.

dure  most  analogous  to  the  PRRB  process—the  1972

efits]  decision  as  may  be prescribed  in  regulations  of

See  id.  § 1395ff(b)  (Supp.  II  1973)  (incorporating
§ 405(b)  by reference).  In  effect,  this  provision  sets  a
minimum  filing  period  of  six  months  but  gives  the

and  to  provide  for  extensions  of  that  deadline,  by

virtually  the  identical  period  of  180  days  for  a  pro-

istrative  discretion  to extend.

approach  to  timely  filing  requirements  in  the  two

26  Emphasizing  that  the  time  limit  appears  “in  a  statute  that
Congress  designed  to  be  ‘unusually  protective’  of  [individual]
claimants,”  this  Court  reasoned  that  authorizing  the  Secretary
to  extend  the  time  for  filing  “express[ed]  [a]  clear  [legislative]
intention  to  allow  tolling  in  some  cases.”  Bowen,  476  U.S.  at
480  (quoting  Heckler,  467  U.S.  at  106-07).  From  this  starting
point,  the  Court  had  no  difficulty  holding  that  other  forms  of
equitable  tolling  by the  judiciary  were  consistent  with  legisla-
tive  intent.
Jd.  The  Court  had  previously  concluded,  without
extended  reasoning,  that  § 405(g)’s  filing  period  is a  statute  of

Eldridge,  424  U.S.  319,  328  n.9  (1976);  Weinberger  v.  Salfi,  422
U.S.  749,  763-64  (1975).

23  To  be  sure,  “{njot  all  mandatory  ‘prescriptions,  however

130  S.  Ct.  at  596  (quoting  Arbaugh,  546  U.S.  at  510)  (ellipsis  in

40

This  Court  has  often  applied  the  familiar  rule  that,
“‘when  the  legislature  uses  certain  language  in
one  part  of  the  statute  and  different  language  in
another,  the  court  assumes  different  meanings  were
intended.’”  Sosa  v.  Alvarez-Machain,  542  U.S.  692,
711  n.9  (2004)  (quoting  2A  NORMAN  J.  SINGER,
STATUTES  AND  STATUTORY  CONSTRUCTION  § 46:06,  at
194  (6th  ed.  2000)).  In particular,  “[w]here  Congress
includes  particular  language  in  one  section  of  a
statute  but  omits  it  in  another  section  of  the  same
Act,  it  is  generally  presumed  that  Congress  acts
intentionally  and  purposely  in  the  disparate  inclu-
Russello  v.  United  States,  464
sion  or  exclusion.”
U.S.  16,  23  (1983)  (internal  quotation  marks  omitted).
The  provisions  of § 1395ff  show  that,  when  Congress
wished  to  confer  administrative  discretion  over  time-
liness  requirements,  it  knew  how  to  do  so.
See,
e.g.,  City  of  Chicago  v.  Environmental  Def.  Fund,
511  U.S.  328,  337-38  (1994)  (using  similar  structural
reasoning);  Central  Bank  of Denver,  N.A.  v.  First
Interstate  Bank  of Denver,  N.A.,  511  U.S.  164,  176-77
(1994)  (same).

In  the  end,  interpreting  § 139500(a)(3)  to  be  non-
jurisdictional  would  read  into  the  statute  a  kind  of
discretion  over  timely  filing  that  Congress  conferred
expressly  in  provisions  governing  Medicare  benefi-
ciaries  but  did  not  in  those  governing  Medicare  pro-
This  Court  should  not  construe  two  provi-
viders.
sions  worded  so  differently  to  mean  the  same  thing.
“The  statute  was  evidently  drawn  with  care.
Its
language  is  plain  and  unambiguous....  To  supply
omissions  transcends  the  judicial  function.”  Iselin  v.
United  States,  270  U.S.  245,  250-51  (1926).

(a)(3)'s  timely  filing  requirement  to  operate  as  a  jurisdictional
provision.

41

C.  The  Secretary’s  “Good  Cause”  Exception
To  The  180-Day  Filing  Requirement  Does
Not  Undermine
That
The
§ 139500(a)(3)  Is Jurisdictional

Conclusion

Because  respondents’  claims  fall  outside  the  three-
year  window  within  which  they  could  have  invoked
the  regulatory  “good  cause”  exception  to  the  180-day
time  limit,  see  Pet.  App.  55a;  42  C.F.R.  § 405.1841(b),
the  validity  of  that  regulation  is  not  before  this
Court.  Nonetheless,  it is  necessary  to  speak  briefly
to  the  question  whether  the  agency’s  “good  cause”  ex-
ception  undermines  the  conclusion  that  the  statutory
timely  filing  requirement  is jurisdictional.  In  partic-
ular,  because  jurisdictional]  timely  filing  requirements
admit  of no  equitable  exceptions,  if the  agency’s  posi-
tion  on  the  “good  cause”  exception  were  entitled
to  deference  under  Chevron  U.S.A.  Inc.  v.  Natural
Resources  Defense  Council,  Inc.,  467  U.S.  837  (1984),
that  determination  would  undercut  any  further  con-
clusion  that  subsection  (a)(3)  is  jurisdictional].
See
Western  Med.  Enters.,  Inc.  v.  Heckler,  783  F.2d  1376,
1379-80  (9th  Cir.  1986).  Yet,  while  the  regulations
containing  the  “good  cause”  exception  are  eligible  for
consideration  under  the  Chevron  framework,*°  the
position  on  jurisdiction  implicit  in  those  regulations

39  See  Your  Home  Visiting  Nurse  Servs.,  525  U.S.  at  453-54.
The  Secretary  has  statutory  authority  to  promulgate  regula-
tions  to  carry  the  Medicare  Act  into  effect.  See,  e.g.,  42  U.S.C.
§§ 1302(a),  1395l(a),  1395hh(a)(1).
The  Secretary  employed
notice-and-comment  rulemaking  procedures  to  adopt  the  version
of the  “good  cause”  exception  that  governs  this  case,  as  well  as
the  somewhat  stricter  version  now  in  effect.  See  39  Fed.  Reg.
34,515  (Sept.  26,  1974);  73  Fed.  Reg.  30,190  (May  23,  2008);
see  also  note  6, supra.  Accordingly,  both  sets  of regulations  are
Chevron-eligible  under  United  States  v.  Mead  Corp.,  533  U.S.
218,  226-27,  229-30  (2001).

42

does  not  merit  this  Court’s  deference  because  it con-
tradicts  the  clear  import  of the  statute.

467  U.S.  at  842.

Chevron  instructs  a  reviewing  court  to  ask  first
whether  Congress  “has  directly  spoken  to  the  precise
question  at  issue.”
“If  the  intent
of  Congress  is  clear,”  the  Court  explained,  “that
is  the  end  of  the  matter;  for  the  court,  as  well  as
the  agency,  must  give  effect  to  the  unambiguously
expressed  intent  of Congress.”  Id.  at  842-43.  Only  if
the  statute  is “silent  or  ambiguous”  must  a  reviewing
court  accept  the  agency’s  interpretation,  provided
that  it is “permissible”  or  “reasonable.”  Id.  at  843-44.
As  discussed,  see  Parts  A and  B, supra,  the  text  and
structure  of § 139500(a)  and  the  overall  structure  of
the  Medicare  Act  make  clear  that  the  180-day  limit
Indeed,
for  filing  with  the  Board  is  jurisdictional.
because  the  Court  has  indicated  that  it  will  treat  a
procedural  rule  as  nonjurisdictional  unless  Congress
has  given  some  “‘clear’  indication  that  [it]  wanted
the  rule  to  be  ‘Sjurisdictional,’”  Henderson,  131  S.  Ct.
at  1203,  any  statutory  finding  requisite  to  satisfy
the  Court’s  jurisdictional  test  should  also  satisfy
Chevron’s  first  step.

Nor  does  the  agency’s  embrace  of  a  “good  cause”
exception  offer  any  persuasive  indirect  evidence  that
Congress,  in  fact,  meant  § 139500(a)(3)  to  be  non-
jurisdictional.?!_  The  agencies  responsible  for  imple-

31  Quite  apart  from  Chevron,  the  Court  will  “give  an  agency's
interpretations  and  practices  considerable  weight  where  they
involve  the  contemporaneous  construction  of  a  statute  and
where  they  have  been  in long  use.”  Davis  v.  United  States,  495
U.S.  472,  484  (1990).  This  practice  presupposes  that  the  views
of those  charged  with  implementing  a  statute—and,  thus,  most
familiar  with  its  political  context—may  provide  at  least  indirect
evidence  of what  it  means.  See,  e.g.,  Andrus  v.  Shell  Oil  Co.,

43

menting  § 139500  have  never  adopted  a  clearly
articulated  or  consistently  held  position  that  sub-
section  (a)(3)  is  nonjurisdictional.  Rather,  the  views
expressed  by  the  agencies,  if anything,  tend  to  pro-
vide  somewhat  greater  support  for  the  conclusion
that  the  limitation  is jurisdictional.  Even  though  the
Secretary  adopted  the  “good  cause”  exception  almost
contemporaneously  with  § 139500’s  enactment,  the
regulation’s  statement  of basis  and  purpose  neither
explained  the  legal  basis  for the  agency’s  authority  to
adopt  such  an  exception  nor  addressed  the  question
whether  § 139500(a)  is  jurisdictional.
See  39  Fed.
Reg.  34,515  (Sept.  26,  1974).  And,  when  the  agencies
responsible  for administering  the  statute  have  spoken
to  the  jurisdictional  status  of § 139500(a)  in  contexts
other  than  the  “good  cause”  exception,  they  have  rou-
tinely  interpreted  it as  a jurisdictional  requirement.

The  Board,  for  example,  “has  consistently  treated
[{§ 139500(a)’s]  three  criteria—dissatisfaction,  amount
and  timeliness—as  jurisdictional
in  controversy,
requirements.”

44

1064771,  at  *2  (HCFA  Adm’r  Dec.  21,  1998).  At  least
some  of  these  cases,  moreover,  have  quite  plainly
used  “jurisdiction”  in  the  precise  technical  sense
developed  in  this  Court’s  post-Kontrick  case  law.  In
Marion  General  Hospital  v.  Blue  Cross  & Blue  Shield
Ass'n,  No.  2007-D8,  2007  WL  1004392  (CMS  Adm’r
Feb.  12,  2007),  for  example,  CMS  made  clear  that  it
does  not  matter  when  the  intermediary  interposes  a
timeliness  objection  before  the  Board  because  “juris-
diction  cannot  be  waived  and  may  be  raised  at  any
time.”  Id.  at  *4  & n.9.  Similarly,  in cases  arising  out
of the  DSH  calculation  that  underpins  this  litigation,
the  Board  has  repeatedly  explained  that  the  time
limits  prescribed  by  the  statute  and  by  the  regula-
tions  that  (purport  to)  implement  it are  jurisdictional
and  not  subject  to equitable  tolling.  See,  e.g.,  Medical
Coll.  of Georgia  Hosp.  v.  BlueCross  BlueShield  Ass'n,
No.  2010-D30,  2010  WL  4214222,  at  *3  (PRRB  May
25,  2010),  aff'd,  2010  WL  5570990  (CMS  Adm’r  July
13,  2010);  Valley  Presbyterian  Hosp.  v.  BlueCross
BlueShield  Ass'n,  No.  2009-D18,  2009  WL  1973490,
at  *3  (PRRB  Apr.  9, 2009);  SKI  1987-1994,  2009  WL
981317,  at  *4.33

33  In  reaffirming  that  the  timeliness  requirement  is jurisdic-
tional,  the  Board  thus  explained  that  “[t]he  time  limit  for filing
is  embedded  in  the  legislation  establishing  the  very  right  to  a
hearing  and  clearly  indicates  Congress’  intent  that  filing  within
the  time  specified  is a  condition  precedent  to  the  right  to  a  hear-
ing.”  SKI  1987-1994,  2009  WL  981317,  at  *5.  The  Board  added
that  it possesses  “only  the  powers  granted  to  it  by statute  and
regulation”  and  that  “Congress  imposed  time  limits  for  filing
appeals  to  the  Board  and  did  not  confer  power  to  apply  equita-
ble  tolling  principles  to  extend  that  time.”  Id.  at  *4.  Although
the  Board  relied  in  part  on  the  Eighth  Circuit’s  decision  in
St.  Joseph's  Hospital  v.  Heckler,  786  F.2d  848  (8th  Cir.  1986),
its  position  also  rested  independently  on  its  own  reading  of the

45

While  HHS  regulations  did  not  speak  directly  to
the  jurisdictional  status  of  the  timeliness  require-
ment  until  recently,  the  2008  amendments  make
clear  that  the  Secretary  interprets  § 139500(a)’s
requirements  to  be  jurisdictional.
In  a  provision
entitled  “Board  Jurisdiction,”  the  current  regulations
specify  that,  before  exercising  its  adjudicatory  powers
(such  as  conducting  discovery,  issuing  subpoenas,  or
holding  hearings),  “[t]he  Board  must  make  a  prelim-
inary  determination  of  the  scope  of  its  jurisdiction
(that  is, whether  the  request  for  hearing  was  timely,
and  whether  the  amount  in  controversy  requirement
has  been  met),  if  any,  over  the  matters  at  issue
42  C.F.R.  § 405.1840(a)(2)  (2011).
in  the  appeal.”
The  rule  further  states  that  “[t]he  Board  has  juris-
diction  to  grant  a  hearing  over  a  specific  matter  at
issue  in  an  appeal  only  if the  provider  has  a  right
to  a  Board  hearing  ...  under  § 405.1835  of  this
subpart.”  Id.  § 405.1840(b).  Putting  to  one  side  the
“good  cause”  exception  discussed  below  (see  pp.  46-47,
infra),  § 405.1835  merely  incorporates  and  elaborates
on  the  three  requirements  specified  by  § 139500(a).
Consistent  with  well-settled  understandings  of juris-
dictional]  limitations,  the  regulations  also  specify  that
the  Board’s  preliminary  determination  of its jurisdic-
tion  may  be  revised  “at  any  subsequent  stage  of the
proceedings.”  42  C.F.R.  § 405.1840(a)(3)  (2011).%4

statute  and  the  Secretary’s  regulations.
2009  WL  981317,  at  *5-6.

See  SKI  1987-1994,

34  This  Court  has  long held  that  a challenge  to  subject-matter
jurisdiction  may  be raised  at  any  stage  of the  proceedings.  See,
e.g.,  Mansfield,  C.  & L.M.  Ry.  Co.  v.  Swan,  111  U.S.  379,  382
(1884).  Agency  guidance  documents  confirm  the  understanding
that  an  objection  under  § 139500(a)  cannot  be  waived  and  can
be  raised  at  any  time.  In  the  Rules  published  by the  Board  to

46

Although  both  the  Secretary  and  the  Board  have
now  reaffirmed  that  the  requirements  of § 139500(a)
are  jurisdictional,  neither  has  yet  to  reconcile  the
regulatory  “good  cause”  exception  with  the  jurisdic-
tional  status  of subsection  (a)(3).  As  discussed,  this
Court’s  case  law  makes  clear  that  a  jurisdictional
requirement  is  absolute  and  subject  to  neither  for-
feiture,  nor  waiver,  nor  equitable  tolling.  See  p.  15,
supra.
And  because  a  jurisdictional  requirement
defines  the  scope  of a  tribunal’s  power,  the  tribunal
See,  e.g.,
lacks  authority  to  relax  such  a  limitation.
Kendall  v.  United  States,  107  U.S.  123,  125  (1883).
The  Secretary  herself  has  recognized  but  not  resolved
the  internal  tension  in  the  agency’s  positions.  When
the  Secretary  initiated  the  latest  round  of revisions
to  the  implementing  regulations,  the  notice  of  pro-
posed  rulemaking  identified  the  split  in  the  circuits
on  the  status  of § 139500(a)(3)  and  stated:

[W]e  see  some  merit  to  both  sides  of  the  split
of judicial  authority  as  to  our  authority  to  pro-
vide  for  good  cause  extensions  ....  Although  our
proposal  to  retain  and  revise  these  regulations
is  based  primarily  on  policy  considerations,  we
note  that  adoption  of this  proposal  may  result  in
additional  court  decisions  and  lead  to  a  definitive

implement  § 139500,  Rule  4.4—entitled  “Dismissal  for  Lack  of
“Appeals  that  fail  to  meet  the  jurisdic-
Jurisdiction”—states:
tional  requirements  will  be  dismissed.  A  jurisdictional  chal-
lenge  may  be  raised  at  any  time  during  the  appeal  ....  The
Board  may  review  jurisdiction  on  its  own  motion  at  any  time.
The  parties  cannot  waive  jurisdictional  requirements.”  PRRB,
Provider  Reimbursement  Review  Board  Rules  at  3 (effec.  Aug.
21,  2008),  available  at  http://www.cms.gov/Regulations-and-
Guidance/Review-Boards/PRR  BReview/downloads/PRRBRules

47

resolution  of whether  the  regulations  are  consis-
tent  with  section  [139500(a)(3)}.

69  Fed.  Reg.  35,716,  35,725  (June  25,  2004).  In  the
statement  of  basis  and  purpose  accompanying  the
new  rule,  the  Secretary  offered  only  the  following
account  of the  agency’s  authority  to  retain  the  “good
cause”  exception:

[Ujnder  [42  U.S.C.  §§ 1302(a)  and  1395hh(a)],
which  give  the  Secretary  authority  to  issue
regulations  for  the  efficient  administration  of the
Medicare  program,  we  believe  we  are  authorized
to  provide  for  discretionary  grants  and  denials
of  requests  to  extend  the  time  for  requesting  a
hearing  ....

73  Fed.  Reg.  at  30,206.

an

Of  course,

agency’s  rulemaking  authority
does  not  extend  beyond  the  scope  of  the  underlying
authority  delegated  to  it by statute,  see,  e.g.,  Lyng  v.
Payne,  476  U.S.  926,  937  (1986),  and  a  grant  of rule-
making  power  cannot  empower  an  agency  to  alter  the
jurisdictional  or  nonjurisdictional  status  of  a  statu-
tory  procedure,  see,  e.g.,  Union  Pacific,  130  S.  Ct.  at
590;  cf. Kontrick,  540  U.S.  at  452.  Thus,  the  agency
has  yet  to  offer  a  reasoned  explanation  of how  it  is
possible  to  treat  § 139500(a)(3)  as  jurisdictional  while
simultaneously  subjecting  it  to  exceptions  nowhere
specified  by the  statute.  For  the  Court  to  hold  that
the  requirements  of § 139500(a)(3)  are  jurisdictional,
therefore,  would  not  contradict  any  clearly  articu-
lated  or  consistently  held  agency  understanding  of its
statutory  authority.*5

35  The  Secretary’s  regulations  also  provide  that  the  Board
may  reopen  its  prior  decisions,  but  this  set  of regulations  is con-

48

sistent  with  the  conclusion  that  § 139500(a)(3)  is jurisdictional.
Within  three  years  after  issuing  a  decision,  the  Board  may
reopen  that  decision  “either  through  [its]  own  motion  ...  or
by  granting  the  request  of  the  provider  affected  by  the  ...
decision.”
42  C.F.R.  § 405.1885(a)(2)  (2011);  see  also  id.
§ 405.1885(a)  (pre-2008  regulation  recognizing  similar  reopen-
ing  authority).  Though  less  carefully  defined,  this  authority  is
analogous  to  the  district  court’s  power  to  reopen  a  judgment
under  Federal  Rule  of Civil  Procedure  60(b)}—which  recognizes
and  codifies  a  court’s  traditional  discretion  to  set  aside  a  final
judgment  “whose  enforcement  would  work  inequity.”  Plaut  v.
Spendthrift  Farms,  Inc.,  514  U.S.  211,  233-34  (1998).

Nothing  inherent  in  the  authority  to  grant  relief  from  a  final
judgment  undercuts  the  time  requirements  for  filing  an  action
(or  appeal)  in  the  first  instance.  To  be  sure,  one  could  construe
a  reopening  provision  in a  way  that  permits  the  parties  to  make
For  example,  if the
an  end  run  around  such  a  requirement.
Board  permitted  a  party,  on  reopening,  to  raise  novel  issues
that  had  not  been  part  of  the  original  appeal  and  would
otherwise  be  untimely,  such  an  interpretation  of the  reopening
provision  might  conflict  with  the  spirit,  if  not  the  letter,  of
§ 139500(a).  Yet  nothing  in  the  reopening  regulation  compels
such  an  interpretation,  and  there  is at  least  some  indication,  in
a  guidance  document,  that  the  responsible  agencies  understand
the  need  to  avoid  that  outcome.
See  HHS,  Medicare  Provider
Reimbursement  Manual—Part  1,  § 2931.1(D)  (“Issues  which
were  not  raised  at  the  Board  hearing  may  not  be  reopened  by
the  Board.”).  Indeed,  in  the  context  of an  intermediary’s  similar
power  to  reopen  an  NPR,  see  42  C.F.R.  § 405.1885(a),  the  federal
courts  and  the  Board  have  taken  pains  to  construe  the  Secre-
tary’s  reopening  regulations  to  preserve  the  efficacy  of  timely
filing  requirements,  including  the  one  at  issue  here.  See,  e.g.,
Your  Home  Visiting  Nurse  Servs.,  525  U.S.  at  451;  HCA  Health
Serus.  of Oklahoma,  Inc.  v.  Shalala,  27  F.3d  614,  620  (D.C.  Cir.
1994);  Texas  Disproportionate  Share  v.  Various,  No.  95-0800GE,
1996  WL  887660,  at  *7  (HCFA  Adm’r  July  22,  1996).  Because
there  is  no  intrinsic  conflict  between  the  reopening  provision
and  strict  enforcement  of the  180-day  timely  filing  requirement,
the  mere  existence  of that  reopening  provision  does  not  under-
cut  the jurisdictional  status  of that  requirement.

49

CONCLUSION

The  judgment  of  the  court  of  appeals  should  be

reversed.

la

STATUTORY  PROVISIONS  INVOLVED

42  U.S.C.  § 139500  provides:

§ 139500.

Provider
Board

(a)  Establishment

Reimbursement

Review

Any  provider  of services  which  has  filed  a  required
cost  report  within  the  time  specified  in  regulations
may  obtain  a  hearing  with  respect  to  such  cost  report
by a  Provider  Reimbursement  Review  Board  (herein-
after  referred  to  as  the  “Board”)  which  shall  be  estab-
lished  by the  Secretary  in accordance  with  subsection
(h)  of this  section  and  (except  as  provided  in  subsec-
tion  (g)(2)  of this  section)  any  hospital  which  receives
payments  in  amounts  computed  under  subsection
(b)  or  (d)  of section  1395ww  of  this  title  and  which
has  submitted  such  reports  within  such  time  as  the
Secretary  may  require  in  order  to  make  payment
under  such  section  may  obtain  a  hearing  with  respect
to  such  payment  by the  Board,  if—

(1) such  provider—

(A)(i)  is dissatisfied  with  a  final  determination
of the  organization  serving  as  its  fiscal  interme-
diary  pursuant  to  section  1395h  of this  title  as  to
the  amount  of total  program  reimbursement  due
the  provider  for  the  items  and  services  furnished
to  individuals  for  which  payment  may  be  made
under  this  subchapter  for  the  period  covered  by
such  report,  or

(ii)  is dissatisfied  with  a  final  determination  of
the  Secretary  as  to  the  amount  of  the  payment
under  subsection  (b)  or  (d)  of section  1395ww  of
this  title,

(B)  has  not  received  such  final  determination
from  such  intermediary  on  a  timely  basis  after

2a

filing  such  report,  where  such  report  complied
with  the  rules  and  regulations  of  the  Secretary
relating  to  such  report,  or

(C)  has  not  received  such  final  determination
on  a  timely  basis  after  filing  a  supplementary
cost  report,  where  such  cost  report  did  not  so
comply  and  such  supplementary  cost  report  did
so  comply,

(2) the  amount  in controversy  is $10,000  or  more,

and

(3)  such  provider  files  a  request  for  a  hearing
within  180  days  after  notice  of the  intermediary’s
final  determination  under  paragraph  (1)(A)(i),  or
with  respect  to  appeals  under  paragraph  (1)(A)(ii),
180  days  after  notice  of the  Secretary’s  final  deter-
mination,  or  with  respect  to  appeals  pursuant  to
paragraph  (1)(B)  or  (C),  within  180  days  after  notice
of  such  determination  would  have  been  received
if such  determination  had  been  made  on  a timely
basis.

(b)  Appeals  by groups

The  provisions  of subsection  (a) of this  section  shall
apply  to  any  group  of  providers  of  services  if  each
provider  of  services  in  such  group  would,  upon  the
filing  of an  appeal  (but  without  regard  to  the  $10,000
limitation),  be  entitled  to  such  a  hearing,  but  only  if
the  matters  in  controversy  involve  a  common  ques-
tion  of  fact  or  interpretation  of  law  or  regulations
and  the  amount  in  controversy  is,  in  the  aggregate,
$50,000  or  more.

(c)  Right  to  counsel;  rules  of evidence

At  such  hearing,  the  provider  of  services  shall
have  the  right  to  be  represented  by counsel,  to  intro-
duce  evidence,  and  to  examine  and  cross-examine
Evidence  may  be  received  at  any  such
witnesses.

3a

hearing  even  though  inadmissible  under  rules  of
evidence  applicable  to court  procedure.

(d)  Decisions  of Board

A  decision  by  the  Board  shall  be  based  upon
the  record  made  at  such  hearing,  which  shall  include
the  evidence  considered  by  the  intermediary  and
such  other  evidence  as  may  be  obtained  or  received
by the  Board,  and  shall  be  supported  by  substantial
evidence  when  the  record  is viewed  as  a  whole.  The
Board  shall  have  the  power  to  affirm,  modify,  or  re-
verse  a  final  determination  of the  fiscal  intermediary
with  respect  to  a  cost  report  and  to  make  any  other
revisions  on  matters  covered  by such  cost  report  (in-
cluding  revisions  adverse  to  the  provider  of services)
even  though  such  matters  were  not  considered  by the
intermediary  in making  such  final  determination.
(e)  Rules  and  regulations

The  Board  shall  have  full  power  and  authority
to  make  rules  and  establish  procedures,  not  in-
consistent  with  the  provisions  of  this  subchapter  or
regulations  of  the  Secretary,  which  are  necessary
or  appropriate  to  carry  out  the  provisions  of  this
section.  In  the  course  of any  hearing  the  Board  may
administer  oaths  and  affirmations.
The  provisions
of  subsections  (d)  and  (e)  of  section  405  of  this  title
with  respect  to  subpenas  shall  apply  to  the  Board  to
the  same  extent  as  they  apply  to  the  Secretary  with
respect  to  subchapter  II of this  chapter.
(f)  Finality  of  decision;  judicial  review;  deter-
minations  of  Board  authority;  jurisdiction;
venue;  interest  on  amount  in controversy
(1) A decision  of the  Board  shall  be final  unless  the
Secretary,  on  his  own  motion,  and  within  60  days
after  the  provider  of services  is notified  of the  Board’s
decision,  reverses,  affirms,  or  modifies  the  Board’s

4a

decision.
Providers  shall  have  the  right  to  obtain
judicial  review  of any  final  decision  of the  Board,  or
of  any  reversal,  affirmance,  or  modification  by  the
Secretary,  by  a  civil  action  commenced  within  60
days  of the  date  on  which  notice  of any  final  decision
by the  Board  or  of any  reversal,  affirmance,  or  mod-
ification  by the  Secretary  is received.  Providers  shall
also  have  the  right  to  obtain  judicial  review  of any
action  of  the  fiscal  intermediary  which  involves  a
question  of law  or  regulations  relevant  to the  matters
in  controversy  whenever  the  Boe  i  determines  (on
its  own  motion  or  at  the  request  of a  provider  of ser-
vices  as  described  in the  following  sentence)  that  it is
without  authority  to  decide  the  question,  by a civil
action  commenced  within  sixty  days  of  the  date  on
which  notification  of such  determination  is received.
If a  provider  of services  may  obtain  a  hearing  under
subsection  (a)  of this  section  and  has  filed  a  request
for  such  a  hearing,  such  provider  may  file  a  request
for  a  determination  by  the  Board  of its  authority  to
decide  the  question  of  law  or  regulations  relevant
to  the  matters  in  controversy  (accompanied  by  such
documents  and  materials  as  the  Board  shall  require
for  purposes  of  rendering  such  determination).  The
Board  shall  render  such  determination  in  writing
within  thirty  days  after  the  Board  receives  the
request  and  such  accompanying  documents  and
materials,  and  the  determination  shall  be considered
a  final  decision  and  not  subject  to  review  by the  Sec-
retary.  If the  Board  fails  to  render  such  determina-
tion  within  such  period,  the  provider  may  bring  a
civil  action  (within  sixty days of the end  of such  period)
with  respect  to  the  matter  in  controversy  contained
in  such  request  for  a  hearing.  Such  action  shall  be
brought  in  the  district  court  of the  United  States  for
the  judicial  district  in  which  the  provider  is  located
(or,  in  an  action  brought  jointly  by several  providers,

5a

the  judicial  district  in  which  the  greatest  number  of
such  providers  are  located)  or  in  the  District  Court
for  the  District  of  Columbia  and  shall  be  tried  pur-
suant  to  the  applicable  provisions  under  chapter  7
of  Title  5  notwithstanding  any  other  provisions  in
section  405  of  this  title.  Any  appeal  to  the  Board
or  action  for  judicial  review  by  providers  which  are
under  common  ownership  or  control  or  which  have
obtained  a  hearing  under  subsection  (b)  of  this
section  must  be  brought  by  such  providers  as  a
group  with  respect  to  any  matter  involving  an  issue
common  to  such  providers.

(2)  Where  a  provider  seeks  judicial  review  pur-
suant  to  paragraph  (1),  the  amount  in  controversy
shall  be  subject  to  annual  interest  beginning  on  the
first  day  of the  first  month  beginning  after  the  180-
day  period  as  determined  pvrsuant  to  subsection
(a)(3)  of this  section  and  equal  to  the  rate  of interest
on  obligations  issued  for  purchase  by  the  Federal
Hospital  Insurance  Trust  Fund  for  the  month  in
which  the  civil  action  authorized  under  paragraph
(1)  is  commenced,  to  be  awarded  by  the  reviewing
court  in favor  of the  prevailing  party.

(3)  No  interest  awarded  pursuant  to  paragraph  (2)
shall  be  deemed  income  or  cost  for  the  purposes  of
determining  reimbursement  due  providers  under  this
chapter.

(.)  Certain  findings  not  reviewable

(1)  The  finding  of  a  fiscal  intermediary  that  no
payment  may  be  made  under  this  subchapter  for  any
expenses  incurred  for  items  or  services  furnished  to
an  individual  because  such  items  or  services  are
listed  in  section  1395y  of  this  title  shall  not  be
reviewed  by  the  Board,  or  by  any  court  pursuant  to
an  action  brought  under  subsection  (f) of this  section.

6a

(2)  The  determinations  and  other  decisions  de-
scribed  in  section  1395ww(d)(7)  of this  title  shall  not
be  reviewed  by the  Board  or  by any  court  pursuant  to
an  action  brought  under  subsection  (f)  of this  section
or  otherwise.
(h)  Composition  and  compensation

The  Board  shal]  be  composed  of  five  members
appointed  by  the  Secretary  without  regard  to  the
provisions  of Title  5  governing  appointments  in  the
competitive  services.  Two  of such  members  shall  be
All  of  the
representative  of  providers  of  services.
members  of  the  Board  shall  be  persons  knowledge-
able  in  the  field  of payment  of providers  of services,
and  at  least  one  of  them  shall  be  a  certified  public
accountant.  Members  of the  Board  shall  be  entitled
to  receive  compensation  at  rates  fixed  by  the  Secre-
tary,  but  not  exceeding  the  rate  specified  (at the  time
the  service  involved  is  rendered  by  such  members)
for  grade  GS-18  in  section  5332  of Title  5.  The  term
of office  shall  be  three  years,  except  that  the  Secre-
tary  shall  appoint  the  initial  members  of  the  Board
for  shorter  terms  to  the  extent  necessary  to  permit
staggered  terms  of office.

(i)  Technical  and  clerical  assistance

The  Board  is  authorized  to  engage  such  technical
assistance  as  may  be  required  to  carry  out  its
functions,  and  the  Secretary  shall,  in  addition,  make
available  to  the  Board  such  secretarial,  clerical,  and
other  assistance  as  the  Board  may  require  to  carry
out  its functions.

(j) “Provider  of services”  defined

In  this  section,  the  term  “provider  of  services”
includes  a  rural  health  clinic  and  a  Federally  quali-
fied  health  center.

COMPLAINT

Plaintiffs,  by and  through  their  undersigned  attor-
neys,  bring  this  action  against  defendant  Michael  O.
Leavitt,  in his official  capacity  as  Secretary  of the  Unit-

16

ed  States  Department  of Health  and  Human  Services,
and  state  as  follows:

A.  Preliminary  Statement

1.  This  action  arises  under  Title  XVIII  of the  Social
Security  Act,  42  U.S.C.  §$1395  et  seq.  (“the  Medicare
Act”),  and  the  Administrative  Procedure  Act  (“APA”),
5 U.S.C.  §§ 551  et seq.  The  plaintiff-hospitals  (“the  Hos-
pitals”)  seek  to  have  their  Medicare  disproportionate
share  hospital  (“DSH”)  payment  determinations  for the
tiseal  years  (“F Ys”)  at issue  recalculated  because  those
payment  determinations  were  made  using incorrect  da-
ta.  Defendant,  the  Secretary  (“the  Secretary”)  of the
Department  of  Health  and  Human  Services  (“HHS”),
has  refused  to consider  the  Hospitals’  requests  for relief
on  the  grounds  that  the  Hospitals  did  not  timely  file
their  administrative  appeals.  Here,  however,  the  time
for  filing  these  appeals  should  be  equitably  tolled  be-
cause  the  Hospitals’  failure  to file timely  appeals was  the
direct  result  of  the  Secretary’s  knowing  and  unlawful
refusal  to  inform  the  Hospitals  that  the  DSH  payment
determinations  at  issue  were  based  on  incorrect  data.
Medicare  hospitals  generally,  and  the  Hospitals  specifi-
cally,  were  made  aware  of these  flaws  when  an  adminis-
trative  decision  identifying  them  was  issued  in  2006.
The  Hospitals  hereby  seek  an  order  stating  that  the
Provider  Reimbursement  Review  Board  (“Board”  or
“PRRB”)  has jurisdiction  over  the  appeals  at issue  and
remanding  this  matter  to  the  Board  to  determine
whether  correct  data  was  used  in  the  Hospitals’  DSH
determinations  for  the  FYs  at issue  in this  action.

and Venue

12.  Defendant  Michael  O. Leavitt  is the  Secretary  of
the  Department  of Health  and  Human  Services,  the fed-
eral  department  which  contains  the  Centers  for  Medi-
care  & Medicaid  Services  (“CMS”).  The  Secretary,  the
federal  official  responsible  for  administration  of  the
Medicare  Program,  has  delegated  the  responsibility  to
administer  that  program  to CMS.  Before  June  14, 2001,
CMS  was  known  as  the  Health  Care  Financing  Admin-
istration  (“HCFA”).  In this  complaint,  plaintiffs  refer  to

20

the  agency  as  CMS  or  HCFA,  as  appropriate,  depending
on  the  context.

dD.

>

ound  of  the

Medicare  Program

13.  The  Medicare  Act  establishes  a system  of health
insurance  for the  aged,  disabled,  and  individuals  afflict-
ed  with  end-stage  renal  disease.  Pursuant  to  42  U.S.C.
§1395cc,  each  of  the  Hospitals  entered  into  a  written
agreement  with  the  Secretary  to  provide  hospital  ser-
vices  to  eligible  individuals.

14.  Under  42  U.S.C.  $13895x(v)(1)(A),  providers  of
inpatient  hospital  services,  such  as  the  Hospitals,  are
entitled  to payment  from  Medicare  for their  “reasonable
costs”  incurred  in  providing  services  to  Medicare  pa-
tients,  in  accordance  with  regulations  adopted  by  the
Secretary.  Reasonable  cost  is defined  as  the  “cost  actu-
ally incurred,  excluding  therefrom  any  part  of incurred
cost  found  to  be unnecessary  in the  efficient  delivery  of
needed  health  services,  and  shall  be  determined  in  ac-
cordance  with  regulations  establishing  the  method
or  methods  to  be  used,  and  the  items  to  be  included,  in
determining  such  costs  for  various  types  or  classes
of  institutions,  agencies,  and  services.”
42  U.S.C.
§$1395x(v)(1)(A).

15.  In adopting  regulations,  the statute  requires  the
Secretary  to  “take  into  account  both  direct  and  indirect
costs  of providers  or  services  ..
.  in order  that,  under
the methods  of determining  costs,  the  necessary  costs  of
efficiently  delivering  covered  services  to  individuals
covered  by [Medicare]  will  not  be  borne  by individuals
not  so  covered.”  Jd.  The  Secretary  has  elected  to  pub-
lish  many  rules  implementing  the  Medicare  program  in
various  manuals,  such  as  the  Provider  Reimbursement
Manual.

21

16.  Effective  with  cost  reporting  years  beginning  on
or  after  October  1, 1983,  Congress  adopted  a prospective
payment  system  (“PPS”)  to reimburse  hospitals,  such  as
the  Hospitals,  for inpatient  hospital  operating  costs.  See
42  U.S.C.  §1395wwid).

17.  Payment  to  providers  of  services  is  commonly
carried  out  by Medicare  fiscal  intermediaries,  acting  as
agents  of the  Secretary  pursuant  to  contracts  with  him.
An  intermediary  is assigned  to each  hospital  (including
each  of  the  Hospitals)  that  participates  in  Medicare.
Fiscal  intermediaries  make  periodic  payments  to  pro-
viders,  subject  to subsequent  adjustments  for  overpay-
ments  or  underpayments.  42  U.S.C.  §1395h.

18.  At  the  close  of its  FY,  a  hospital  must  submit  a
“cost  report”  showing  both  the  cost  incurred  by it dur-
ing the  F’'Y and  the  appropriate  portion  of those  costs  to
be  allocated  to,  and  paid  by,  Medicare.
42  C.ER.
§§ 413.24  and  413.50.  The  hospital’s  intermediary  is re-
quired  to  analyze  and  audit  the  cost  report  and  issue  a
Notice  of Program  Reimbursement  (“NPR”),  which  in-
forms  the  hospital  of the  final  determination  of its Medi-
care  reimbursement  for  the  cost  reporting  period.

19.

If a  hospital  is  dissatisfied  with  its  intermedi-
ary’s  final  determination  of total  Medicare  program  re-
imbursement  for a cost  reporting  period,  as  reflected  in
the  NPR,  it may  seek  to  have  the
NPR  corrected  or  al-
tered  in  one  of two  ways:

a.  The  hospital  has  a right to obtain  a hearing  before
the  Board  by filing an  appeal  with  the  Board  with-
in  180  days  of  receiving  its  NPR.  42  U.S.C.
$139500.

22

b.  The  hospital  may  request  reopening  of an  inter-
mediary’s  determination  within  three  years  of the
date  of notice  to the hospital  of the intermediary’s
determination.  42  C.F.R.  $405.1885(a).

20.  Regardless  whether  a  hospital  files  for  a  Board
hearing  or  seeks  reopening,  regulations  adopted  by the
Secretary  require  an  intermediary’s  determination  to be
reopened  within  three  years  of the  date  of notice  of it to
the  hospital  if CMS  notifies  the  intermediary  that  the
determination  is in any  way  “inconsistent  with  the appli-
cable  laws,  regulations,  or  general  instructions”  issued
by CMS.  42  C.F-R.  $4085. —

21.

In addition  to  having  the  authority  to  make  sub-
stantive  decisions  concerning  Medicare  reimbursement
appeals,  under  42 C.F.R.  §405.1873,  the  Board  is author-
ized  to  decide  questions  relating  to  its jurisdiction.  The
decision  of the  Board  on  substantive  or jurisdictional  is-
sues  constitutes  final  administrative  action  unless  the
Secretary  reverses,  affirms,  or  modifies  the  decision
within  60  days  of  the  hospital’s  notification  of  the
Board’s  decision.  42  U.S.C.  §139500(f)(1);  42  C.FR.
$$405.1875  and  405.1877.  The  Secretary  has  delegated
his  authority  under  the  statute  to  review  Board  deci-
sions  to  the  CMS  Administrator.

22.  A  hospital  may  obtain  judicial  review  of a final
administrative  decision,  including  a decision  relating  to
the  jurisdiction  of  the  Board,  by  filing  suit  within  60
days  of receipt  of the  decision  in the  United  States  Dis-
trict  Court  for  the judicial  district  in which  the  hospital
is located  or  in the  United  States  District  Court  for  the
District  of Columbia.  42  U.S.C.  §139500(f).

23

23.  The  Supreme  Court  has  held  that  a  decision
denying  a  provider’s  request  for  reopening  under  42
C.E.R.  §405.1885(a)  is  not  subject  to  judicial  review.
Your Home  Visiting  Nurse  Services,  Inc.  v.  Shalala,  525
U.S.  449  (1999).  However,  judicial  relief  is available  un-
der the  equitable  remedy  of mandamus  where a hospital
has  a  clear  right  to  the  relief  sought  and  the  Secretary
has  a defined  and  non-discretionary  duty  to  honor  that
right.  Monmouth  Med.  Ctr.  (“Monmouth”)  v.  Thomp-
son,  257  F.3d  807  (D.C.  Cir.  2001).

F.  PPS  and the DSH Adjustment

24.  Under  PPS,  Medicare  payments  for hospital  op-
erating  costs  are  not  based  directly  on  the costs  actually
incurred  by  the  hospitals.  Rather,  they  are  based  on
predetermined,  nationally  applicable  rates,  subject  to
certain  payment  adjustments.  42 U.S.C.  $1398ww(d)(1)-
(5);  42  C.F.R.  Part  412.  One  of  these  is  known  as  the
“disproportionate  share  hospital)”  or  “DSH”  payment.
See  42  U.S.C.  §1395ww(d)(5)(F).

25.  Hospitals  that  treat  a  disproportionately  large
number  of low-income  patients  are  entitled  by statute
to  a  payment,  known  as  the  DSH  adjustment,  in  addi-
tion
to  standard  Medicare  payments.  42  U.S.C.
§1395ww(d)(5)(F).  Congress  enacted  the  DSH  payment
in  recognition  of the  relatively  higher  costs  associated
with  providing  services  to  low-income  patients.  These
higher  costs  have  been  found  to result  from  the general-
ly poorer  health  of low-income  patients.  The  DSH  pay-
ment  provides  additional  Medicare  reimbursement  to
hospitals  for  the  increased  cost  of providing  services  to
low-income  Medicare  beneficiaries.

24

26.  There  are  two  methods  of determining  qualifica-
tion  for  a  DSH  adjustment:  the  “proxy  method”  (42
U.S.C.  §1395ww(d)(5)(F)(i)(1))  and  the  “Pickle  method”
(42 U.S.C.  §1395ww(d)(5)(F)G)(ID).  The  Hospitals’  enti-
tlement  to  a  DSH  adjustments  was  determined  using
the  proxy  method,  under  which  their  entitlement  to  a
DSH  adjustment,  as  well  as  the  amount  of the  DSH  ad-
justment,  is  based  on  the  Hospitals’  DSH  percentage.
42  U.S.C.  §1395ww(d)(5)(F)(v)  and  (vi).

27.  The  DSH  percentage  is the sum  of two  fractions,
which  are  designed  to capture  the number  of low-income
patients  a  hospital  serves  on  an  inpatient  basis.  The
first  fraction,  referred  to as  the  “Medicare  Proxy,”  is in-
tended  to account  for inpatients  who are  Medicare  bene-
ficiaries  entitled  to  supplementary  security  income
(“SSI”),  a federal  low-income  benefit  supplement.  The
second  fraction,  referred  to  as  the  “Medicaid  Proxy,”  is
intended  to  account  for  inpatients  who  are  not  entitled
to  Medicare  benefits,  but who  qualify  for medical  assis-
tance  under  a  State’s  Medicaid  State  plan.

28.  This  action  concerns  the  Medicare  Proxy,
U.S.C.
follows

is  defined

under

42

which
as
$1395ww(d)(5)(D)(vi)(D):

the fraction  (expressed  as  a percentage),  the numera-
tor  of which  is the  number  of such  hospital’s  patient
days  for such  period  which  were  made  up of patients
who  (for  such  days)  were  entitled  to  benefits  under
part  A  of this  subchapter  and  were  entitled  to  sup-
plementary  security  income  benefits  (excluding  any
State  supplementation)  under  subchapter  XVI  of this
chapter,  and  the  denominator  of which  is the  number
of such  hospital’s  patient  days  for  such  fiscal  year
which  were  made  up  of patients  who  (for  such  days)

25

were  entitled  to  benefits  under  part  A  of  this  sub-
chapter.

(Emphasis  added.)  The  Medicare  Proxy,  therefore,  is
the  percentage  of a  hospital’s  inpatients  who  received
Medicare  Part  A benefits  who  were  also  entitled  to  SSI
benefits  when  they were  receiving  inpatient  services  at
the  hospital.  This  is also  known  as  the  “SSI  percentage.”

29.  CMS  determines  the  SSI  percentage  for  hospi-
tals that qualify  for DSH  payment  but does  not  automat-
ically share  the  underlying  data with  the affected  hospi-
tals.  In  fact,  CMS  takes  the  position  that  some  of the
See  70  Fed.  Reg.
data  cannot  be  given  to  hospitals.
47,440  (August  12, 2005).  CMS  calculates  these  percent-
ages  using  federal  fiscal  year  (“FFY”)  (October  1
through  September  30)  data.

30.  Section  3-20.3  of the Medicare  Claims  Processing
Manual  (“MCPM”)  requires  Medicare  fiscal  intermedi-
aries  to  make  DSH  payment  determinations  using  the
SSI  percentage  supplied  to them  by CMS.  Accordingly,
DSH  hospitals,  such  as  the  Hospitals,  do  not  develop
their  own  SSI  percentages.  Further,  CMS  has  acknow]l-
edged  that  a  provider  is  not  required  to  claim  a DSH
payment  as  part  of the  formal  cost  reporting  process.
See 51  Fed.  Reg. 31457-58  (September  3, 1986).  Rather,
Medicare  fiscal  intermediaries  are  required  to make  the
proper  DSH  payment  determinations.  In  accordance
with  this  duty,  and  the  MCPM,  the  Hospitals’  Medicare
fiscal  intermediaries  made  the  DSH  payment  determi-
nations  at issue  in this  action  using the  SSI  percentages
furnished  to  them  by CMS.

26

G.  Calculation  of the  SSI  Percentage

31.  The  data  used  to  determine  the  SSI  percentage
was  addressed  in  significant  detail  in  the  decisions  is-
sued  by  the  Board  and  CMS  Administrator  in  Bay-
state  Hospital  v.  Mutual  of  Omaha  Insurance  Co.
(“Baystate”),  PRRB  Case  No.  2006-D20  (PRRB  Dec.
March  17,  2006),  modified  (CMS  Adm.  Dec.  May  11,
2006),  complaint  filed  July  14,  2006  sub  nom  Baystate
Medical  Center  v.  Leavitt  et al.,  06-cv-1263-JDB  (D.D.C.
filed  July  14,  2006).

32.  As  explained  in the  Baystate  administrative  de-
cisions,  CMS  retrospectively  calculates  the  SSI  percent-
age  using  data  from  the  June  updates  of the  Medicare
Provider  Analysis  and  Review  (“MEDPAR”)  file  follow-
FFY.  CMS  Adm.  Dee.  at  27.'
ing  the  end  of the  prior
CMS  determines  these  percentages  by  matching  its
MEDPAR  files  of all  Medicare  claims  paid  to  a hospital
during  the  period  with  the  file  maintained  by the  Social
Security  Administration  (“SSA”)  of all  individuals  enti-
tled  to  SSI  benefits  during  the  same  period.  /d.  at  16-
17.  The  resulting  “match”  of these  two  data  sets  yields  a
hospital’s  number  of patient  days for the  prior  F F'Y that
were  attributable  to hospital  patients  who  were  entitled
both  to  Medicare  Part  A  benefits  and  to  SSI  benefits
under  Title  XVI  of the  Social  Security  Act.  This  number
is divided  by the  total  number  of days attributable  to pa-
tients  who  were  entitled  to  Medicare  Part  A benefits,
and  the  final  result  is the  hospital’s  SSI  percentage.

’  References  in  this  Complaint  to  CMS  Administrator's  Decision
in  Baystate  are  set  torth  as  “CMS  Adm.  Dec.  at  XX,”  while  refer-
ences  to  the  Board’s  decision  in that  appeal  are  set  forth  as  “Board
Dec.  at  XX.”

27

33.  The  match  process  is based  on  two  data  sets—an
annual  data  tape  from  SSA  and  CMS’  MEDPAR  file.
Each  year  SSA  sends  CMS a  file  that  contains  the  fol-
lowing  information  for  each  SSI  recipient  on  the  tape:
(1) truncated  last name  and  first  initial,  (2) social  securi-
ty  number  (“SSN”)  or  railroad  retirement  program
identification  number,  (3)  date  of birth,  (4)  gender,  and
(5)  42  monthly  entries  using  the  “Title  II  number,”
which  indicates  the  payment  or  non-payment  of  SSI
benefits  during  the  period  covered  by the  file.  /d.  at  15-
16.  The  Title  II number  consists  of the  SSN  of the  per-
son  whose  work  history  qualified  for benefits  (the  “Title
II record  holder”),  and  an  alpha-numeric  suffix  that  re-
flects  the  relationship  between  the  beneficiary  and  the
Title  II record  holder.  Board  Dec.  at  13.  Because  of the
various  ways  of receiving  Title  II  benefits,  the  Title  II
number  is not  necessarily  unique  to  an  individual.  /d.

34.  The  MEDPAR  file  contains,  for  each  inpatient
stay  during  the  period  covered  by the  file,  (1) the  hospi-
tal’s  Medicare  provider  number,  (2) the  patient’s  health
insurance  claim  account  number  (“HICAN”),  (3)  the
dates  of admission  to  and  discharge  from  the  hospital,
(4)  the  total  inpatient  length  of stay,  (5)  the  number  of
days of the  stay covered  by Medicare  Part  A, and  (6) the
number  of days in the  stay  for which  the  patient was  de-
termined  to  be eligible  for SSI  benefits,  based  on  CMS’
match  of SSA  and  MEDPAR  data.  CMS  Adm.  Dec.  at
16.  CMS  generates  the  HICAN  by taking  the  SSN  or
railroad  retirement  number,  which  may  or  may  not  be
the  beneficiary’s  own  number,  from  a SSI  record  gener-
ated  by CMS  and  an  alpha-numeric  beneficiary  identifi-
cation  code.  Board  Dec.  at  15.

35.  CMS  accomplishes  the  matching  process  by at-
tempting  to  match  the  HICAN  contained  in  the

28

MEDPAR  file  to  the  Title  II  number  from  the  SSA  file.
Id.  Patient  days  that  are  matched  as  a  result  of this
process  are  included  in  the  numerator  of the  Medicare
Proxy  for  purposes  of  determining  a  hospital’s  DSH
payment.  In general,  the  more  days that  are  counted  in
the  numerator  of the  Medicare  proxy,  the  greater  the
hospital’s  DSH  payment.

H.  Flaws  in  the

SSI  Percentage

Identified

in  Bayst

Board  Decision
36.  The  Board’s  decision  in Baystate  included  some
background  and  identified  many  flaws  concerning  CMS’
calculation  of the  SSI  percentage,  and  CMS’  awareness
of these  flaws.

37.  CMS  requires  PPS  hospitals  to use  the  SSI  per-
centages  that  CMS  provides.  “42  C.F.R.  412.106(b)  pro-
vides  that  CMS  will  calculate  a hospital’s  Medicare  frac-
”  Board  Dec.  at  6.  “[T]he  DSH  statute  di-
tion.
”  /d.
rects  CMS  to  determine  the  SSI  fraction.
at  8.

.

.

.

.

.

.

38.  CMS  has  known  for many  years  that  its SSI  per-
centages  were  incorrect  for  many  FYs,  including  the
ones  at issue  in the  Baystate  appeal,  because  the agency
was  aware  that  (1)  the  process  it used  to  match  benefi-
ciary  specific  information  with  SSA  data  to  determine
the  SSI  percentage  was  flawed  and  (2) several  problems
existed  with  data  used  to determine  the SSI  percentage.

a.

“Both  parties  consistently  acknowledged  during the
hearing  that  the  Social  Security  number  is the  best
unique  identifier.  However,  despite  CMS’  stated  in-
tent  to  use  Social  Security  numbers  as  the  patient
match  criteria,  the  evidence  showed  that  CMS  has
never  matched  Social  Security  numbers  on  the  SSI

29

data  tape  with  Social  Security  numbers  on  the
MEDPAR  in the  calculation  of the  SSI  ratios.”  Jd.
at  12-13.

“(I ]ndividuals  who  received  a  forced  payment  dur-
ing their  inpatient  stays  were  not  shown  as  having
been  entitled  to SSI  for such  periods  on  SSA  annual
tapes.
Shafer  [a  CMS  employee}  testified  that
forced  payments  were  a common  occurrence  during
the  periods  at issue  here.  Thus,  the  omission  of al!
these  records  was  a systematic  and  recurring  error
that  had  the  effect  of understating  the  SSI  ratios.”
Id.  at 26.

CMS  should  have  known  that  the  omission  of retro-
active  SSI  awards  was  a  problem.  “CMS’  1996
summary  report  showed  that  the  ratio  of the  num-
ber  of stays  with  new  SSI  days  added  .
.  tothe
number  of stays  with  old  SSI  days  deleted  .
was  greater  than  17:1  for most  years.”  Theoretical-
ly, and  as  CMS  claimed,  the  two  categories  could
have  offset  each  other  resulting  in  awash.  Yet, with
the ratio  at  17:1,  the  stays  with  new  SSI  days added
were  not  offset  by any  countervailing  situation.  /d.
at  28.

.

CMS  knew  that  stale  records  were  a  problem  with
SSI  fractions.  Yet,  in a June  1995  proposed  rule  to
change  the  process  of recalculation  of the  SSI  ratio
based  on  a  hospital’s  cost  reporting  period,  CMS
claimed  that  the result  of recalculating  the ratio was
“invariably”  lower  SSI  ratios  due  to problems  with

quested  corrected  SSI  records  from  SSA  because
its
MEDPAR  records  were  contaminated.  CMS  cre-
ated  a set  of “special”  MEDPAR  files  to  allow  CMS

to  determine  the  number  of hospital  stays  in which

“The  uncontroverted  evidence  shows  that  the  CMS
