KATHLEEN  SEBELIUS,  SECRETARY  OF  HEALTH  AND
HUMAN  SERVICES,  PETITIONER

AUBURN  REGIONAL  MEDICAL  CENTER,  ET  AL.

Vv.

ON  WRIT  OF CERTIORARI
TO  THE  UNITED  STATES  COURT  OF APPEALS
FOR  THE  DISTRICT  OF COLUMBIA  CIRCUIT

REPLY  BRIEF  FOR  THE  PETITIONER

deadline

Since  the  creation  of  the  Provider  Reimbursement
Review  Board,  the Secretary  of Health  and  Human  Ser-
vices  has  prohibited  the  Board  from  extending  the  180-
in  42  U.S.C.
appeal
day  administrative
139500(a)(3),  except  as  provided  by  regulation.
For
nearly  40 years,  the applicable  regulation  has  permitted
an  extension  caly  for  good  cause  shown  and  only  if the
request  is filed  within  three  years  of the  intermediary’s
Notice  of Program  Reimbursement  (NPR).  42  C.F.R.
405.1841(b);  42 C.F.R.  405.1836  (2011).'  And  for  the
same  40  years,  the  Secretary  has  offered  providers  an
opportunity  to correct  errors  in the  NPR  of the  sort  re-
spondents  allege  here  (and  recover  underpayments)
through  a  motion  to  reopen  that  likewise  must  be filed

'  Unless  otherwise  indicated,  al] references  to Title 42 of the C.F.R.

are  to the 2007  version.

(1)

2

within  three  years.
42  C.F.R.  405.1885(a);  42  C.F.R.
405.1885(b)  (2011).  Under  well-settled  principles  of ad-
ministrative  law,  the  Court  should  sustain  this  estab-
lished  statutory  and  regulatory  framework  and  reject
respondents’  proposed  open-ended  equitable  tolling  re-
gime.  Such  a regime  would  undo  the  repose  those  time
limits  afford  and  fundamentally  alter  the administrative
appeals  process  that  has  governed  Medicare  provider
payments  from  the  beginning  and  thereby  maintained
order  and  finality  in an  exceedingly  complex  regulatory
scheme.

Respondents  contend  that the  Medicare  Act itself re-
quires  equitable  tolling  and,  thus,  adherence  to  the
framework  established  by the Secretary  is not warrant-
ed.  But the statutory  text  does  not even  mention  tolling;
rather,  respondents  rely  solely  on  a  presumption  in fa-
vor  of  equitable  tolling  that  has  no  application  here.
Without  that thumb  on  the scale,  the Secretary’s  consid-
ered  judgment  and  reasonable  regulations  are  control-
ling.  And  even  with  a  presumption,  the  Secretary’s
judgment  and  reasonable  regulations  remain  sound:  eq-
uitable  tolling  has  no  place  in the complex  and  technical
world  of Medicare  provider  payments.  In  the  end,  re-
spondents  fail  to  establish  that  the  Secretary’s  regula-
tions  are  arbitrary,  capricious,  or  contrary  to  Section
139500(a)(3)  of the  Medicare  Act.

ARGUMENT

A.  Respondents  Mischaracterize  The  Facts  Of  This  Case

And  The  Secretary’s  Regulatory  Regime

Respondents  tell  a story of agency  “misfeasance”  in-
volving  the “knowing  concealment  of known  errors”  that
has  deprived  them  of money  “promised”  by Congress.
Br.  3, 18,  20, 31,  32, 33, 34,  55,  56,  58.  In  respondents’
view,  such  agency  “misconduct”  is protected  by a regu-

3

latory  regime  that  they say  operates  as  a “one-way  toll-
ing rule,”  allowing  “perpetual  reopening  when  a provid-
er  is at fault,  but not when  the Secretary  is at fault.”  Br.
18,  19, 38, 39 n.13,  48, 50, 51, 55, 56, 58.  Notwithstanding
the  number  of times  respondents  retell  that  story,  it is
wrong.

1.  Respondents’  charges  of “misfeasance”  arise  from
administrative  and  judicial  proceedings  brought  by
Baystate  Medical  Center  (Baystate).  Baystate  (unlike
respondents  here)  timely  appealed  its disproportionate
share  hospital  (DSH)  adjustment  determinations.  The
Board  ultimately  concluded  that the information  used  by
the Centers  for  Medicare  & Medicaid  Services  (CMS)  to
calculate  the SSI  fraction  was  not  “the  best available  da-
ta”  and  that  there  were  flaws  in the  data-matching  pro-
cess.  Baystate  Med.  Ctr.,  PRRB  Dec.  No.  2006-D20,
2006  WL  752453,  at  *33-*34  (Mar.  17,  2006);  see
Baystate  Med.  Ctr.  v.  Leavitt,  545  F. Supp.  2d 20, 26-31,
amended  in  part,  587  F.Supp.  2d  37  (D.D.C.  2008)
(Baystate).  The  Administrator  reversed  in  relevant
part,  concluding  that  the  Board  was  wrong  to  require
CMS  to  achieve  “an  errorless  match”  in computing  the
SSI  fraction  and  that,  even  using the  provider’s  sample,
only  “approximately  1.59  percent  of  the  individuals
failed  to  match”  and  thus  the  provider  failed  to demon-
strate  that  its reimbursement  was  adversely  affected  by

*  CMS  calculates  the SSI  fraction  because  it “[rJecogniz[ed]  the dif-
ficulty  hospitals  would  have  identifying  their  Medicare  patients  who
are  also  SSI  recipients.”  51  Fed.  Reg.  31,459  (1986).  To  “remove[]
this  burden  from  hospitals,”  CMS  decided  to “undertake[]  the  task
(and expense)  of determining  for each  hospital  the number  of patient
days  of those  dually entitled  to Medicare  Part  A and  SSI.”  /bid.;  cf.
60 Fed.  Reg.  45,812  (1995)  (“Because  the  SSI  data  used  in the calcu-
lation  are  protected  by the Privacy  Act, we  cannot  provide  an  oppor-
tunity  for hospitals  to verify  these  data.”).

4

the asserted  errors.  Baystate,  2006  WL  16846339,  at *26,
*35  (May  11, 2006).  The  district  court  in turn  reversed
in part,  holding  that  CMS  had  not  used  “the  best  availa-
ble  data”  to  calculate  the  SSI  fraction.  Baystate,  545
F.  Supp.  2d  at  40-50.  Specifically,  the  court  concluded
that  CMS  should  have  but  did  not  use  the  most  up-to-
date  SSA  data  in its possession,  id.  at 42-43,  and  that  the
data  used  omitted  certain  types  of  records  that  SSA
could  have  included,  zd.  at 44-46.

Contrary  to respondents’  allegations,  no  administra-
tive  or  judicial  tribunal  has  ever  found  that  CMS  “af-
firmative[ly]  conceal[ed]  known  errors.”  Br.  19.  Indeed,
the district  court  and the  Board  both  found  precisely  the
opposite.  The  district  judge  in this  case,  who  was  also
the  district judge  in Baystate,  stated  unequivocally  that
“nothing  in  either  {the  Board’s  or  the  district  court’s]
Baystate  decisions  reflects  a finding  that  CMS  ‘deliber-
ately concealed’  the  data  flaws  at issue  or  otherwise  en-
gaged  in  fraud.”  Pet.  App.  44a.  The  court  explained
that,  in  Baystate,  the  Board  simply  “rejected  the  con-
tention  that  CMS  had  used  the  ‘best  available  data,’”
and  the  Baystate  court  reached  the  same  conclusion.
Ibid.  The  Board  has similarly  made  clear  that  “(t]he  de-
cisions  in Baystate  did  not  result  in a finding  of fraud.”
Medical  Coll.  of Ga.  Hosp.,  PRRB  Dec.  No.  2010-D30,
2010  WL  4214223,  at  *4 (May  25, 2010),  aff’d,  2010  WL
5570990  (CMS  Adm’r  July  18, 2010).  The  administrative
and  judicial  findings  that  CMS  failed  to  use  the  best
available  data  to calculate  the SSI  fraction  bear  little  re-
semblance  to the  “knowing|]  and  systematic[]”  effort  to
underpay  providers  that  respondents  seek  to  portray.
Br.  3.°

°  After  the  district  court’s  decision  in Baystate,  the  Secretary  re-
vised  the matching  process  for all Medicare  providers  through  notice-

5

Respondents’  related  suggestion  that  CMS  is able  to
“conceal  its  errors  until  the  clock  runs  out”  (Br.  58)  ig-
nores  a critical  fact:  respondents  were  in the same  posi-
tion  as  the hospital  in Baystate.  In 1999,  Baystate  time-
ly challenged  the  intermediary’s  calculation  of its  DSH
adjustments  and  “mounted  diligent  efforts”  to  obtain
records  from  CMS  and  SSA.  Baystate,  545  F.  Supp.  2d
at  51;  A.R.  at  8767-8768,  Baystate,  supra  (No.  1:06-cv-
1263)  (11/19/99  Letter  from  Baystate  to  PRRB).  The
Baystate  proceedings  were  paid for by a healthcare  con-
sulting  firm  that  represents  hundreds  of  other  DSH
hospitals  and  that  negotiated  a  35%  contingency  fee  to
prosecute  Baystate.  Southwest  Consulting  Assocs.  Ami-
cus  Br.  1-3,  19  (Southwest  Amicus  Br.);  Baystate,  545
F.  Supp.  2d at 34n.22.  Beginning  in 1993,  the consulting
firm  began  looking  into  what  it believed  were  errors  in
CMS’s  calculation  of  the  SSI  fraction  and  ultimately
“advise[d]  Baystate  to question  the  agency’s  SSI  calcu-
lations.”  Southwest  Amicus  Br.  9-10.  Although  that
same  consulting  firm  now  suggests  that  “(t]he  Board’s
2006  decision  in Baystate  really was  the  earliest  clue  to
the  regulated  community  that they might  have  been  un-
derpaid  as  a  result  of flaws  in CMS’s  calculation  of the
SSI  fraction,”  zd.  at 20,  that  firm  and  its  clients  neces-
sarily  had  a “clue”  many  years  prior.*  And  Baystate  was

and-comment  rulemaking,  75 Fed.  Reg.  50,275-50,286  (2010), and the
Administrator  directed  that the revised  matching  process  be applied
to  every  “properly  pending  DSH  appeal  of the  SSI  fraction  data
matching  process  issue,”  HHS,  CMS  Ruling  1498-R,  at  6, Apr.  28,
2010,  http:/Awww’.cms.gov/regulations-and-guidance/guidance/rulings/
downloads/CMS1498R. pdf.

*  Many of the purported  “facts”  set forth  by Southwest  represent  a
disputed  interpretation  of the record  in Baystate  and  not the findings
by any administrative  or judicial  tribunal.  See Gov’t  Mot.  for Summ.
J. at 29-44,  Baystate,  supra  (No.  1:06-cv-1263)  (explaining  the alleged

6

not  the  first  hospital  to  challenge  the  SSI  fraction.  In
1993,  another  hospital  timely  appealed  seeking  recalcu-
lation  of its  DSH  adjustments  based  on  its  own  inde-
pendent  calculation  of the  SSI  fraction.  See Loma  Lin-
da Cmty.  Hosp.  v.  Shalala,  907  F. Supp.  1399,  1400-1402
(C.D.  Cal.  1995).

Respondents,  in contrast,  allowed  the  statutory  and
regulatory  deadlines  to  expire,  and  sat  on  the  sidelines
during  seven  years  of  administrative  proceedings  in
Baystate.  Only  when  that  test  case  proved  successful
did  respondents  file  piggyback  appeals,  more  than  a
decade  late.°  The  ease  with  which  respondents  (and
nearly  200  other  hospitals,  Gov’t  Br.  App.  34a-37a)  now
assert  “fraud”  and  proclaim  ignorance  only  further
demonstrates  why an  equitable-tolling  regime  previous-
ly unknown  to  the  Medicare  provider  appeals  process
would  prove  highly  disruptive  and  burdensome.  See
United  States  v.  Brockamp,  519  U.S.  347,  352  (1997);

data errors  and CMS’s  response  and refuting “Baystate’s  contentions
that agency  employees  repeatedly  lied about the DSH  calculations”).
°  The hospital  provider community was well aware  of issues  regard-
ing the SSI  calculation  throughout  the pendency of the Baystate  pro-
See,  e.g.,  Saint  Mary’s  Mercy  Med.  Ctr.,  PRRB  Dec.
ceedings.
No.  2007-D63,  2007  WL  3341598,  at *2 (Aug.  24, 2007)  (in 2000,  pro-
vider  “undertook  a review”  of its SSI  fraction  “after  questions  arose
in the hospita]  reimbursement  community”);  St.  Joseph Mercy Hosp.,
2010 WL  5571062,  at *3-*4  (CMS  Adm’r  Oct.  6, 2010)  (provider  chal-
lenged  SSI  calculation  no  later  than  March  2001  and  appeal  was
placed  in  abeyance  pending  Baystate);
John  R.  Jacob,  Hospitals
Want  Bigger Piece  of Medicare  Pie,  Nat’!  L.J.  (Aug.  30, 2004)  (“hos-
pitals  have  long  believed  that  (SSI)  percentages  were  improperly

7

High Country  Home  Health,  Inc.  v.  Thompson,  359  F.3d
1307,  1311  (10th  Cir.  2004).°

2.  Respondents  repeatedly  describe  the  regulatory
regime,  first  adopted  in  1974,  as  a “one-way  ratchet”  or
a “one-way  tolling  rule.”  Br.  34, 50, 51,55.  Contrary  to
respondents’  contention,  however,  the limited  opportuni-
ties  for additional  administrative  review  after  the  expi-
ration  of 180  days  are  largely  neutral.

The  good-cause  regulation,  allowing  limited  exten-
sions  of the appeal  deadline,  inures  only to the  benefit  of
providers.  The  reopening  regulation,  for  its  part,  is
largely  neutral.  42 C.F.R.  405.1885(a)  (allowing  reopen-
ing “on  motion”  of the intermediary,  the  Board,  the  Sec-
retary,  or  the provider).  Reopening  may  remedy  under-
payments  or  result  in  the  recovery  of  overpayments.
Your  Home  Visiting  Nurse  Servs.,  Inc.  v.  Shalala,  525
U.S.  449,  455-456  (1999)  (Your  Home).  And  while  pro-
viders  cannot  seek  discretionary  reopening  after  three
years,  neither  can  the  intermediary,  the  Board,  or  the
Secretary.  42 C.F.R.  405.1885(a)  (“No  such  determina-
tion  or  decision  may  be reopened  after  such  3-year  peri-
od”);  42 C.F.R.  405.1885(b)  (requiring  reopening  in cer-
tain  circumstances  if “within  the 3-year  period”).  Thus,
an  intermediary  determination  or  Board  decision  could
not be reopened  under  the  regulations  after  three  years
if the Secretary  discovered  that  errors  in calculating  the
SSI  fraction  had  overstated  hospitals’  DSH  payments.
The  opportunity  for  a  hospital  to  seek  an  extension  for
taking  an  appeal  for  good  cause,  or  to  seek  reopening,
within  three  years  is a  manifestation  of the  Secretary’s

*  Approximately  80%  of the  hospitals  paid under  the  Prospective
Payment  System  (PPS)  receive  DSH  payments.  See  77  Fed.  Reg.
53,721-53,724  (2012)  (3423  PPS  hospitals,  including  1903  urban  and
820  rural  DSH  hospitals).

8

“grace,”  Your  Home,  525  U.S.  at  454,  not  a  one-way
ratchet—particularly  not  one  that  favors  the  Secretary.
The  only  example  of the  purported  inequity  that  re-
spondents  identify  is  42  C.F.R.  405.1885(d),  which  re-
quires  reopening  “at  any  time  if it  is  established  that
[the] determination  or  decision  was  procured  by fraud  or
similar  fault  of any  party  to  the  determination  or  deci-
sion.”  The  so-called  “fraud”  exception  to  the  absolute
three-year  deadline  for reopening  is limited  in scope  and
rarely  used.  CMS  has  taken  the  position  that  the  term
“established”  requires  an  independent  administrative  or
judicial  finding  of fraud  or  similar  fault  separate  from
any  reopening,  such  as  in a  False  Claims  Act case.  See,
e.g.,  Gov’t  Mot.  to  Dismiss  or  Summ.  J.  at  22,  Bradley
Mem 'l Hosp.  v.  Thompson,  1:04-cv-416  Docket  entry
(D.D.C.  July 30, 2004).  A regulatory  regime  that  allows
the  agency  to  recover  overpayments  in  those  circum-
stances  based  on  fraud  should  not  “shock[]”  this  Court,
which  was  willing to countenance  “a system  in which  un-
derpayments  could  never  be  the  basis  for  reopening.”
Your  Home,  525  U.S.  at 455.’

3.  Respondents’  insistence  that  somehow  Congress
promised  them  the  payments  they seek  in this  litigation,
no  matter  how  long after  the fact,  is equally  unfounded.
E.g.,  Br.  57-58.  When  a  provider  chooses  to participate
in  the  Medicare  program,  its  payments  depend  on  the
Medicare  Act and  the Secretary’s  implementing  regula-
tions,  which  require  providers  to comply  with  specified
procedures  in order  to receive  payment.  A provider  that

"  Contrary to respondents’  contention  (Br.  10, 50), the fraud  excep-
tion  applies  equally  to a fiscal  intermediary  if it “procured”  a Board
decision  by  “fraud  or  similar  fault.”  A  fiscal  intermediary  is  a
“part[y]”  to the  Board  hearing,  42 C.F.R.  405.1843(a),  even  though  it
is not  a  “part[y]”  to its  own  determination,  42 C.F.R.  405.1805.

9

fails  to  comply  with  those  procedures  is not  entitled  to
payment  any  more  than  a  taxpayer  who  fails  to  seek  a
refund  within  a  specified  period  of time.  Respondents’
characterization  of the  payments  allegedly  due  as  “fully
funded”  and  “set aside”  by Congress,  id.  at 57, when  the
suit  is based  on  cost  reports  that  have  been  closed  for  a
decade  or  more,  blinks  reality.  Congress  did  not  prom-
ise or  set  aside  money  to pay  Medicare  providers  in 2012
for services  rendered  in 1987  in a case  not appealed  until
2006.

B.  Respondents’  Arguments  For  Ignoring  Well-Established
Principles  of Administrative  Law  Are  Unpersuasive
Respondents  do not dispute  that, through  notice-and-
comment  rulemaking  contemporaneous  with  the  enact-
ment  of Section  139500(a)(3)  nearly  40  years  ago,  the
Secretary  determined  that the  Board  has no  authority  to
hear  appeals  filed  beyond  the  180-day  deadline,  except
as  provided  by regulation—.e.,  for good cause  shown  if
the  request  is filed  within  three  years  of the
NPR.  Re-
spondents  also  do  not  dispute  that  an  equitable-tolling
regime  would  conflict  with  the  limitations  in the  Secre-
tary’s  regulations.  Respondents  nevertheless  contend
that  settled  principles  of administrative  law  precluding
courts  from  imposing  extra-statutory  procedural  re-
quirements  on  an  administrative  tribunal,  see  Vermont
Yankee  Nuclear  Power  Corp.  v.  NRDC,  435  U.S.  519
(1978)  (Vermont  Yankee),  and  from  setting  aside  a duly
promulgated  regulation  that is neither  arbitrary  nor  ca-
pricious,  should  not apply here.  Respondents  fail to con-
front  the  force  of this  Court’s  precedents.

1.  The  government’s  opening  brief  argues  at length
(Br.  17-29)  that  Vermont  Yankee  prohibits  courts  from
unilaterally  imposing  on  agencies  additional  procedural
rules  not  required  by statute,  and  that  the  equitable-

10

tolling  regime  adopted  by  the  court  of  appeals  here
would  do precisely  that.  Respondents  devote  three  par-
agraphs  to arguing  that  Vermont  Yankee  is “inapt.”  Br.
54-55.  Contrary  to  respondents’  suggestion,  Vermont
Yankee  is not  limited  to  an  agency’s  rulemaking  proce-
dures,  nor  to  an  analysis  of the  procedures  imposed  by
the  Administrative  Procedure  Act  (APA).  See  Pension
Benefit  Guar.  Corp.  v.  LTV Corp.,  496  U.S.  633,  653-656
(1990)  (applying  Vermont  Yankee  to informal  agency  ad-
judication  where  no  provision  in  governing  statute  or
APA  afforded  asserted  procedural  rights).  Rather,  as
this  Court  explained  in Vermont  Yankee,  “[e]ven  apart
from  the  [APA]  this  Court  has  for  mure  than  four  dec-
ades  emphasized  that the formulation  of procedures  was
basically  to be left within  the  discretion  of the  agencies
to  which  Congress  had  confided  the  responsibility  for
substantive  judgments.”  435  U.S.  at  524;  see  FCC  v.
Pottsville  Broad.  Co.,  309  U.S.  134,  1438  (1940).

2.  Respondents  make  no  attempt  to  argue  that  the
Secretary’s  good-cause  regulation  for  an  extension  of
the appeal  deadline,  including  the three-year  outer  limit,
is arbitrary  or  capricious.  Gov’t  Br.  29-31.  Neither  do
respondents  dispute  that the good-cause  regulation  fal!s
squarely  within  the  Secretary’s  broad  rulemaking  au-
thority.  Jd.  at 18.  Respondents  do contend  (Br.  54) that
Congress  would  not  have  delegated  authority  to  the
Secretary  to avoid  performing  administrative  review  re-
quired  by Congress,  but  the  substance  of respondents’
If they  are  suggesting  that  the
objection  is  not  clear.
Secretary  has  no  authority  to  interpret  the  Board’s  ad-
judicatory jurisdiction,  the Court  held  otherwise  in Your
Home.  Gov’t  Br.  50-51;  zd.  at  49-50  (citing  cases).  If
they  are  merely  suggesting  that  the  Secretary  has  no
authority  to reject open-ended  equitable  tolling because

1]

See  Part  C,  infra.

in their  view  Congress  mandated  it, their  nondelegation
argument  collapses  into a question  of statutory  interpre-
tation.
On  its  face,  Section
139500(a)(3)  provides  for  Board  review  only “if,” among
other  things,  the  provider  files  a hearing  request  within
180  days.  42  U.S.C.  139500(a)(3)  (emphasis  added).  If
there  were  any  conflict  between  the  good-cause  regula-
tion  and  the  plain  language  of the  statute,  it would  be
with  the  Secretary’s  allowance  of any  extension  of the
180-day  period,  not  her  failure  to  adopt  an  amorphous
equitable-tolling  regime  that  has  had  no  place  in  the
Medicare  provider  payment  program  for the entire  his-
tory  of its  existence.  See  Court-Appointed  Amicus  Br.
41-47.

C.  Congress  Did  Not  Mandate  Open-Ended  Equitable  Toll-

ing Of The  180-Day  Appeal  Period

Respondents  argue  that  Chevron  deference  is  not
warranted  because  Section  139500(a)(3)  is not  ambigu-
ous.  Br.  51-52.  That  argument  rests  not  on  the statuto-
ry text,  which  says  nothing about  equitable  tolling,  or  on
generally  applicable  tools  of  statutory  interpretation.
Instead,  respondents  rely exclusively  on  the  presump-
tion  in favor  of equitable  tolling  that  this  Court  adopted
in Irwin  v.  Department  of Veterans  Affairs,  498  U.S.  89,
95-96  (1990),  for  a  statute  of  limitations  applicable  to
suits  filed  in  court.  See  Br.  52  (“[A]pplication  of  the
presumption  eliminates  any  ambiguity  for the agency  to
interpret.”).  No  such  presumption  applies  here.  But
even  if a  presumption  applied  in some  form,  the  Secre-
tary’s  implementation  of Section  1395vo(a)(3)  is sound
and  respondents  cannot  prevail.

1.  a.  This  Court  has  never  applied  the  /rwin  pre-
sumption  to impose  equitable  tolling  on  internal  agency
review  procedures.  Gov’t  Br.  32-36.  Neither  case  cited

12

by respondents  (Br.  44) applied  a presumption  and  each
is inapposite  for other  reasons.  The government’s  open-
ing  brief  (Br.  33-34)  explains  in  detail  why  Zipes  v.
Trans  World Airlines,  Inc.,  455  U.S.  385 (1982),  is readi-
ly distinguishable.  Union  Pacific  R.R.  v.  Sheehan,  439
U.S.  89, 92 (1978)  (per curiam),  is even  further  removed
because  the  Court  did  nothing  more  than  explain  that,
as  a  matter  of fact,  the  National  Railroad  Adjustment
Board  had considered  the railroad  employee’s  claim  that
the  applicable  time  deadline  (in a  collective-bargaining
agreement)  should  be  equitably  tolled.

Respondents  contend  that  it  would  “make[]  little
sense”  to  forgo  a  “statutory  analysis”  where  “the  time
period  at issue”  is “for  administrative,  rather  than judi-
cial,  review.”  Br.  42.  That  misapprehends  the  govern-
ment’s  argument.  If Congress  intends  a statutory  time
limit  to  be  subject  to  equitable  tolling,  that  intent  of
course  controls.  Before  its decision  in Jrwin,  the  Court
used  traditional  tools  of statutory  interpretation  to  de-
termine  whether  equitable  tolling  of a  suit-filing  time
limit  implicating  the  Federal  Treasury  would  be  “con-
sistent  with  Congress’  intent,”  Bowen  v.  City  of N.Y.,
476  U.S.  467, 480  (1986).  Gov’t  Br.  40-41.  In the  admin-
istrative  context,  traditional  tools  of statutory  construc-
tion  entail  application  of the rules  governing judicial  re-
view  of  an  agency  action  or  interpretation,  which  are
drawn  from  the  APA,  Chevron,  and  Vermont  Yankee.
Where  Congress  has  expressly  granted  authority  to an
agency  to  promulgate  rules  and  procedures  to  govern
the  administrative  appeals  process,  it  makes  “little
sense”  (Resp.  Br.  42) to presume  that  Congress  intend-
ed the  Judiciary  to adopt  freestanding  equitable  excep-
tions  to  statutory  and  regulatory  deadlines  and  other
Far
prerequisites  to  internal  administrative  review.

13

from  “a realistic  assessment  of legislative  intent,”  zd.  at
42  (quoting  /rwin,  498  U.S.  at 95),  incantation  of a pre-
sumption  would  redistribute  the  authority  granted  by
Congress  from  the  expert  agency  to  the  courts.  See
Pottsville  Broad.,  309  U.S.  at  144  (cautioning  against
reading  “the  laws  of  Congress  through  the  distorting
lenses  of inapplicable  legal  doctrine”);  id.  at  141  (“[T]Jo
the  extent  that  a federal  court  is authorized  to review  an
administrative  act,  there  is superimposed  upon  the  en-
forcement  of  legislative  policy  through  administrative
control  a  different  process  from  that  out  of which  the
administrative  action  under  review  ensued.”).

For these  reasons,  respondents’  assertion  that “equi-
table  tolling  by an  agency  adjudicator”  is “the  rule,  not
the  exception,”  Br.  43,  proves  very  little.  There  are
many  instances  in  which  the  administering  agency
agrees  that equitable  tolling should  be available,  or  that
some  other  exception  to  finality  should  be  allowed,  at
least  in certain  circumstances.  Indeed,  here,  the  Secre-
tary  has  done  so  by adopting  the  regulations  allowing
reopening  and  extension  of the  appeal  deadline,  subject
to the three-year  limitation.  There  may  also  be instanc-
es  in which  the administering  agency  has declined  to al-
low equitable  tolling,  but a contrary  congressional  intent
is clear.  A presumption  im favor  of equitable  tolling  is
not  needed  to identify  those  cases.°

*  The courts  of appeals  cases  cited by respondents  (Br.  43 n.16)  are
distinguishable.  Jackson  v.  Astrue,  506  F.3d  1349,  13852-1353  (11th
Cir.  2007),  did  not  involve  an  administrative  deadline,  but rather  the
same judicial  review  deadline  at issue  in City of New  York.  In several!
other  cases,  the  administering  agency  agreed  that  equitable  tolling
was  available.  Hill v.  Merit  Sys.  Prot.  Bd.,  No.  2012-3024,  2012  WL
1663002,  at *3 (Fed.  Cir.  May  14, 2012)  (discussing  equitable  tolling
of administrative  complaint deadline  which  govermment  conceded  was
available  in prior  case,  see  Kirkendall  v.  Department  of Army,  479

b.  To  the  extent  respondents  argue  that  this  Court
can  apply  /rwin’s  presumption  without  identifying  a
“precise  private  equivalent,”  Resp.  Br.  45-46,  the  gov-
ernment  does  not  disagree,  Gov’t  Br.  38.  But  respond-
ents’  reliance  on  Holland  v.  Florida,  130  S.  Ct.  2549,
2560-2562  (2010),  to suggest  that  they  need  not  identify
any  private  analogue  is misplaced.  As the  government
explains  in its  opening  brief  (Br.  39  & n.18),  Holland,  a
state  habeas  corpus  case,  did  not  require  the  Court  to
identify  any  private  analogue  because  habeas  (quite  un-
like  statutory  payments  under  Medicare)  has  historical-

Holland  was  not  a  suit  against  the  United  States,

immunity  principles  that  govern  when  a  party  seeks
money  from  the  Federal  Treasury.  Respondents  also
cite  City  of New  York,  476  U.S.  at  480,  and  Honda  v.
Clark,  386  U.S.  484,  500-501  (1967),  but  Honda  did  not

F.3d 830, 837 (Fed.  Cir.) (en banc),  cert.  denied,  552  U.S.  948  (2007));
Ajalla  v.  White,  81  Fed.  Appx.  715,  719  (10th  Cir.  2003)  (29 C.F.R.
1614.604(c)  expressly  provides  for  equitable  tolling  of  regulatory
deadline);  Von  Eye v.  United  States,  92 F.3d 681, 684 (8th Cir.  1996);
White  v.  Bentsen,  31  F.3d  474,  475  (7th  Cir.  1994),  cert.  denied,  514

moval  proceedings  do not involve  sophisticated  institutional  litigants.

15

involve  payments  from  the  general  Treasury,  and  both
decisions  predated  Jrwin  and  therefore  looked  to  con-
gressional  intent  without  applying  any  presumption  in
favor  of equitable  tolling.

c.  Ifa presumption  in favor  of equitable  tolling were
to  apply  at  all  in this  context,  it would  be  substantially
weakened  because  equitable  principles  have  not  tradi-
tionally  governed  Medicare  provider  payments,  and  be-
cause  the  180-day  administrative  appeal  period  was  en-
acted  18  years  before  this  Court’s  decision  in  /rwin.
Gov’t  Br.  39-40.  Respondents’  answer  is to describe  the
Board’s  powers  as  “flexible.”  Br. 46.  But the only “flex-
ibility”  respondents  point  to  is  expressly  set  forth  by
statute;  the  Board  does  not  have  any  inherent  equitable
powers  outside  those  prescribed  by the  Medicare  Act
and the Secretary’s  implementing  regulations.  Gov't  Br.
20-22.

2.  Even  if the  /rwin  presumption  had  some  applica-
tion,  there  are  “good  reason(s]  to believe  that  Congress
did  not  want  the  equitable  tolling  doctrine  to  apply|.]”
Brockamp,  519  U.S.  at 350.

a.  For  nearly  40 years,  the  Secretary  has  prohibited
the  Board  from  extending  the  180-day  deadline,  except
as  provided  by regulation.  Until  the  decision  below,  no
court  had ever  superimposed  equitable-tolling  principles
onto  that statutory  and  regulatory  scheme.  Against that
backdrop,  Congress  amended  Section  139500  on  six sep-
arate  occasions  and  never  expressed  any  disapproval  of
the  Secretary’s  interpretation.  Gov’t  Br.  20 & n.8.  “It is
well  established  that  when  Congress  revisits  a  statute
giving  rise  to a longstanding  administrative  interpreta-
tion without  pertinent  change,  the ‘congressional  failure
to revise  or  repeal  the agency’s  interpretation  is persua-
sive  evidence  that the interpretation  is the one  intended

16

by Congress.’”  CFTC  v.  Schor,  478  U.S.  833,  845-846
(1986)  (citation  omitted).

the

has

treated

Secretary

Respondents  do  not  dispute  any  of this  history.  In-
deed,  they rely on  it to argue  that  the  180-day  adminis-
trative  appeal  deadline  is not jurisdictional.  Resp.  Br.
17-18,  34.  According  to  respondents,  “for  nearly  four
decades,
Section
139500(a)(3)’s  time  limit  as  nonjurisdictional”  and  “Con-
gress’s  longstanding  acceptance  of  (a)(3)’s  nonjuris-
dictional  status  merits  *  *  *  respect.”  Br.  34.’  It is
true  that  the  Secretary  has  not  treated  Section
139500(a)(3)’s  time  limit  itself  as  “jurisdictional”  in the
sense  that she could  not allow  for any extension  by regu-
lation.  But  from  the  outset  the  Secretary  has  imposed
strict  limitations  on  such  extensions.  Congress’s  long-
standing  acceptance  of the Secretary’s  treatment  of Sec-
tion  1389500(a)(3)  thus  necessarily  includes  acceptance  of
those  strict  limitations,  which  are  incompatible  with  the
equitable  tolling  respondents  seek.

*  Respondents  contend  that  “for  nearly  forty  years,  most  courts
and  the  Executive  Branch’s  published  regulations  have  treated”  the
180-day  limit  “as  nonjurisdictional  and  subject  to  equitable  excep-
tions.”  Br. 23 n.6.  That is incorrect.  First,  two  of the three  courts  of
appeals  to consider  the issue  concluded  that the time limit is jurisdic-
tional  in the strictest  sense  and  that  even  the Secretary’s  regulation
allowing  extensions  for good cause  is invalid.  Compare  St.  Joseph's
Hosp.  v.  Heckler,  786  F.2d  848, 851-853  (8th Cir.  1986),  and Alacare
Home  Health  Servs.,  Inc.  v.  Sullivan,  891  F.2d  850,  856  (11th  Cir.
1990),  with  Western  Med.  Enters.  v.  Heckler,  783  F.2d  1376,  1379-
1380  (9th  Cir.  1986).  Second,  during  that  40-year  time  frame,  no
court  ever  recognized  an  “equitable”  exception  to the  statutory  and
regulatory  appeal deadline.  Third,  the Secretary’s  “published  regula-
tions”  likewise  never  recognized  any “equitable”  exceptions.  See pp
19-20,  infra.

17

b.  Nothing  about  the  60-day  deadline  set  forth  in 42
U.S.C.  139500(f)(1)  for seeking judicial  review  suggests
otherwise.  Resp.  Br.  41-42.  Even  assuming  that  time
limit  is subject  to  equitable  tolling,’®  it does  not  follow
that  the  administrative  time  limit  for seeking  Board  re-
view  must  also  be subject  to tolling that  the  Secretary’s
regulations  do  not  allow.  A provider  is entitled  to judi-
cial  review  only if it has satisfied  the statutory  precondi-
tions,  which  include  securing  a  “final  decision  of  the
Board.”  42  U.S.C.  1389500(f)(1).  As  relevant  here,  a
provider  may  obtain  a  hearing  before  the  Board  (and,
thus,  a final  decision)  only if “such  provider”  is dissatis-
fied with  the intermediary’s  payment  determination;  the
amount  in  controversy  is  $10,000  or  more;  and  the  re-
quest is filed  within  180  days.  42 U.S.C.  139500(a).  Pur-
suant  to  that  statutory  scheme,  if the  provider  is  not
“dissatisfied,”  or  if the  amount  in  controversy  is  less
than  $10,000,  the  provider  is not  entitled  to  any judicial
review  of the  intermediary’s  determination.  The  same  is
true  of  a  provider  who  fails  to  file  a  hearing  request
within  the  period  specified  by Section  189500(a)  and  the
Secretary’s  regulations.

There  is another,  more  practical  reason  to distinguish
the  judicial  review  deadline  in  Section  139500(f)(1)
from  the  administrative  appeal  deadline  in  Section

'°  The  Secretary  has argued  that the 60-day judicial  review  period
in  Section  139500(f)(1)  is not  subject  to  equitable  exceptions.  The
courts  have  reached  different  conclusions.  Compare  Lloyd  Noland
Hosp.  & Clinic  v.  Heckler,  762  F.2d  1561,  1564-1565  (11th  Cir.  1985)
(subject  to waiver  and  equitable  tolling),  and  Bedford  Cnty.  Mem'l
Hosp.  v.  Heckler,  583  F. Supp.  367, 370-371  (W.D.  Va.  1984)  (subject
to waiver),  aff’d,  769  F.2d  1017  (4th  Cir.  1985),  with  Wilmac  Corp.  v.
Thompson,  No.  03-5936,  2007  WL 2599238,  at *2-*3  (E.D.  Pa. Sept.  4,
2007)  (not subject to equitable  tolling),  and Kidney  Ctr.  v.  Shalala,  63
F.  Supp.  2d 51, 55-57  (D.D.C.  1999)  (not  subject  to waiver).

18

139500(a)(3).  The  universe  of reasons  for invoking  equi-
table  tolling  of the  60-day judicial  review  deadline  would
be  quite  limited.  Once  a  provider  has  “notice”  that  a
final  administrative  decision  has  issued,  42  U.S.C.
139500(f)(1),  it would  be exceedingly  difficult  to assert  a
justification  for  missing  the  filing  deadline,  especially
more  than  a  few  days  or  weeks.  The  limited  case  law
touching  on  equitable  tolling of the  60-day  deadline,  see
note  10,  supra,  bears  that  out.  By contrast,  as  amply
demonstrated  by this  case  and  the  other  related  cases
pending  in  district  court,  Gov’t  Br.  App.  34a-37a,  the
same  cannot  be  said  of the  180-day  administrative  ap-
peal  deadline.  Superimposing  an  open-ended  equitable
tolling  regime  onto  the carefully  structured  administra-
tive  review  process  would  deprive  closed  payment  de-
terminations  from  many  years  earlier  of the finality they
have  long enjoyed,  and would  place substantial  adminis-
trative  burdens  on  the  agency,  its  contractors,  and  the
Medicare  Trust  Fund,  Gov’t  Br.  23-28,  in a way  that  eq-
uitable  tolling of the judicial  review  deadline  would  (and
has)  not.  Even  if Congress  intended  the  latter,  it would
not  have  envisioned  (let  alone  mandated)  the  former.
Simply  put, the  program  could  not  function  with  any  de-
gree  of efficiency  without  the order  and  finality achieved
through  the  statutory  and  regulatory  regime  that  has
long  governed  the  administrative  review  process.

c.  Respondents’  attempts  to minimize  the importance
of administrative  finality  and  the  practical  effects  of an
equitable-tolling  regime  are  unavailing.

Respondents  first  contend  (Br.  47-48)  that  the  Board
is perfectly  capable  of engaging  in a fact-intensive  equi-
table  tolling  inquiry  and,  indeed,  has  been  “regularly”
engaging  in “equitable  inquiries.”  Respondents  conflate
the  Secretary’s  focused  good-cause  regulation  with  the

19

open-ended  equitable  tolling  regime  they  urge.''  The
good-cause  regulation  is substantially  more  limited  than
judicially  imposed  equitable  tolling.  Most  significantly,
like  the  reopening  regulation,  it has  a mandatory  three-
year  outer  limit.  Respondents  barely  acknowledge  that
critical  distinction.  There  is  a  world  of  difference  be-
tween  the  possibility  that  a  past  cost  year  may  be revis-
ited  within  three  years  of the intermediary’s  determina-
tion,  but  no  later,  and  the  possibility  that  a  past  cost
year  may  be revisited  at any  time.  Moreover,  while  eq-
uitable  tolling  is  itself  a  narrow  doctrine,  it  is  still
broader  than  the  Secretary’s  limited  good-cause  excep-
tion  to  the  180-day  appeal  deadline—particularly  after
the  2008  regulatory  amendments.  73  Fed.  Reg.  30,206
(2008);  42 C.F.R.  405.1836(b)  (2011)  (limiting  good cause
to “extraordinary  circumstances  beyond  [the hospital’s]
control  (such  as  a  natural  or  other  catastrophe,  fire,  or

'  Only four  of the cases  respondents  cite  discuss  equitable  tolling.
Two  involved  the same  Baystate  issue  presented  here.  In those  cas-
es,  the  Board,  after  holding  that  equitable  toiling  is unavailable  as  a
matter  of law, briefly  explained  that,  even  if tolling were  available,  it
would  not  be warranted  based  on  the  “undisputed  facts.”  Medical
Coll.  of Ga.  Hosp.,  2010  WL  4214223,  at  *3;  SKJ  1987-1994  DSH
SSI%  Group,  PRRB  Dec.  No. 2009-D12,  2009  WL  981317,  at *4 (Mar.
5, 2009).  The  other  two  cases  did  not  involve  the  180-day  appeal
deadline  at issue  here,  and  any  analysis  of equitable  principles  was
cursory  at best.  See  Bradford  Reg’l  Med.  Ctr.,  PRRB  Dec.  No.  99-
D19,  1999  WL  10149,  at *8 (Jan.  7, 1999)  (90-day  period  for  submit-
ting  supporting  documentation  relating  to  capital-related  costs),
rev'd,  1999 WL 398034  (HCFA Adm’r  Mar.  12, 1999),  rev’d, Bradford
Hosp.  v.  Shalala,  136  F. Supp. 2d 428 (W.D.  Pa. 2001);
New Hanover
Reg'l Med.  Ctr.  & Psychiatric  Unit,  PRRB  Dec.  No.  2003-D37,  2003
WL  21804275,  at  *6  (July  17, 2003)  (180-day  period  for  requesting
adjustment  to rate-of-increase  ceiling).  The equitable-tolling  inquiry
respondents  advocate  would  in fact  be a drastic  departure  from  the
Board’s  “regular[]”  practice  that  has  endured  for  nearly  40 years.

20

strike)”).  Accordingly,  application  of the  good-cause  ex-
ception  does  not  require  the agency  to devote  significant
resources  to  adjudicating  untimely  appeals.  Gov't  Br.
24.

Respondents  also contend  that  no  “floodgates”  would
open  because  equitable-tolling  claims  would  be  limited
to  “the  agency’s  own  misfeasance,”  which  is  “entirely
within  the  Secretary’s  own  control.”  Br.  55-56.  That
equitable-tolling  relief should  rarely  be granted,  howev-
er,  does  not  mean  that  the  doctrine  would  rarely  be  in-
voked.  Recent  history  shows  that what  constitutes  “mis-
feasance”  is in the  eye  of the  beholder.  Gov't  Br.  25-27.
As this  Court  recognized  in Brockamp,  the  mere  availa-
bility of equitable  tolling  “could  create  serious  adminis-
trative  problems  by forcing  the  [agency]  to  respond  to,
and  perhaps  litigate,  large  numbers  of late  claims,  ac-
companied  by requests  for  ‘equitable  tolling’  which,  up-
on  close  inspection,  might  turn  out  to lack  sufficient  eq-
uitable  justification.”  519  U.S.  at 352;  see
High Coun-
try,  359  F.3d  at  1311  (“[F  Jor  every  plaintiff  whose  sub-
stantive  claim  or  reason  for default  leads  an  adjudicator
to excuse  the  default,  ten  less  sympathetic  plaintiffs  are
likely  to  demand  similar  treatment.”).

The  Medicare  Act’s  “complex  and  highly  technical
regulatory  program”  heightens  the  need  for  finality.
Thomas  Jefferson  Univ.  v.  Shalala,  512  U.S.  504,  512
(1994)  (citation  omitted);  see  Methodist  Hosp.  v.  Shala-
la, 38  F.3d  1225,  1235  (D.C.  Cir.  1994)  (“policy  of retro-
active  corrections  would  erode  the  predictability  and  fi-
nality that underlie  the  [Medicare  Prospective  Payment
System]  scheme”).  This  Court  recognized  in Your
Home
that  “the  statutory  purpose  of imposing  a  180-day  limit
on  the  right to seek  Board  review  of NPRs  *  *  *  would
be frustrated  by permitting  requests  to reopen  to be re-

21

viewed  indefinitely.”  525  U.S.  at  454.  Engrafting  an
open-ended  equitable  tolling  regime  onto  Section
139500(a)(3)  would  have  an  even  more  dramatic  effect,
because  it would  not  be  cabined  by a  three-year  outer
limit.  Contrary  to  respondents’  assertion  (Br.  39  n.13),
the  need  for  order  and  finality  under  Medicare  is espe-
cially  critical  in light  of the  backlog  of cases  before  the
Board.  Gov’t  Br.  30-31.  “(T]he  Board  is burdened  by an
immense  caseload,”  and  “procedural  rules  requiring
timely  filings  are  indispensible  devices  for  keeping  the
machinery  of the  reimbursement  appeals  process  run-
ning smoothly.”  High  Country,  359  F.3d  at  1310.”

D.  The  Board  Has  No  Authority  To  Adjudicate  Appeals
Filed  Outside  The  Statutory  And  Regulatory  Deadlines
Respondents  contend  (Br.  34)  that  the  government
agrees  that  Section  139500(a)(3)  is  not  jurisdictional.
What  the government  explains  in its opening  brief,  how-
ever,  is that  although  Section  139500(a)(3)  does  limit the
Board’s  jurisdiction,  it does  not  preclude  the  Secretary
from  adopting  a narrow  and  time-limited  good cause  ex-
ception  to  the  180-day  filing  deadline,  in the  exercise  of
her  broad  authority  under  the  Medicare  Act.  Section

Respondents  compare  the  size  of the  Medicare  program  to that
of Social  Security,  Br.  49 n.19,  but  there  are  at least  two  critical  dis-
tinctions.  First,  the disability  benefits  scheme  at issue  in City of New
York  was  “designed  to  be  ‘unusually  protective’  of claimants,”  476
U.S.  at 480, whereas  the Medicare  payment  scheme  at issue  here  in-
volves  “sophisticated”  institutional  providers,  Your  Home,  525  U.S.
at 456.  Second,  a determination  of individual]  entitlement  to disability
benefits  does  not have the breadth  and technica)  complexity  involved
in a determination  of Medicare  provider  payments.  In that  respect,
Medicare  provider  payments  are  more  analogous  to the subject mat-
ter  in Brockamp  (tax collection)  than  to the subject  matter  in City of
New  York  (Social  Security  disability  benefits).

22

139500(a)(3)  and  the  regulations  implementing  it then
together  establish  mandatory  time  limits  on  the  Board’s
adjudicative  authority.  The  court  of  appeals  erred  in
overriding  those  mandatory  limits  by imposing  an  un-
precedented  open-ended  equitable  tolling regime  on  the
Medicare  provider  appeals  process  that  Congress  enact-
ed  40 years  ago.

*_

*&*

*&*

&

&

For  the  foregoing  reasons  and  those  stated  in  our
opening  brief,  the  judgment  of  the  court  of  appeals
should  be  reversed.

Respectfully  submitted.

DONALD  B. VERRILLI,  JR.

Solicitor  General

IN THE
Supreme  Court  of the Gnited  States

No.  11-1231

KATHLEEN  SEBELIUS,
SECRETARY  OF  HEALTH  AND  HUMAN  SERVICES,

Petitioner,

Vv.

AUBURN  REGIONAL  MEDICAL  CENTER,  ET AL.,

Respondents.

On  Writ  of Certiorari
to  the  United  States  Court  of Appeals
for  the  District  of Columbia  Circuit

REPLY  BRIEF  FOR  COURT-APPOINTED
AMICUS  CURIAE  SUPPORTING  REVERSAL

The  explicit,  precisely  drawn,  and  unqualified
timely  filing  requirements  prescribed  by  42  U.S.C.
§ 139500(a)(3)  clearly  constitute  a  jurisdictional  limi-
tation  on  the  authority  of  the  Provider  Reimburse-
ment  Review  Board  (“PRRB”  or  “Board”)  to  hear
Medicare  provider  appeals.  This  conclusion  rests  on
two  basic  premises  derived  from  the  Medicare  Act
itself:  First,  § 139500(a)  uniquely  defines  the  Board’s
authority  to  hear  cases  (viz.  its jurisdiction);  no  other
provision  of  the  Medicare  Act  plausibly  serves  that
purpose.  Second,  Congress  granted  the  Secretary  of
Health  and  Human  Services  (“the  Secretary”)  express
administrative  discretion  to  extend  filing  deadlines

2

for  beneficiary  appeals  under  the  Medicare  Act  but
omitted  any  such  discretion  for  the  deadlines  govern-
ing Medicare  provider  appeals.

Respondents  squarely  deny  the  first  proposition,!
urging  this  Court  (1)  to  focus  on  whether  Congress
used  a  jurisdictional  “label,”  (2)  to  hold  that  a  provi-
sion  cannot  be  jurisdictional  if it  is  framed  in  terms
of the  litigant’s  right  to  invoke  the  tribunal’s  author-
ity,  and  (3)  to  adopt  a  per  se  test  treating  nearly  all
filing  deadlines  as  claims-processing  rules.  See  Resp.
Br.  21-25.  But  these  results  find  no  support  in  this
Court’s  precedents.
Respondents—here,  joined  by
petitioner—also  reject  the  second  proposition;  they
deny  that  Congress’s  contrasting  approach  to  benefi-
ciary  and  provider  appeals  has  any  bearing  on  legis-
lative  intent.
See  Pet.  Br.  48-49;  Resp.  Br.  29  n.9.
Their  position  contradicts  this  Court’s  well-settled
approach  to  interpreting  statutes,  in  general,  and
the  Medicare  Act,  in  particular.  These  errors  cause
respondents  to  take  a  one-dimensional  view  of  this
with
Court’s  precedents,  equating  § 139500(a)(3)
timely  filing  requirements  that  appear  in  remedial
statutes  lacking  one  or  both  of subsection  (a)’s  salient
characteristics.  See  Resp.  Br.  22-24.

Respondents  and  petitioner  also  offer  a  hodge-
podge  of  arguments  built  on  supposed  legislative
acquiescence  in  the  Secretary’s  regulatory  exception
to  § 139500(a)(3)  (see  Pet.  Br.  43;  Resp.  Br.  34),  on
speculation  about  the  general  purposes  of the  Medi-

1  Petitioner  has  a  more  complicated  position;  she  believes
that  § 139600(a)  and  her  implementing  regulations  together
impose  a  jurisdictional  constraint  on  the  Board’s  authority.
See  Pet.  Br.  47.  Petitioner's  position.  however.  contradicts  this
Court’s  understanding  of the  nature  of jurisdictional  constraints.
See  infra  pp.  17-18.

3

care  Act  (see  Resp.  Br.  30-34),  and  on  the  Secretary’s
asserted  discretion  to  prescribe  her  own  procedural
rules,  even  when  doing  so  deviates  from  subsection
(a)(3)’s  filing  deadline  (see  Pet.  Br.  47-48).  These  con-
tentions  are  wrong,  but  even  if they  were  right  they
could  not  displace  the  clear  jurisdictional  import  of
§ 139500(a)(3)’s  text  and  structure.

that  subsection

(a)’s  other
“dissatisfaction”

Finally,  respondents  seek  to  buttress  their  reading
of  subsection  (a)(3)’s  timely  filing  requirement  by
threshold
asserting
requirements—namely,
and
the
amount-in-controversy  provisions—are  themselves
Respondents’  position  finds  no
nonjurisdictional.
support  in  any  decision  of the  Board,  the  Centers  for
Medicare  & Medicaid  Services  (“CMS”),  or  the  Secre-
tary.  More  importantly,  to  the  extent  that  respond-
ents  suggest  that  Congress  gave  the  Board  author-
ity  to  hear  cases  that  do  not  satisfy  the  carefully
detailed  threshold  requirements  for  a  Board  hearing,
thelr  position  represents  an  unlikely  reconstruction
of congressional  intent.

l.a.  The  text  of  § 139500(a)  clearly  defines  the
Board’s  power  to  hear  Medicare  provider  appeals,
specifying  the  appropriate  subject  matter  for  a  Board
hearing,  the  requisite  amount  in controversy,  and  the
time  within  which  a  provider  appeal  must  be  filed.
See  42  U.S.C.  § 139500(a)(1)-(3);  see  also  Amicus
Br.  18-24.
Although  respondents  are  correct  that
Congress  did  not  affix  a  “jurisdictional”  label  to
§ 139500(a),2  this  Court  has  made  clear  that  Con-

2 The  opening  brief  for Court-Appointed  Amicus  relied  on  the
caption  “Establishment”  as  evidence  that  Congress  understood
subsection  (a)  as  a  jurisdictional  provision.  See  Amicus  Br.  11,
19,  28.  Respondents  correctly  note  that  the  caption  “Establish-
ment”  originated  in  the  codifiers’  revisions  to  §139500(a),  rather

4

gress  need  not  “use  magic  words  in  order  to  speak
clearly”  in  establishing  jurisdictional  requirements.
Henderson  v.  Shinseki,  131  S.  Ct.  1197,  1203  (2011).
Rather,  what  matters  is  that  subsection  (a)  alone
“delineatfes]  the  classes  of cases”  that  the  Board  has
authority  to  adjudicate—the  defining  characteristic
of  a  jurisdictional]  provision.  Kontrick  v.  Ryan,  540
U.S.  443,  456  (2004).  In  fact,  if § 139500(a)  does  not
define  the  Board’s  jurisdiction  to  adjudicate  Medicare
provider  appeals,  then  no  provision  of  the  Medicare
Act  does.

Respondents  nevertheless  contend  (at  23)  that  the
timely  filing  requirement  must  be  nonjurisdictional
because  Congress  worded  it in  terms  of the provider’s
right  to  a  hearing,  rather  than  in  terms  of  the
Board’s  authority  as  such.  But  respondents’  conclu-
sion  does  not  follow  from  their  premise.  The  right
to  invoke  a  tribunal’s  authority  to  hear  a  case  is
the  mirror  image  of the  tribunal’s  power  to  hear  the
case.  Accordingly,  in  several  recent  cases,  the  Court
has  found  procedural  conditions  on  a  party’s  right  to
See,  e.g.,  Bowles  v.  Russell,
sue  to  be  jurisdictional.
551  U.S.  205,  213  (2007)  (28 U.S.C.  § 2107);  Miller-El
v.  Cockrell,  537  U.S.  322,  336  (2003)  (28  U.S.C.
§ 2253(c)(1));  FEC  v.  NRA  Political  Victory  Fund,  513

than  in the  enactment  itself.  See  Resp.  Br.  24-25  & n.7.  At  the
same  time,  however,  respondents  err  in further  suggesting  that
Congress  must  label  a  provision  “jurisdictional”  in order  to  sat-
isfy  the  Court’s  clear-statement  rule.  See,  e.g.,  id.  at 24  (stress-
ing  that  “Congress  nowhere  labeled  the  timeframe  as  jurisdic-
tional”).  As  this  Court  has  made  clear.  the  appropriate  inquiry
uses  all  of the  relevant  tools  of statutory  interpretation—text,
context,  structure,  and  purpose.
Here.
those  tools  lead  to  the  conclusion  that  Congress  intended  sub-
section  (a) to be jurisdictional.

See  Amicus  Br.  16.

5

U.S.  88,  90  (1994)  (28  U.S.C.  § 2101(c)).5  Congress’s
choice  to  phrase  a  provision  in  terms  of  the  one
rather  than  the  other  thus  does  not  alter  the  reality
that  § 139500(a)  uniquely  specifies  the  scope  of  the
Board’s  adjudicative  authority.

Respondents  further  suggest  that  this  Court  has
treated  time  limits  as  categorically  nonjurisdictional
except  in  those  areas  in  which  this  Court’s  prece-
dents  establish  “a  long  tradition”  of  treating  such
See  Resp.  Br.  22-23
requirements  as  jurisdictional.
&  n.6  (discussing  John  R.  Sand  &  Gravel  Co.  uv.
United  States,  552  U.S.  130,  139  (2008);  Bowles,  551
If  that  suggestion  were  true,  this
U.S.  at  210-13).
Court  in Henderson,  131  S.  Ct.  at  12038,  could  simply
have  stopped  its  inquiry  once  it  concluded  that  the
time  limit  at  issue  there—as  in  this  case,  a  deadline
for  appealing  from  one  non-Article  III  tribunal  to
another—fell  outside  any  long  tradition  establishing
its  jurisdictional  status.  But  the  Court  did  not  stop
there.  Instead,  before  determining  whether  the  time

3  Respondents  cite  (at  22)  Reed  Elsevier,  Inc.  v.  Muchnick.
130  S.  Ct.  1237  (2010).  for  the  proposition  that  filing  deadlines
define  the  rights  of the  parties  rather  than  the  power  of the  tri-
bunal  That  characterization  does  not,  however,  fairly  describe
the  Court’s  opinion,  which  relied  on  a  variety  of contextual  fac-
tors  to  determine  that  the  procedural  requirement  at issue—the
obligation  to  register  a  copyright  with  the  Copyright  Office  be-
fore  filing  suit—was  nonjurisdictional.  The  Court  emphasized
that  the  registration  requirement  was  “located  in  a  provision
‘separate’  from  those  granting  federal  courts  subject-matter
Noting
jurisdiction  over  [copyright]  claims.”
that  the  registration  requirement  was  subject  to  several  impor-
tant  statutory  exceptions,  the  Court  added  that  it would  be  “at
least  unusual  to ascribe  jurisdictional  significance  to  a condition
subject  to  these  sorts  of  exceptions.”  Jd.  at  1246.  Reed  Else-
uier’s  contextual  analysis  contradicts  rather  than  supports  the
categorical  approach  that  respondents  press  here.

6

limit  was  or  was  not  jurisdictional,  the  Court  found
it  necessary  to  examine  carefully  the  text,  context,
structure,  and  purpose  of  the  veterans’  statute  at
issue  in the  case.  See  id.  at  1205-06.  In  other  words,
this  Court  uses  ail  the  traditional  tools  of  statutory
interpretation  to  determine  whether  Congress  ex-
pressed  a  clear  intent  to  treat  a  timely  filing  require-
ment—or  any  other  requirement—as  jurisdictional.

b.

Reading  § 139500(a)(3)  in  light  of the  structure
of  the  Medicare  Act  as  a  whole  reveals  a  clear
congressional  intention  to  adopt  a  strict  and  unquali-
fied  filing  requirement  for  Medicare  providers.
See
Amicus  Br.  31-40.  In the  1972  legislation  that  estab-
lished  the  PRRB  review  scheme  for  provider  appeals,
Congress  incorporated  by reference  the  Social  Secur-
ity  Act’s  procedures  for  administrative  appeals  and
Judicial  review  of beneficiary  claims.  Compare  Social
Security  Amendments  of  1972,  Pub.  L.  No.  92-603,
§ 243(a),  86  Stat.  1329,  1420  (provider  appeals),  with
id.  § 2990,  86  Stat.  1464-65  (beneficiary  appeals).
Of  central  importance  here,  while  the  provisions
governing  Medicare  providers  adopted  unqualified
deadlines  for  such  providers  to  seek  administrative
and  judicial  review  (see  42  U.S.C.  § 139500(a)(3),  (f)
(Supp.  II  1973)),  the  provisions  incorporated  by  ref-
erence  for  Medicare  beneficiaries  gave  the  Secre-
tary  explicit  administrative  discretion  to  extend  the
analogous  deadlines  for  such  beneficiaries  to  seek
administrative  and judicial  review  (see  id.  § 1395ff(b)
(Supp.  II  1978)  (incorporating  by reference  42  U.S.C.
§ 405(b)  and  (g) (1970))).

Petitioner  suggests  that  the  contrasting  approach-
es  of the  two  sets  of provisions  tell  us  little  because
(1)  the  1972  amendments  merely  reenacted  benefi-
ciary  provisions  that  had  originated  in  the  Medicare

7

Act  of  1965,  and  (2)  the  administrative  discretion
prescribed  for  Medicare  beneficiary  appeals  was  in-
corporated  by reference  from  provisions  of the  Social
Security  Act  that  had  been  enacted  many  years
before  any  of  the  Medicare  legislation.  See  Pet.  Br.
48-49.  Even  if Congress  had  adopted  the  two  sets  of
contrasting  provisions  at  different  times  in  different
pieces  of legislation,  it would  still  be  appropriate  for
this  Court  to  read  § 139500(a)(3)  “to  contain  that
permissible  meaning  which  fits  most  logically  and
comfortably  into  the  body  of both  previously  and  sub-
sequently  enacted  law.”  West  Virginia  Univ.  Hosps.,
Inc.  v.  Casey,  499  U.S.  83,  100  (1991).*  In  any  event,
neither  of  petitioner’s  contentions  alters  the  reality
that,  in  a  single  piece  of legislation,  Congress  plainly
adopted  one  strategy  for  beneficiary  appeals  (explicit
administrative  discretion)  and  another  for  provider
appeals  (no such  discretion).

For  their  part,  respondents  merely  argue  (at  29
n.9)  that  “the  nonjurisdictional  character  of  (a)(3)  is
reinforced,  not  metamorphosized,  by the  existence  of
another  nonjurisdictional  provision  ...  that  ...  spe-
cifically  permits  regulatory  exceptions  to  a  statutory
deadline  for  Medicare  beneficiary  claims.”  But  this
Court  has  made  clear  that,  “‘when  the  legislature
uses  certain  language  in  one  part  of the  statute  and
different  language  in  another,  the  court  assumes
different  meanings  were  intended.’”  Sosa  v.  Alvarez-
Machain,  542  U.S.  692,  711  n.9  (2004)  (quoting
2A  NORMAN  J.  SINGER,  STATUTES  AND  STATUTORY

4  This  Court  employs  such  an  approach  “not  because  that
precise  accommodative  meaning  is  what  the  lawmakers  must
have  had  in  mind  ...,  but  because  it  is  [the  judiciary's]  role
to  make  sense  rather  than  nonsense  out  of  the  corpus  juris.”
Casey,  499  U.S.  at  100-01.

8

CONSTRUCTION  § 46:06,  at  194  (6th  ed.  2000)).  More
specifically,  this  Court  has  repeatedly  viewed  the
disparate  inclusion  and  exclusion  of  particular  lan-
guage  within  the  Medicare  Act  as  strong  evidence  of
congressional  intent.  See,  e.g.,  Bowen  v.  Georgetown
Univ.  Hosp.,  488  U.S.  204,  213-14  (1988)  (“[W]here
Congress  intended  to  grant  the  Secretary  the  author-
ity  to  act  retroactively,  it  made  that  intent  explicit.
...  § 1395x(v)(1)(A)(Qi)  directs  the  Secretary  to  estab-
lish  procedures  for  making  retroactive  corrective
adjustments;  in view  of this  indication  that  Congress
considered  the  need  for  retroactive  agency  action,  the
absence  of  any  express  authorization  for  retroactive
cost-limit  rules  weighs  heavily  against  the  Secre-
tary’s  position.”);  United  States  v.  Erika,  Inc.,  456
U.S.  201,  208  (1982)  (“Conspicuously,  [§ 1395ff]  fails
to  authorize  further  review  for  determinations  of the
amount  of Part  B awards.  In  the  context  of the  stat-
ute’s  precisely  drawn  provisions,  this  omission  pro-
vides  persuasive  evidence  that  Congress  deliberately
intended  to  foreclose  further  review  of such  claiuns.”).
Accordingly,  by  giving  the  Secretary  express  discre-
tion  to  extend  the  filing  deadline  for Medicare  benefi-
ciliary  appeals  while  omitting  like  authority  from  the
provisions  setting  nearly  identical  deadlines  for  Med-
icare  provider  appeals,  Congress  signaled  its  inten-
tion  to  withhold  such  discretion  in  the  latter  context.
See  Russello  v.  United  States,  464  U.S.  16,  23  (1983)
(“[W]here  Congress  includes  particular  language  in
one  section  of  a  statute  but  omits  it  in  another  sec-
tion  of  the  same  Act,  it  is  generally  presumed  that
Congress  acts  intentionally  and  purposely  in the  dis-
parate  inclusion  or  exclusion.”)  (internal  quotation
marks  omitted).  This  Court  should  not  construe  two
sets  of  provisions  worded  so  differently  to  mean  the
same  thing.

9

c.

Respondents  rely  (at  22)  on  a  series  of  cases
involving  what  they  deem  to  be  “similar  filing  dead-
lines.”  But  respondents  can  characterize  these  other
deadlines  as  “similar”  to  § 139500(a)  only  by  trans-
forming  this  Court’s  context-sensitive  inquiry  into
a  one-dimensional  per  se  rule.
In  fact,  none  of  this
Court’s  cases  has  found  time  limits  to  be  non-
jurisdictional  when  they  have  shared  the  two  salient
characteristics  that  mark  § 139500(a)(3).

First,

in  Henderson  v.  Shinseki—upon  which
respondents  place  central  reliance  (at  23-24)—this
Court  emphasized  that  the  timely  filing  requirement
for  appeals  from  the  Board  of  Veterans’  Appeals
(“BVA”)  to  the  U.S.  Court  of  Appeals  for  Veterans
Claims  (“Veterans  Court”)  appears  “in  a  subchapter
fof  the  Veterans’  Judicial  Review  Act,  Pub.  L.
No.  100-687,  Div.  A,  102  Stat.  4105  (1988),]  entitled
‘Procedure,’”  and  not  in  the  subchapter  defining  the
tribunal’s  jurisdiction.  131  S.  Ct.  at  1205.5  A similar
consideration  stood  out  in  two  other  cases  upon
which  respondents  rely  (at  22),  Kontrick  v.  Ryan,
supra,  and  Zipes  v.  Trans  World  Airlines,  Inc.,  455
U.S.  385  (1982).  Kontrick  emphasized  that  the  timely
objection  requirement  there  at  issue  appeared  not  in
any  statute  defining  the  bankruptcy  courts’  juris-
diction,  but  rather  in  a  Federal  Rule  of  Bankruptcy
Procedure  issued  pursuant  to  28  U.S.C.  § 2075.  See
540  U.S.  at  453-54.  Zipes  underscored  that  the  timely
filing  requirement  relevant  there  was  contained  in
“an  entirely  separate  provision”  from  those  defining

5 The  veterans’  statute,  moreover,  explicitly  defines  the  Vet-
erans  Court’s  jurisdiction  in  a  distinct  provision.  See  38  U.S.C.
§ 7252(a)  (“The  Court  of Appeals  for  Veterans  Claims  shall  have
exclusive  jurisdiction  to  review  decisions  of the  Board  of Veter-
ans’  Appeals.”).

10

the  court’s  jurisdiction.  455  U.S.  at  393-94.  In  none
of  these  cases,  then,  did  this  Court’s  holding  entail
the  implausible  notion  that  the  statute  in  question
nowhere  defined  the  tribunal’s  jurisdiction—which  is
precisely  what  respondents’  position  would  require
this  Court  to  conclude  here,  given  that  § 139500(a)
constitutes  the  one  and  only  provision  that  defines
the  classes  of cases  that  the  Board  may  hear.

contrast  between

statute  confirmed

The  related  provision—which
nonjurisdictional.
prescribes  the  method  of taking  an  appeal  from  the

the  deadlines  for  noticing  an  appeal  from  a  district
court  to  a  court  of appeals.  See  38  U.S.C.  § 7292(a).
Because  this  Court  had  long  construed  the  deadlines

6  This  Court  added  that  the  legislative  history  to  Title  VII
of  the  Civil  Rights  Act  of  1964  suggested  that  key  legislators
understood  the  timely  filing  requirement  as  a  statute  of limita-

il

Buttressing  its  structural  comparison,  the  Hender-
son  Court  found  the  “most  telling”  evidence  of legisla-
tive  intent  to  be  the  fact  that  the  veterans’  review
scheme  as  a  whole  reflected  procedural  informality,
flexibility,  and  solicitude  for  veterans.
Jd.  at  1205-
06;  see  also  id.  at  1206  (concluding  that  reading  the
filing  deadline  for  the  Veterans  Court  as  jurisdic-
tional  would  “clash  sharply  with
[the  overall]
scheme”).  Similarly,  in  two  other  cases  on  which  re-
spondents  rely,  the  Court  found  that  the  overarching
remedial  frameworks  at  issue  called  for  special  solici-
tude  for  plaintiffs’  procedural  rights.  See  Burnett  v.
New  York  Central  R.R.  Co.,  380  U.S.  424,  427  (1965)
(arguing  that  equitable  tolling  served  the  “humane
and  remedial”  purposes  of  the  Federal  Employers’
Liability  Act);  Honda  v.  Clark,  386  U.S.  484,  495-98
(1967)  (stressing  that  Congress  consciously  modeled
the  Trading  with  the  Enemy  Act’s  remedial  scheme
after  bankruptcy  statutes  that  had  long  incorporated
equitable  tolling).
In  contrast,  the  Medicare  Act
draws  sharp  lines  between  its  flexible  treatment  of
beneficiary  deadlines  and  its  stringent  requirements
for  generally  more  sophisticated  Medicare  providers.
Cf.  Your  Home  Visiting  Nurse  Servs.,  Inc.  v.  Shalala,
525  U.S.  449,  456  (1999)  (discussing  the  sophistica-
tion  of Medicare  providers  in general).

In  light  of these  considerations,  the  prescription  of
a  filing  deadline  is  the  only  thing  that  § 139500(a)
has  in  common  with  the  statutes  in  the  cases  on
which  respondents  rely.  In  fact,  if one  looks  beyond
respondents  one-dimensional  approach  to  jurisdic-
tion,  the  contextual  factors  that  define  § 139500(a)
strongly  point  to jurisdictional  status.

2.

In  addition

to  the  foregoing  arguments,
respondents  and  petitioner  offer  various  contentions

12

resting  on  generalized  assertions  of legislative  intent
and  purpose  and  (in petitioner’s  case)  on  well-known,
but  ultimately  inapposite,  principles  of  administra-
None  of  those  arguments,  however,  can
tive  law.
overcome  the  clear  import  of  § 139500(a)’s  text  and
the  clarifying  backdrop  of the  Medicare  Act's  overall
structure.

a.

Respondents—here  joined  by  petitioner—urge
this  Court  to  hold  that  Congress  has  acquiesced  in  a
flexible  understanding  of § 139500(a)(3)  by repeatedly
amending  the  statute  without  disavowing  the  long-
standing  regulatory  “good  cause”  exception  to  subsec-
tion  (a)(3).  Pet.  Br.  20,  43;  Resp.  Br.  34; see  42  C.F.R.
§ 405.1841(b)  (2007).  There  is  no  evidence,  however,
that  Congress  intended  to  endorse,  much  less  adopt,
the  flexible  understanding  of subsection  (a)(3)  implic-
it in  the  Secretary’s  regulation.

At  one  time,  this  Court  took  the  view  that,  when
Congress  revises  an  organic  act  without  altering  a
longstanding  agency  interpretation  of that  act,  Con-
gress’s  failure  to  express  disapproval  could  constitute
persuasive  evidence  that  the  agency’s  position  cap-
tured  legislative  intent.  See,  e.g.,  CFTC  v.  Schor,  478
U.S.  833,  846  (1986);  NLRB  v.  Bell  Aerospace  Co.,
416  U.S.  267,  274-75  (1974).  Recognizing  the  “limita-
tions”  of  acquiescence  “as  an  expression  of  congres-
sional  intent,”  this  Court  has  in recent  years  deemed
it “‘unpossible  to  assert  with  any  degree  of assurance
that  congressional  failure  to  [overturn  an  established
interpretation]  represents’  affirmative  congressional
approval  of  {that}  interpretation.”
Central  Bank  of
Denver,  N.A.  v.  First  Interstate  Bank  of Denver,  N.A.,
511  U.S.  164,  186  (1994)  (quoting  Patterson  v.
McLean  Credit  Union,  491  U.S.  164,  175  n.1  (1989)).
Congress  may  fail  to  correct  an  existing  interpreta-

13

tion  of a  statute  for  any  number  of reasons,  including
the  dilatory  effect  of parliamentary  tactics,  a  failure
to  focus  on  the  problem,  the  absence  of any  firm  leg-
islative  view  about  the  correctness  of  an  interpreta-
tion,  or  the  desire  not  to  confront  important  interest
groups.  See,  e.g.,  Rapanos  v.  United  States,  547  U.S.
715,  750  (2006)  (plurality  opinion)  (discussing  poten-
tial  reasons  for  legislative  inaction);  Helvering  v.
Hallock,  309  U.S.  106,  119-21  (1940)  (same).  As Jus-
tice  Frankfurter  once  wrote  for  the  Court,  “we  walk
on  quicksand  when  we  try  to  find  in  the  absence  of
corrective  legislation  a  controlling  legal  principle.”
Hallock,  309  U.S.  at  121.

Accordingly,  while  this  Court  has  found  “congres-
Slonal  acquiescence  to  administrative  interpretations
of a  statute  in  some  situations,  [it has]  done  so  with
extreme  care.”  Solid  Waste  Agency  of Northern  Cook
County  v.  United  States  Army  Corps  of Eng’rs,  531
In  particular,
U.S.  159,  169  (2001)  (“SWANCC”).
this  Court  has  explained  that,  “[aJbsent  ...  over-
whelming  evidence  of  acquiescence,  [it  is]  loath  to
replace  the  plain  text  and  original  understanding  of a
statute  with  an  amended  agency  interpretation.”  7d.
at  169n.5.  Thus,  in SWANCC,  this  Court  made  clear
that  inferring  acquiescence  is  appropriate  where  the
legislative  record  indicates  that  Congress  was  aware
of  the  agency’s  position  and,  in  some  way,  strongly
signaled  its  approval  or  acceptance  of  that  position.
See  id.; see  also  Bob  Jones  Univ.  v.  United  States,  461
U.S.  574,  600-01  (1983)  (finding  acquiescence  in light
of multiple  committee  hearings  on  the  precise  agency
interpretation  at  issue  and  Congress’s  rejection  of
13  separate  proposed  bills  aimed  at  overturning  that
interpretation).  In  this  case,  petitioner  and  respond-
ents  offer  no  evidence  that  Congress  was  aware

14

of  the  “good  cause”  regulation  when  it  amended
§ 139500,’  much  less  that  Congress  showed  any  dis-
position  to embrace  that  interpretation.®

7  Further,  even  if one  assumed  that  Congress  were  generally
aware  of the  agency's  construction  of § 139500(a)(3),  one  would
still  have  a  hard  time  pinning  down  the  precise  position  in
which  Congress  putatively  acquiesced.
As  discussed  in  our
opening  brief,  the  Secretary,  CMS.  and  the  Board  have  long
held  internally  inconsistent  views  on  § 139600(a)(3)’s  jurisdic-
tional  status.  See  Amicus  Br.  42-47  (explaining  that,  notwith-
standing  the  Secretary's  “good  cause”  exception.  all  of the  agen-
cies  charged  with  implementing  subsection  (a)(3)  have  repeat-
edly  characterized  that  provision  as  a jurisdictional  prerequisite
to the  exercise  of the  Board’s  jurisdiction).

8 Petitioner  correctly  notes  (at  48)  that  at  least  one  congres-
sional  committee  was  aware  that,  several  months  prior  to  the
enactment  of  § 139500,  the  Department  of  Health,  Education,
and  Welfare  (“HEW”)  had  promulgated  a  set  of  regulations
establishing  a  scheme  in which  providers  dissatisfied  with  their
reimbursements  could  seek  hearings  from  intermediaries.  See
8S.  Rep.  No.  92-1230,  at  248  (1972)  (referring  to  HEW’s  regula-
tions).
As  petitioner  notes  (at  48),  moreover,  one  of  HEW’s
pre-enactment  regulations  prescribed  a  regulatory  “good  cause”
exception  “virtually  identical”  to  the  one  at  issue  in  this  case.
See  20  C.F.R.  § 405.493  (1973).
To  the  extent  that  the  pre-
enactment  HEW  regulations  served  as  a  model  for  § 139500,
however,  they  cut  against  finding  that  Congress  embraced
the  “good  cause”  exception  prescribed  by the  regulations.  The
regulation  establishing  a  right  to  an  intermediary  hearing
prescribed  the  same  sort  of jurisdictional  elements  as  § 139500
ultimately  set  forth—a  “dissatisfaction”  requirement  governing
the  subject  matter  of  the  hearings,  a  $10,000  amount-in-
controversy  requirement,  and  a  60-day  timely  filing  require-
The  “good  cause”  exception  ap-
ment.
peared  in  the  very  next  regulatory  provision.  If Congress  used
the  HEW  regulations  as  a  model  for  § 139500.  it  is  significant
that  Congress  patterned  § 139500(a)  after  § 405.492(a)  but  did
not  incorporate  any  form  of the  “good  cause”  exception  found  in
the  immediately  adjacent  § 405.493.  Accordingly,  if Congress's
awareness  of  the  pre-enactment  HEW  regulations  tells  us

b.  Respondents  argue  (at  33-34)  that  reading
§ 139500(a)  as  jurisdictional  will  thwart  the  statute’s
general  purpose  of  “establish[ing]  the  Board  to  give
providers  the  right  to  an  administrative  hearing  to
assure  accuracy  in  their  payments,”  and  that  treat-
ing  the  filing  period  as  “nonjurisdictional  is  the  only
viable  mechanism  for  enforcing  a  much-needed  insti-
tutional  check  on  agency  missteps,  knowing  miscal-
culations,  and  other  serious  errors.”®  Reasoning  from
such  general  purposes  to  specific  statutory  outcomes
is  particularly  treacherous  when  it  comes  to  the
design  of  timely  filing  requirements.
Such  require-
ments,  by the  nature,  sometimes  cut  off meritorious
claims  in  order  to  serve  “a  broader  system-related
goal,  such  as  facilitating  the  administration  of
claims,  limiting  the  scope  of  a  governmental  waiver
of  sovereign  immunity,  or  promoting  judicial  effi-
ciency.”  John  R.  Sand  &  Gravel,  552  U.S.  at  133-34
In  other  words,  the  systemic
(citations  omitted).
objective  of  protecting  administrative  workability
through  the  imposition  of filing  deadlines  necessarily
comes  at  “the  price  of  occasional  unfairness  in  indi-

anything.  it  is  that  Congress  opted  against  adopting  the  “good
cause”  exception  that  accompanied  its  model  for  the  Board’s
juriediction.

9  Respondents  (at  33)  and  amicus  Southwest  Consulting
Associates,  LP  (at  5) argue  that  equitable  tolling  is particularly
necessary  here  because  of  alleged  agency  concealment  of facts
necessary  for  respondents  to  challenge  their  adjustment  for
serving  a  “disproportionate  share”  of low  income  patients  under
the  Act.  These  factual  allegations,  however,  go  to  the  ultimate
question  whether  equitable  tolling  would  be  appropriate,  if
available,  and  not  to  the  antecedent  legal  question  whether
Congress  adopted  a  categorical  jurisdictional  deadline  that  pre-
cludes  equitable  tolling.

vidual  cases.”
347,  352-53  (1997).

United  States  v.  Brockamp,  519  U.S.

Accordingly,  it  is  uninformative  to  emphasize,  as
respondents  do,  that  reading  a  timely  filing  require-
ment  as  jurisdictional  will  sometimes  preclude  a
provider  from  receiving  payments  they  are  due.  To
say  that  the  purpose  of the  Medicare  Act  is to  ensure
the  payment  of meritorious  claims  is  to  say  nothing
about  how  Congress  struck  the  balance  between
that  goal,  on  the  one  hand,  and  the  competing
goals  of  administrability  and  repose,  on  the  other.
Respondents  offer  no  evidence  to  suggest  that  read-
ing  § 139500(a)  as  jurisdictional  would  contradict  a
specific  purpose  articulated  in  the  text  of the  statute
or  even  its  legislative  history.'!°  To  the  contrary,  the
Medicare  Act’s  disparate  treatment  of the  respective
deadlines
for  beneficiary  and  provider  appeals
demonstrates  that  Congress  knew  how  to  provide  for
administrative  flexibility  in  Medicare  appeals  and
opted  against  doing  so  where  provider  appeals  are
concerned.
To  disregard  that  concrete  and  direct
textual  signal  in  favor  of  respondents’  generalized
assertions  of  purpose  would  disregard  the  fact  that
interpreters  are  “bound,  not  only  by  the  ultimate

10  The  legislative  history  says  almost  nothing  about  the  par-
ticular  character  of the  timely  filing requirement.  To the  extent
that  it  does.  the  House  and  Senate  committee  reports  merely
state  that  any  provider  of services  “which  has  filed  a  timely  cost
report  may  appeal  an  adverse  final  decision  of the  fiscal  inter-
mediary”  and  that  “[t]he  appeal  must  be  filed  within  180  days
after  notice  of  the  fiscal  intermediary's  final  determination.”
H.R.  Rep.  No.  92-231,  at  108  (1971).  reprinted  in 1972  U.S.C.CAN.

c.

Petitioner  argues  that  general  principles  of
administrative  law  justify  her  regulatory  “good
cause”  exception  to  § 139500(a)’s  180-day  timely
filing  requirement.  In  particular,  petitioner  contends
that  “‘agencies  should  be  free  to  fashion  their  own
rules  of procedure’”  as  long  as  the  resultant  rules  are
not  arbitrary,  capricious,  or  contrary  to  law.  Pet.  Br.
17  (quoting  Vermont  Yankee  Nuclear  Power  Corp.  v.
Natural  Res.  Def.  Council,  Inc.,  435  U.S.  519,  543-44
(1978)).  From  that  starting  point,  petitioner  argues
that,  while  it is  “reasonable  ...  to  characterize  th[e]
time  limit  as  ‘jurisdictional’  in  the  sense  that  it
constrains  the  Board’s  adjudicative  powers,”  the
Secretary's  “authority  to  interpret  and  implement
Section  139500”  enables  her  “to  extend  the  time  limit
in  certain  circumstances.”
Accordingly,
she  argues,  the  statutory  filing  deadline  and  the
implementing  regulations—including  the  regulatory
“good  cause”  exception  to  subsection  (a)(3)—“together
establish  the  time  limits  on  the  Board’s  adjudicative
powers.”  Id.

Jd.  at  47.

Petitioner's  argument  begs  the  question.

This
Court  has  made  clear  that,  because  jurisdictional
rules  define  a  tribunal’s  very  authority  to  act,  such
rules  are  absolute  and  not  subject  to  waiver,  forfei-
ture,  or  (in the  case  of filing  deadlines)  equitable  toll-
ing.  See,  e.g.,  Gonzalez  v.  Thaler,  132  8.  Ct.  641,  648
(2012);  Dolan  v.  United  States,  130  S.  Ct.  2533,  2538
(2010);  John  R.  Sand  &  Gravel,  552  U.S.  at  133-34;
United  States  v.  Cotton,  535  U.S.  625,  630  (2002).
Accordingly,  because  § 139500  is  jurisdictional,  the

18

Secretary  may  not  authorize  the  Board,  in  effect,  to
waive  the  statutory  filing  deadline  where  conditions
specified  by  the  Secretary  are  met.  This  reality  pre-
cludes  the  Secretary  from  simultaneously  asserting
(at  47)  that  “Section  139500(a)(3)  limits  the  Board’s
jurisdiction”  but  “does  not  preclude  the  Secretary
from  adopting  a  ...  good-cause  [exception]”  to  that
jurisdictional  limit.

3.

Finally,  the  unsustainable  breadth  of respond-
ents’  position  is  underscored  by  their  contention  (at
26-28)  that  no  part  of  subsection  (a)—including  its
dissatisfaction  and  amount-in-controversy  require-
ments—is  jurisdictional.  Initially,  respondents  argue
(at 27)  that,  “for  decades  without  congressional  objec-
tion,  the  Board  has  been  empowered  to  issue  deci-
sions  on  matters  other  than  those  specifically  listed”
among  subsection  (a)(1)’s  subject-matter  require-
ments.
As  noted  in  our  opening  brief  (at  21  n.12),
however,  the  Board,  CMS,  and  the  Secretary  herself
have  all  consistently  treated  subsection  (a)(1)’s  “dis-
satisfaction”  requirement  as  binding,  nonwaivable,
and  jurisdictional.  Respondents  do  not  cite  a  single
instance  in which  the  Board  has  claimed  authority  to
adjudicate  a  matter  falling  outside  subsection  (a)(1)’s
ambit.  Nor  do respondents  point  to  a  single  sentence
in  any  applicable  regulation,  regulatory  docket,  or
decision  of the  Board  or  CMS  that  suggests  that  any
agency  responsible  for  implementing  this  review
scheme  regards  the  subject-matter  requirements  of
subsection  (a)(1)  as  optional.?!

11  Rather,  in  support  of their  view  that  the  Board  may  hear
cases  that  fall  outside  subsection  (a){1)’s  scope,  respondents
point  only  to  a  1987  rulemaking  that.  in their  view.  permits  the
Board  to  review  “hospitals’  challenges  to  the  specific  diagnosis-
related  group  [(“DRG”)]  assigned  to  their  inpatient  stays.  even

Respondents
nevertheless  contend  (at  27-28)  that,  by  permitting

Secretary  has  displayed  a  “flexib[ility]”  in construing
