1

INTEREST  OF AMICUS  CURIAE

Quality  Reimbursement  Services  (QRS)  is a  large
healthcare  consulting  organization  with  offices  through-
out  the  United  States.’  QRS  has  extensive  experience
in  assisting  hospitals  with  respect  to  analyzing  the
various  components  of the  Medicare  Disproportionate
Share  (DSH)  payment  adjustment.  QRS  represents  a
significant  number  of hospitals  in  appeals  before  the
Provider  Reimbursement  Review  Board  (PRRB)  in
cases  involving  the  DSH  payment  adjustment,  as  well
as  other  aspects  of  Medicare  reimbursement.  QRS
is  currently  assisting  several  hospitals  in  connection
with  numerous  equitable  tolling  appeals  now  pending
before  the  PRRB.  The  Court’s  decision  in  this  case
will  likely  be  dispositive  as  to  the  outcome  of  these
appeals.

4

SUMMARY  OF ARGUMENT

I.  The  Secretary’s  regulations  authorizing  an  exten-
sion  of  the  180  day  appeal  period  are  arbitrary  and
capricious.  These  regulations  impose  an  absolute  bar
against  extending  the  time  period  for  filing  appeals
more  than  three  years  after  the  determination  of the

No  counsel  for  a  party  authored  any  part  of this  brief,  and
no  person  other  than  amicus  or  its counsel  made  any  financial  or
other  material  contribution  to  fund  the  preparation  or  submis-
sion  of  this  brief.  Both  petitioner  and  respondents  have  fur-
nished  their  written  consent  to the  filing  of this  brief.

2

fiscal  intermediary.  These  regulations  are  therefore
inconsistent  with  generally  established  equitable  toll-
ing  principles,  which  require  that  the  appropriate
tolling  period  be determined  on a case  by case  basis.

II.  The  duties  and  obligations  of  a  Medicare  pro-
vider  are  set  forth  in  an  agreement  with  the  Secre-
tary  mandated  by  § 1866  of  the  Social  Security  Act,
42  U.S.C.  § 1395cc.  This  contract  stipulates  that  the
provider  will  perform  certain  services  in  accordance
with  statutes  and  regulations,  and  in  return  will  be
eligible  to  receive  reimbursement  from  the  Secretary
for those  services.

Prior  to  the  establishment  of  the  PRRB,  the
former  U.S.  Court  of  Claims  had  jurisdiction  under
the  Tucker  Act,  28  U.S.C.  § 1491,  over  provider  reim-
bursement  disputes  arising  under  the  Medicare  Act.
The  basis  for  this  jurisdiction  was  the  section  of the
Tucker  Act  authorizing  the  U.S.  Court  of  Claims  to
adjudicate  contract  disputes  between  private  parties
and  the  U.S.  government.  Accordingly,  Auburn’s  claims
in  this  case  are  analogous  to  claims  sounding  in  con-
tract,  which  is  an  area  familiar  to  private  litigation.
This  further  supports  the  availability  of  equitable
tolling  to  the  case  at  bar.

III.  The  Centers  for  Medicare  and  Medicaid  Ser-
vices  (CMS)  has  a  fiduciary  obligation  with  respect
to  its  administration  of the  Federal  Hospital  Insur-
ance  Trust  Fund.  Medicare  disproportionate  share
(DSH)  payments  are  made  for  the  benefit  of hospital,
as  opposed  to  Medicare  beneficiaries.  Therefore,  the

3

Secretary’s  fiduciary  obligation  to  disclose  payment
errors  extended  to  the  hospitals  in  this  case.  The
Secretary’s  failure  to  disclose  these  errors  deprived
the  hospitals  of an  opportunity  to  appeal  within  the
180  day  period  prescribed  by  statute.  Allowing  equi-
table  tolling  would  be  consistent  with  the  intent  of
Congress  in  imposing  fiduciary  disclosure  obligations
upon  the  Secretary.

4

ARGUMENT

I.

The  Regulation  Authorizing  the  Board  to
Extend  the  180  Day  Filing  Deadline  Cannot
Withstand  Scrutiny  under  the  Arbitrary
and  Capricious  Standard  of Review

Pursuant  to  42  U.S.C.  § 139500(a)(3),  hospitals
may  appeal  a  final  determination  of  its  fiscal  inter-
mediary  (often  referred  to  as  an  “NPR”)  to  the  Pro-
vider  Reimbursement  Review  Board  (“PRRB”)  within
180  days  after  receipt  of  the  NPR.  The  Secretary’s
regulations  further  provide  for  a  “good  cause”  exten-
sion  to  the  180  day  requirement.  42  C.F-R.  § 405.1836.
An  extension  may  be  granted  only  if  the  provider
demonstrates  that  “it  could  not  reasonably  be  ex-
pected  to  file  timely  due  to  extraordinary  circum-
stances  beyond  its  control.”  42  C.F.R.  § 405.1836(b).
These  regulations  prohibit  the  Board  from  hearing
any  appeal  filed  more  than  three  (3)  years  after  is-
suance  of  the  NPR,  regardless  of the  circumstances
of the  individual  case.  42  C.F.R.  § 405.1836(c)(2).  The
Secretary’s  regulations  further  provide  that  a  decision

4d

by  the  PRRB  that  the  provider  did  not  demonstrate
good  cause  for  requesting  an  extension  is  not  subject
to judicial  review.  42  C.F.R.  § 405.1836(ex4).

The  Secretary  concedes  that  the  provisions  of 42
C.F.R.  § 405.1836,  which  authorizes  the  PRRB  to  ex-
tend  the  180  day  filing  deadline,  is  subject  to  review
under  an  arbitrary  and  capricious  standard.  Pet.  Br.
at  16-17;  29-31.  This  regulation  cannot  withstand
scrutiny  under  this  standard,  even  assuming  that  its
promulgation  was  within  the  scope  of the  Secretary’s
regulatory  authority.

The  hallmark  of review  under  the  arbitrary  and
capricious  standard  is  reasoned  decision-making.
This  requires  the  agency  to  articulate  a  satisfactory
explanation  for  its  action,  including  a  “rational  con-
nection  between  the  facts  found  and  the  choice  made.”
Motor  Vehicle  Mfrs.  Ass’n  v.  State  Farm  Mut.  Auto.
Ins.  Co.,  463  U.S.  29,  43,  77  L. Ed.  2d  443,  103  S. Ct.
2856  (1983)  (quoting  Burlington  Truck  Lines,  Inc.  v.
United  States,  371  U.S.  156,  168  (1962)).  In  review-
ing  the  agency’s  explanation,  a  court  must  “consider
whether  the  decision  was  based  on  a  consideration  of
the  relevant  factors  and  whether  there  has  been  a
clear  error  of  judgment.”  Jd.  See  also  Citizens  to
Preserve  Overton  Park  v.  Volpe,  401  U.S.  402,  416,  28
L. Ed.  2d  136,  91  S. Ct.  814  (1971).

The  regulation  permitting  the  Board  to  extend
the  180  day  filing  deadline  is not  technically  an  equi-
table  tolling  provision.  This  regulation  permits  the
PRRB  to  accept  appeals  “after  the  applicable  180  day

5

time  limit”  has  expired.  42  C.F.R.  § 405.1836(a).  On
the  other  hand,  equitable  tolling  does  not  extend  the
180  day  period,  but  rather  stops  the  running  of  the
180  day  clock.  Harper  v.  Ercole,  648  F.3d  132,  139-140
(2d  Cir.  2011).

Nonetheless,  the  normative  principles  governing
equitable  tolling  are  at  least  by analogy  applicable  to
the  Secretary’s  regulations.  The  Secretary’s  regula-
tions  are  utterly  inconsistent  with  these  principles.

As  a  general  rule,  a  party  is  entitled  to  equitable
tolling  by  demonstrating  that  “(1)  that  he  has  been
pursuing  his  rights  diligently,  and  (2)  that  some
extraordinary  circumstance  stood  in  his  way.”  Pace  v.
DiGuglielmo,  544  U.S.  408,  418,  125  S.  Ct.  1807,  161
L.  Ed.  2d 669  (2005).  The  decision  as  to  whether  equi-
table  tolling  applies  must  be  made  “on  a  case-by-case
basis.”  Holland  v.  Florida,  130  S.  Ct.  2549,  2563,  177
L.  Ed.  2d  130  (2010).  Accordingly,  the  court  must  be
guided  by “awareness  of the  fact  that  specific  circum-
stances,  often  hard  to  predict  in  advance,  could  war-
rant  special  treatment  in  an  appropriate  case.”  Jd.
at  2563.  As  noted  in  Holland,  courts  must  be  cogni-
zant  of “the  need  for  flexibility”  and  “for  avoiding  me-
chanical  rules”  in  deciding  whether  equitable  tolling
should  be  applied.  Jd.  at  2563  (internal  quotations
omitted).

The  Secretary’s  regulations  eschew  a  flexible
approach  in  favor  of  rigid  and  absolute  prohibi-
tions.  These  regulations  provide  that  an  appeal  filed
more  than  three  years  after  the  fiscal  intermediary’s

6

determination  is  prohibited  under  any  and  all  cir-
cumstances.  42  C.F.R.  § 405.1836(cX2).  The  imposi-
tion  of such  fixed  and  rigid  deadlines  is not  consistent
with  well  established  principles  of equitable  tolling.

In  her  preamble  to  the  2008  revisions,  the  Secre-
tary  stated  that  the  three  year  limit  was  justified
because  it  is  a  sufficient  period  of  time  “to  recover
from  the  unseen  event  and  gather  the  necessary
records,  and  make  the  necessary  preparations  for
filing  an  appeal.”  73  Fed.  Reg.  30190,  30206,  May  23,
2008.  However,  the  amount  of  time  required  to  re-
cover  from  the  extraordinary  circumstances  which
prevented  timely  filing  will  inevitably  vary  from  case
to  case.  Holland,  130  S. Ct.  at  2563.  As  recognized  in
Holland,  it is  not  possible  to  fix  an  outer  limit  to  the
equitable  tolling  period.

The  regulation  at  42  C.F.R.  § 405.1836  is  not
consistent  with  the  Secretary’s  stated  purpose  of pro-
viding  sufficient  time  for  providers  to  file  their  ap-
peals  when  “extraordinary  circumstances”  occur.  Id.
at  § 405.1836(b).  This  case  demonstrates  that  pro-
viders  may  be  deprived  of the  information  required  to
assert  their  appeals  for  a  considerably  longer  period
than  three  years.  The  Secretary’s  refusal  to  acknow!l-
edge  this  possibility  by  arbitrarily  concluding  that
three  years  will  always  be  an  adequate  time  period
for  the  provider  to  recover  from  any  delay  reflects  an
arbitrary  and  capricious  conclusion  on  the  part  of the
agency.  Burlington  Truck  Lines,  Inc.  v.  United  States,
371  U.S.  156,  168  (1962).

7

II.  Claims  under  the  Medicare  Act  Are  Analo-

gous  To Contract  Claims

The  availability  of equitable  tolling  may  depend
upon  whether  the  claims  asserted  against  the  gov-
ernment  are  analogous  to  those  which  arise  in  the
context  of litigation  between  private  parties.  Chung  v.
United  States  DOJ,  333  F.3d  273,  277  (D.C.  Cir.
2003).  The  Court  of Appeals  correctly  held  that  Au-
burn’s  claims  for  additional  provider  reimbursement
under  the  Medicare  Act  were  analogous  to  actions
v.
sounding  in  contract.  Auburn  Reg’l  Med.  Ctr.
Sebelius,  642  F.3d  1145,  1149,  fn.  1 (D.C.  Cir.  2011).

Health  care  facilities  must  sign  a  detailed  con-
tract  with  the  Secretary  if they  choose  to  participate
in  the  Medicare  program.  This  agreement  is  com-
monly  referred  to  as  the  “provider  agreement.”  The
contents  of the  provider  agreement  are  established  by
§ 1866  of the  Social  Security  Act,  42  U.S.C.  § 1395cc.
This  agreement  imposes  detailed  obligations  upon
providers  in  connection  with  the  provision  of services
to  Medicare  beneficiaries.  In exchange,  the  provider  is
eligible  for  payment  for  these  services  as  computed  in
accordance  with  the  provisions  of  the  Medicare  Act.
42  U.S.C.  § 1395¢ec(a)(1).

Participation  in  the  Medicare  program  on  the
part  of  providers  is  voluntary.  Queen  City  Home
Health  Care  Co.  v.  Sullivan,  978  F.2d  236,  247  (6th
Cir.  1992).  Like  other  federal  contractors,  Medicare
providers  voluntarily  agree  to  perform  services  in
the  manner  required  by  their  agreements  with  the

8

government  in  exchange  for  payment.  See  e.g.,  Jn  re
University  Medical  Center,  973  F.2d  1065,  1076-1077
(3d  Cir.  1992)  (Medicare  provider  agreements  are
executory  contracts,  which  like  other  government  con-
tracts  are  subject  to  assumption  or  rejection  by  a
debtor  under  the  Bankruptcy  Code);  Richey  Manor,
Inc.  v.  Schweiker,  684  F.2d  130,  137  (D.C.  Cir.  1982)
(noting  that  the  provider  agreement  is  a  contract  be-
tween  the  provider  and  the  Secretary);  Delta  Health
Group,  Inc.  v.  United  States  HHS,  459  F. Supp.  2d
1207,  1209  (N.D.  Fla.  2006)  (“The  provider  agree-
ment,  at  bottom,  acts  as  a  contract  with  the  federal
government  in  which  the  provider  agrees  to  furnish
quality  nursing  services  to  Medicare  beneficiaries  in
compliance  with  all  the  applicable  federal  and  state
regulations.”).

Prior  to  the  establishment  of the  PRRB  in  1973,
provider  reimbursement  disputes  were  adjudicated  by
the  former  United  States  Court  of Claims,  pursuant
to  its  Tucker  Act  jurisdiction,  i.e.,  28  U.S.C.  § 1491.
Dr.  John  T:  MacDonald  Foundation,  Inc.  v.  Califano,
571  F.2d  328,  332  (5th  Cir  1978);  Humana  of South
Carolina,  Inc.  v.  Califano,  590  F.2d  1070,  1077,  fn.  46
(D.C.  Cir.  1978).  The  Tucker  Act  provided  that  the
Court  of  Claims  (now  the  United  States  Court  of
Federal  Claims)  “shall  have  jurisdiction  to  render
judgment  upon  any  claim  against  the  United  States
founded  either  upon  the  Constitution,  or  any  Act  of
Congress  or  any  regulation  of  an  executive  depart-
ment,  or  upon  any  express  or  implied  contract  with
the  United  States.”  28 U.S.C.  § 1491.

9

In  Whitecliff,  Inc.  v.  United  States,  536  F.2d  347
(Ct.  Cl.  1976),  a Medicare  provider  appealed  the  final
determination  of  its  fiscal  intermediary  denying  a
request  for  increased  reimbursement.  The  Court  of
Claims  held  that  it had  jurisdiction  under  the  Tucker
Act  to  adjudicate  the  dispute  “both  because  of plain-
tiff’s  contract  with  the  Government  and  also  because
the  Medicare  legislation,  fairly  read,  mandates  appro-
priate  payment  to  providers.”  Jd.  at  351;  see  also  Rio
Hondo  Memorial  Hospital  v.  United  States,  689  F.2d
1025,  1028  (Ct.  Cl.  1982).

Thus,  until  the  advent  of  the  PRRB,  provider
reimbursement  claims  under  the  Medicare  Act  were
treated  as  contract  claims  against  the  United  States.
In  Bowen  v.  Massachusetts,  487  U.S.  879,  108  S.  Ct.
2722,  101  L.  Ed.  2d  749  (1988),  Justice  Scalia  in  dis-
sent  suggested  that  the  Court  of Claims,  and  not  the
federal  district  courts,  had  jurisdiction  under  the
Tucker  Act  over  Medicaid  reimbursement  claims  sub-
mitted  by the  State  of Massachusetts.  Justice  Scalia
stated  as  follows  in this  regard:

The  Medicaid  Act  itself can  be  analogized  to
a  unilateral  offer for contract  —  offering  to pay
specified  sums  in  return  for  the  performance
of specified  services  and  inviting  the  States  to
accept  the  offer  by performance.

Bowen  v.  Massachusetts,  487  U.S.  879,  923  (1988).

As  a  result  of  the  enactment  of  42  U.S.C.
§ 139500,  jurisdiction  over  provider  reimbursement
claims  under  the  Medicare  Act  no  longer  lies  under

10

the  Tucker  Act,  but  instead  rests  exclusively  with  the
federal  district  courts.  St.  Vincent’s  Medical  Ctr.  v.
United  States,  32  F.3d  548,  549-550  (Fed.  Cir.  1994).
Nonetheless,  Justice  Scalia’s  offer  to  contract  analogy
in  the  Medicaid  context  is equally  applicable  to  Medi-
care.  The  federal  government  invites  health  care  pro-
viders  to  contract  with  the  Secretary  by entering  into
the  provider  contract  specified  by  } 1956  of the  Social
Security  Act,  42  U.S.C.  § 1395cc.

The  Secretary  in  this  case  breached  the  provider
agreement  by  failing  to  pay  hospitals  in  accordance
with  the  statutory  formula  mandated  by the  Medicare
Act.  Accordingly,  the  Court  of Appeals  correctly  ap-
plied  the  presumption  of  equitable  tolling  on  the
grounds  that  Auburn’s  request  for  additional  Medi-
care  reimbursement  was  analogous  to  a  claim  sound-
ing in contract.

Ill.  Equitable  Tolling  Should  Be  Permitted  in
this  Case  Based  Upon  the  Government’s  Fi-
duciary  Obligation  to  Disclose  Underpay-
ments  to Providers

Auburn  is  seeking  to  recover  disproportionate
share  funds  resulting  from  inpatient  hospitalization
services  rendered  to  program  beneficiaries.  42  U.S.C.
§ 1395ww(d)(5XF).  With  a  few  exceptions  involving
managed  care,  disproportionate  share  payments  to
hospitals  are  made  through  the  Federal  Hospital
Insurance  Trust  Fund  (hereinafter  “Part  A  Trust
Fund”).  42  U.S.C.  § 1395i.  The  Part  A  Trust  Fund

ll

is  itself  financed  through  payroll  contributions.  42
U.S.C.  § 1395i(a).

At  common  law,  a  trust  arises  when  three  ele-
ments  are  present;  i.e.,  a trustee,  a  beneficiary,  and  a
trust  corpus.  White  Mt.  Apache  Tribe  v.  United  States,
249  F.3d  1364,  1373  (Fed.  Cir.  2001).  The  Supreme
Court  has  recognized  that  a  heightened  fiduciary  re-
sponsibility  is  imposed  upon  the  U.S.  government
when  it  acts  as  a  trustee.  United  States  v.  Mitchell,
463  U.S.  206,  224-225,  103  S. Ct.  2961,  77  L. Ed.  2d
580  (1983).

The  Part  A Trust  Fund  is  administered  by  trus-
tees  designated  by  statute  to  serve  in  that  capacity.
The  Secretary  of Health  and  Human  Services,  along
with  other  high  ranking  government  officials,  is  des-
ignated  by  statute  as  a  trustee  of  the  Part  A  Trust
Fund.  42  U.S.C.  § 1395i(b).  The  Secretary  of  Health
and  Human  Services  certifies  to  the  Managing  Trus-
tee  the  payments  which  are  required  to  be  made
under  Medicare  Part A. 42  U.S.C.  § 1395i(h).

The  Secretary,  who  serves  on  an  ex  officio  basis,
is  not  individually  liable  as  a  fiduciary  of  the  trust
fund.  42  U.S.C.  § 1395i(b).  However,  this  does  not  in
any  way  diminish  the  fiduciary  obligation  of the  U.S.
Department  of Health  and  Human  Services  to  prop-
erly  certify  the  amounts  which  are  required  to be  paid
from  the  Part  A  Trust  Fund.  42  U.S.C.  § 1395i(h).
Indeed,  the  Centers  for  Medicare  &  Medicaid  Ser-
vices  (“CMS”)  often  refer  in  various  public  issuances
to  its  fiduciary  responsibility  to  make  appropriate

12

payments  from  the  trust  fund.  See  e.g.,  71  Fed.  Reg.
27798,  27884,  May  12,  2006  (“The  mandate  for  im-
plementing  the  Medicare  program  tasks  CMS  with
fiduciary  responsibilities  that  require  us  to  develop
an  effective  and  efficient  payment  system...  .”);  72
Fed.  Reg.  47130,  47247,  August  22,  2007  (noting  the
Secretary’s  “fiduciary  responsibility  to  the  Medicare
trust  fund  to  ensure  that  Medicare  pays  only  for
covered  services.”);  71  Fed.  Reg.  47870,  47908,  August
18,  2006  (CMS  has  “a  fiduciary  responsibility  to  ad-
minister  the  trust  fund  in  order  to  provide  quality
care  for  our  beneficiaries. . .  .”).

Generally  speaking,  the  intended  beneficiaries  of
the  Medicare  trust  funds  are  individuals  covered  by
the  program,  as  opposed  to  hospitals.  However,  the
present  case  is different,  due  to  the  unique  purpose  of
the  disproportionate  share  (DSH)  payment  adjust-

ment.

The  Medicare  DSH  adjustment  was  intended  to
compensate  hospitals  for  the  higher  per  case  cost  of
treating  all  low  income  patients,  including  both  Medi-
care  and  non-Medicare  patients.  See  H.R.  REP.  No.
99-241(I)  (1985),  reprinted  in  1986  U.S.C.C.A.N.  579,
594.  Congress  chose  two  proxies  for  measuring  the
low  income  patient  population:  (a)  Medicare  patients
who  are  entitled  to  Supplemental  Security  Income
(SSI),  and  (b)  non-Medicare  patients  who  are  eligible
Xvi  DID;
for  Medicaid.  42  U.S.C.  § 13895ww(d)5XF
Jewish  Hosp.  v.  Secretary  of Health  & Human  Servs.,
19  F.3d  270,  272  (6th  Cir.  1994).

13

Hospitals  that  qualify  for  the  disproportionate
share  adjustment  receive  an  increased  payment  amount
for every  Medicare  patient  discharge,  regardless  of the
financial  resources  of the  patient  on  whose  behalf the
payment  is  made.  42  U.S.C.  § 1395ww(d)5XF)(ii).
The  reason  is  that  the  DSH  adjustment  is  not  in-
tended  for  the  benefit  of the  patient  on  whose  behalf
it  is  actually  paid.  Instead,  the  DSH  adjustment  is
intended  to  compensate  hospitals  for  their  higher  per
case  cost  of  treating  all  low  income  individuals,  in-
cluding  both  Medicare  and  non-Medicare  patients.

Of course,  the  purpose  of the  DSH  adjustment  is
not  to  benefit  hospitals  per  se,  but  rather  to  subsidize
their  costs  of treating  low  income  individuals.  None-
theless,  the  DSH  adjustment  represents  a  unique
situation  in which  a  payment  is made  from  the  Part  A
Trust  Fund  that  is  not  for  the  benefit  of the  program
beneficiary  on  whose  behalf  it is made,  but  rather  for
hospitals  who  would  otherwise  be  required  to  absorb
the  added  cost  of treating  low  income  persons.

As  such,  CMS  had a heightened  responsibility  to
deal  fairly  with  hospitals  in  connection  with  its
administration  of the  DSH  program.  At  common  law,
the  duty  to  disclose  is  a  core  fiduciary  obligation.
Eddy  v.  Colonial  Life  Ins.  Co.,  919  F.2d  747,  750-751.
(D.C.  Cir.  1990).  In  the  present  case,  the  Secretary
was  aware  of material  errors  in  her  computation  of
the  Medicare/SSI  fraction  of the  DSH  adjustment,  but
failed  to  disclose  this  information  to  the  plaintiffs.
See  Complaint  at  738.

14

The  Secretary’s  duty  to  disclose  these  errors  was
heightened  by  the  fact  that  she  is  solely  responsible
for  the  computation  of  the  SSI  fraction.  42  C.F.R.
§ 412.106(bxX2).  The  Secretary  enjoys  a  monopoly  of
ownership  over  the  data  required  to  compute  this
fraction  due  to  the  government’s  refusal  to  release
SSI  entitlement  data  to  hospitals.  70  Fed.  Reg.  47278,
47440,  August  12, 2005.

The  circumstances  of this  case  indicate  that  the
government  itself,  either  intentionally  or  inadvertently,
was  responsible  for  the  hospitals’  failure  to  timely  file
their  claims.  Congress  created  a  trust  fund  and  im-
posed  fiduciary  obligations  upon  the  agencies  respon-
sible  for  administering  that  fund.  This  purpose  would
not  be  served  if hospitals  are  precluded  from  filing
their  appeals  solely  as  a  result  of  the  Secretary’s
actions  in  failing  to  disclose  critical  payment  errors.

4

CONCLUSION

For  the  reasons  stated  herein,  amicus  Quality
Reimbursement  Services  respectfully  requests  that
this  Court  affirm  the  decision  of  the  U.S.  Court  of

15

Appeals  for  the  D.C.  Circuit,  and  rule  that  the  180
day  filing  period  specified  in  42  U.S.C.  § 139500(a)(3)
is subject  to  equitable  tolling.

Respectfully  submitted,

STATEMENT  OF  INTEREST!

Southwest  Consulting  Associates,  LP  (“Southwest”)
is a  healthcare  consulting  firm,  founded  in  1989  and
managed  and  staffed  by experienced  accounting  and
information  systems  professionals  as  well  as  former
executives.*
hospital

operations

financial

and

!  No  party  or  counsel  for  a  party  authored  or  paid  for  this
brief  in  whole  or  in  part,  or  made  a  monetary  contribution  to
fund  the  briefs  preparation  or  submission.  No  one  other  than
amicus  or  its counsel  made  a  monetary  contribution  to  the  brief.
Letters  reflecting  all  parties’  consent  to  this  brief  are  on  file
with  the  Clerk.

2  At  its  founding  and  for  some  of the  years  discussed  in  this
brief,  Southwest  did  business  as  Southwest  Consulting  Associ-
ates,  Inc.,  which  is  now  an  affiliate  of the  limited  partnership
entity.

2

Southwest  serves  hospital  clients  in  41  states,  and
devotes  its  practice  to  assuring  that  these  clients
receive  the  reimbursement  due  them  under  the
Medicare  program  each  year  (no  more  and  no  less),
including  particularly  the  correct  amount  of “DSH”
payments?  they  are  supposed  to  receive  for  serving  a
“disproportionate  share”  of poor  patients.

Southwest's  services  include  analysis  of  relevant
public  and  hospital  data  that  may  bear  on  the  client’s
Medicare  reimbursement;  preparation  of  the  neces-
sary  reports  for  submission  to  the  Secretary's  agency
(“CMS”)  and  its  Medicare  Administrative  Contrac-
tors  (formerly  known  as  “fiscal  intermediaries”);
monitoring  of  legislative,  regulatory,  and  litigation
developments  in  the  Medicare  and  Medicaid  reim-
bursement  fields;  and  assistance  with  dispute  resolu-
tion  and  litigation  (both  administrative  and  in  feder-
al court),  in the  event  that  the  hospital  is underpaid.

In  its  litigation  role,  Southwest  has  served  as  pri-
mary  consultant  on  numerous  “group  appeals,”
whereby  large  groups  of hospitals  (sometimes  aggre-
gating  hundred  of hospitals  and  thousands  of fiscal
years)  bring  a  common  challenge  to  a  particular
agency  policy,  practice,  or  regulation  which  they
believe  has  caused  or  will  cause  them  to  be  under-
paid  their  DSH  reimbursemen.  Southwest  has  been
involved  in  some  of the  most  significant  reimburse-
ment  litigation  of the  last  decade,  including  advising
the  lead  plaintiff  and  many  other  hospitals  in consol-
idated  cases  that  successfully  challenged  underpay-

provides  the  factual  grounding  for  Auburn’s  points
about  the  need  for  equitable  relief  as  a  measured,
appropriate,  and  much-needed  check  on  a  key public
healthcare  agency  that  often  performs  enormously
important  reimbursement  calculations  behind  closed
doors.  Based  on  its  direct  involvement  in  Baystate

SUMMARY  OF ARGUMENT

From  the  Secretary’s  description,  one  might  get
the  impression  that  “the  system  worked”  in Baystate,
that  the  provider  spotted  mistakes  in  its  DSH
calculations  soon  enough  to  timely  appeal  them,  and
then  was  able  to  satisfy  the  agency’s  Board  and  a
federal  district  court  that  the  agency  just  came  up  a
little  short,  making  a  few  minor  mistakes  because  of
inevitable  problems  with  complex  data.  See  Pet.  Br.
10.

According  to  the  Secretary,  nothing  in  that  story
should  excuse  “sophisticated”  providers  from  the
demands  of an  appeals  process  that  has  “functioned
appropriately  for  almost  four  decades.”

But  that  is a  deeply  revisionist  story.  The  picture
it  paints  of  a  well-functioning  and  well-intentioned
never  needing  equitable
administrative

regime,

5

intervention  by  the  judiciary,  is  belied  by  what
actually  happened  in  Baystate  and  at  least  one  other
recent,  strikingly  similar  case  called  Cape  Cod.  Each
involved  recurring  errors  in a  vital  but  fairly  obscure
calculation  that  the  agency  performs  on  its  own,
without  any  outside  review.  In each  case,  the  errors
cost  hospitals  billions  in  reimbursement  for  the
services  they  provided  to  Medicare  patients.  They
were  camouflaged  in each  case  by the  agency’s  public
misstatements,  they  persisted  well  past  the  point
when  the  agency  figured  out  what  it  vas  doing
wrong,  and  they  came  to  light  only  because  someone
at  the  agency  let  slip  to  a  consultant  just  enough
information  to  raise  questions  about  what  was  really
going  on.

And  even  then,  especially  in  Baystate,  it  took
years  of litigation  and  millions  in  legal  fees  to  break
through  the  Secretary's  determined  secrecy  and
confirm  the  outlines  of her  agency’s  mistakes  for  the
broader  community  of  providers,  who  otherwise
would  have  had  no  reason  to  think  they  had  been
underpaid—and  may  not  even  today,  thanks  to
measures  the  agency  has  since  taken  to  mask  the
impact  and  obstruct  provider  inquiries.
Indeed,  as
discussed  more  fully  below,  within  months  of losing
Baystate  at  the  district  court  level  in  2008,  the
agency  comprehensively  revised  its  rules  to  limit
testimony,  discovery,  and  the  scope  of  contested
issues  in  its  Board  hearings,  making  it  that  much
more  difficult  for  anyone  ever  again  to  discover  and
prove  agency  error  and  misconduct.  The  Secretary
has  also  conveniently  defined  her  agency  as  a  non-

Particularly  under  circumstances  like  the  ones
presented  in  these  two  real-world  “case  studies,”

allow  providers,  at  the  very  least,  to  make  an
argument  to  a  court  that  equity  should  excuse  them
from  strict  compliance  with  the  180-day  appeal
deadline,  especially  given  the  Secretary’s  attempt  to
foreclose  all  other  avenues  to  reviewing  mistakes
that  her  agency  manages  to  keep  out  of the  public
eye.

ARGUMENT

I.  CASE  STUDY  1:  BAYSTATE  AND  THE  SSI

FRACTIONS

1.  There  are  any  number  of areas  in which  Medi-
care  reimbursement  follows  transparently  from  the
data  that  the  hospital  itself  either  provides  or  can
verify.
Calculation  of  the  Medicaid  fraction  is  one
example—the  hospital  knows  how  many  patient  days
it  had  in  a  given  year  (the  denominator)  and  can
verify  with  the  various  state  Medicaid  agencies  how
many  of those  days  were  eligible  for  the  DSH  calcu-
lation  (the  numerator).
In  that  type  of  situation,
Southwest  fully  agrees  with  the  Secretary:  Barring
some  truly  exceptional  circumstances,  no  one  should
need  more  than  180  days  from  issuance  of the  NPR
to  figure  out  whether  they  have  been  underpaid.

But  the  Medicare/SSI  fraction  is  another  animal
entirely,  particularly  its  numerator.  As  the  parties
explain,  the  numerator  requires  a  “match”  of part  A-
entitled  days  against  non-public  SSI  records  that  are
maintained  by  the  Social  Security  Administration
and  provided  to  CMS  for  it  alone  to  perform  the
match  and  publish  the  resulting  SSI  fractions—with
no  input  from  the  providers  and,  despite  their  re-

5  Unless  otherwise  indicated,  these  facts  are  drawn  from  the
district  court’s  unappealed  decision  in Baystate,  545  F. Supp.  2d
20,  the  Administrative  Record  (“AR”)  filed  by the  Secretary  in
that  case  (D.D.C.  No.  06-1263),  including  the  Board’s  March  17,
2006  decision  (“Board  Dec.,”  No.  2006-D20,  also  available  on  the
CMS  website)  and  Baystate’s  Statement  of Material  Facts  as  to
which  There  Is  No  Genuine  Dispute,  filed  in  support  of  its
motion  for  summary  judgment  [Dkt.  No.  15].  See  Baystate,  545
F.  Supp.  2d  at  25,  34  n.22  (noting  that  the  Secretary  “largely”
did  not  dispute  the errors  and  omissions  found  by her  Board).

8

quest  for  it,  no  data  given  to  them  to  verify  the
calculation.  See  Resp.  Br.  11-12.

The  Secretary’s  many  references  to  “sophisticated
providers”  and  their  consultants  miss  the  mark  here,
because  no  amount  of  sophistication  and  diligence
would  help  to identify  anything  but  possibly  the  most
large-scale  discrepancies  in  the  agency’s  calculation.
This  is  particularly  so  where,  as  here,  the  “false
negatives”  in  the  match  were  fairly  small  in percent-
age  terms,  though  potentially  very  significant  in
dollar  terms  given  the  amount  of reimbursement  at
stake  for  some  hospitals,  the  huge  size  of the  Medi-
care  program,  and  the  fact  that  no  DSH  whatsoever
is  available  to  hospitals  who  miss  the  qualifying
threshold  by even  a  hundredth  of a  percent.  Moreo-
ver,  the  errors  were  repeated  every  year,  making  it
impossible  to  detect  them  by  looking  for  anomalies
from  one  year  to the  next.

2.  Then  what  explains  Baystate?  In  large  part,
luck.
In  1987,  the  year  after  the  DSH  adjustment
was  put  in  place,  Congress  enacted  a  special  rule
allowing  multi-campus  hospitals  to  have  a  separate
DSH  calculation  performed  for  each  campus.  Apply-
ing  that  rule,  CMS  recalculated  its  prior  “whole
hospital”  SSI  fraction  for  a  hospital  called  St.  Joseph
of  Rhode  Island,  now  performing  a  separate  SSI
calculation  for  each  of  St.  Joseph’s  two  campuses.
But  those  separate  campus  recalculations  did  not
add  up  to  the  agency’s  previous  calculation  for  the
hospital  as  a  whole,  as  they  should  have,  for  the
exact  same  time  period.  See  AR1592.  The  problem
was  inexplicable,  and  it  only  got  worse  with  later
recalculation  attempts.  As  a  result,  neither  campus
qualified  for the  DSH  adjustment.

9

In  1993,  St.  Joseph  hired  Southwest  to  look  into
this  issue.
From  April  1993  through  June  1995,
Southwest’s  David  Pfeil  had  numerous  discussions
with  CMS,  including  particularly  Ann  Tayloe,  who
was  the  key  person  at  CMS  responsible  for  DSH
policy.  Tayloe  eventually  told  Pfeil  that  there  must
be  some  kind  of  problem  with  the  SSI  data.
AR1591.  Southwest  urged  CMS  to  revisit  its  pro-
cesses  and  procedures  for  doing  the  SSI  calculation,
but  made  no  apparent  headway  until  March  1995,
when  Southwest  informed  CMS  that  it  had  advised
St.  Joseph  to  seek  assistance  from  Senator  John
Chafee,  who  had  sponsored  the  separate  campus
DSH  calculation  legislation.  In April,  CMS  made  St.
Joseph  a  “one-time”  offer  that  would  qualify  one  of
its  two  campuses  for  DSH  for  the  1989  fiscal  year,
and  St.  Joseph  took  that  deal,  without  ever  learning
what  the  problem  had  been.

Two  months  later,  in  June  1995,  CMS  published
a  notice  of  proposed  rulemaking,  addressing  the
process  for  hospitals  to  request  that  CMS  recalculate
their  SSI  fraction  for  the  hospitals’  own  fiscal  year,
as  opposed  to  the  federal  fiscal  year.  CMS  warned
that  SSI  fractions  “invariably”  decreased  whenever  a
hospital  requested  such  a  recalculation,  suggesting
that  this  was  due  to problems,  not  with  the  SSI  data,
but  with  hospital  data  concerning  their  numbers  of
Medicare  patient  days.  60  Fed.  Reg.  29,202,  29,223-
24  (June  2, 1995).

That  explanation  did  not  mesh  with  what  CMS
had  told  Southwest  in  the  St.  Joseph  matter,  and  it
was  the  more  curious  when  the  agency’s  fiscal  inter-
mediary  revised  St.  Joseph’s  reimbursement  decision
for  1989  to  implement  the  agency’s  one-time  offer.
That  revision  occurred  in  October  1995  without

10

reconsideration  of  the  data  submitted  by  the  hospi-
tal,  even  though,  four  months  earlier,  CMS  had  put
the  blame  on  hospitals  for  problems  with  recalcula-
tions  of their  SSI  fractions.

This  was  suspicious,  an  apparent  effort  to  put
hospitals  off the  track  of whatever  was  going  on,  and
it  eventually  led  Southwest  to  advise  Baystate  to
Baystate
question  the  agency’s  SSI  calculations.
ultimately  did  that  for  fiscal  years  1993-96.
The
hospital  had  already  timely  appeaied  various  issues
within  180  days  of the  NPRs  for  those  years  (issued
in  1995-99,  respectively),  but,  importantly,  not  the
SSI  calculation  issue.  Baystate  added  this  issue  to
its  appeals  for  all  years  much  later,  in  November
1999  (four  years  after  the  fiscal  1993  NPR  issued  in
1995),  under  a  regulation  which  at  the  time  permit-
ted  the  addition  of new  issues  up  until  the  hearing
date  so  long  as  an  appeal  on  some  issues  had  been
filed  within  180  days  (42  C.F.R.  405.1841(a)(1)).
This  liberty  was  sharply  restricted  after  Baystate,  as
discussed  more  fully  in  part  7 below.  At  that  time,
moreover,  there  was  nothing  about  the  agency's
published  SSI  percentages  for  Baystate  that  suggest-
ed  any  underpayment.  Baystate  was  just  taking  an
educated  guess  on  the  basis  of  a  tip—informal,
unspecific,  and  non-public  remarks  by  agency  per-
sonnel  in another  case  which  settled  before  the  facts
could  be developed.

3.  The  story  of what  had  happened  within  CMS
would  only  emerge  after  ten  days  of testimony  before
the  agency’s  Board  in 2003  and  2004,  mainly  through
cross-examination  of  the  employees  responsible  for
DSH  calculation  and  policy  at  CMS,  but  also  through
of
retired  SSA  employees  and  representatives
Southwest.

11

As  it  turns  out,  CMS  had  known  for  a  long  time
that  there  was  a  data  problem  with  its  calculation,
and  that  it had  nothing  to  do with  the  hospitals.  In a
1995  deposition  transcript  that  surfaced  during  the
Baystate  proceeding  years  later,  the  agency’s  desig-
nated  witness,  Tayloe,  revealed  that  CMS  got  ques-
tions  in  1991  from  some  California  hospitals  that
thought  their  SSI  fractions  seemed  low,  but  did  not
have  the  necessary  understanding  of  the  agency’s
calculation  to  raise  any  specific  concerns.  The  ques-
tions  nonetheless  prompted  agency  personne!  to  visit
SSA  to  see  whether  that  agency’s  SSI  data  was
Tayloe  testified  that,  at  the
accurate.
time,  she  saw  no  problem  outside  “the  mind  of  the
people  inquiring,”  told  the  hospitals  as  much,  and
recalls  no  “further  problems.”  AR958-59.  Years  later,
however,  one  of  her  colleagues  would  testify  in
Baystate  that  CMS  knew  it  had  a  problem  with  the
numbers  even  before  Southwest  called  about  St.
Joseph  in  1993.  Board  Dec.  24  & n.120.

AR943-59.

Thus,  the  agency  had  been  concerned  enough  in
1991  to  check  on  the  data  it was  receiving  from  SSA,
and,  in  April  1995,  had  settled  St.  Joseph  rather
than  risk  a  Senator's  questions  about  why  patient
days  kept  dropping  out  of the  SSI  fraction  numera-
tor.
But  when  asked  at  deposition,  the  agency
(through  Tayloe)  testified  that  the  SSI  data  was
“verified  and  correct,”  Board  Dec.  33,  and  produced
no  documents—not  even  the  recent  settlement  with
St.  Joseph—to  comply  with  a  Board  order  directing
CMS  to  produce  records  relating  to  the  accuracy,
validity,  and  potential  for  error  in  its  processes  for
determining  hospitals’  SSI  percentages.  Jd.  Instead,
as  mentioned,  CMS  issued  a  rulemaking  notice
blaming  hospital  data  and  then,  when  finalizing  its

12

rule  in  September  1995,  told  the  public  that  the  SSI
fraction  calculation  was  performed  by SSA,  not  CMS,
and  that  CMS  did  not  even  have  the  SSI  data.  60
Fed.  Reg.  45,778,  45,811-12  (Sept.  1, 1995).

None  of that  was  true.  CMS  had  always  done  the
“match,”  and  the  SSI  tapes  used  to  do  it were  stored
under  the  desk  of an  agency  programmer,  as  reflect-
ed  in  notes  of an  internal  agency  meeting  on  Febru-
ary  15,  1996.  The  same  notes  confirm  CMS’s  under-
standing  that  the  SSI  tapes  provided  by SSA  for  the
match  “has  always  left  off dead  people.”  AR2113.  In
particular,
as  explained  more  fully  during  the
Baystate  proceedings,  a  patient  who  was  alive  and
receiving  SSI  during  his  hospital  stay  during  a  given
fiscal  year  might  not  be  reflected  as  such  in  the  SSI
tape  used  for  CMS’s  match  because  that  tape  is
generated  well  after  the  close  of the  fiscal  year,  and
would  be routinely  purged  of records  for patients  who
had  died  during  their  hospital  stay  or  between  their
date  of discharge  and  the  date  when  SSA  supplied  its
tapes  to  CMS.

Because  of  that  peculiarity,  the  patient  would
not  produce  a  part  A/SSI  match  for  the  SSI  fraction
numerator,  even  though  he  was  entitled  to  both
during  his  hospital  stay  and,  naturally,  the  problem
would  only  get  worse  (i.e.,  more  false  negatives)  as
time  went  on,  more  people  died,  and  more  “stale”
records  were  routinely  purged.  This,  finally,  was  an
explanation  for  St.  Joseph:  With  every  new  recalcu-
lation  of a  given  year,  more  people  left  the  current

6  There  was  nothing  inherently  “wrong”  with  SSA’s  data.
CMS  simply  failed  to  appreciate  the  substantial  limitations  of
that  data  for  CMS’s  purposes  in  performing  the  SSI/part  A
match.

13

SSI  rolls,  producing  fewer  and  fewer  matches  for  the
prior year  at issue.’

Initially,  and  to  its  credit,  CMS  set  out  to  resolve
the  problem  by requesting  corrected  tapes  from  SSA
for  prior  years  and  then  using  them  to  run  what  was
called  a  “Special  MEDPAR  Run  for  SSI  Corrections,”
which  would  compare  the  number  of SSI/part  A days
previously  calculated  against  the  number  of matches
that  should  have  been  counted  had  the  corrected
tapes  been  used.  If the  discrepancy  was  “material,”
the  intent  at  that  time  was  to  recalculate  prior  SSI
ratios  accordingly.  AR5254-55.

SSA  provided  the  tapes  promptly,  and  CMS  had
new  match  results  ready  by the  May/June  timeframe
in  1996.  At  that  time,  a  summary  prepared  by  the
agency  showed  that  a  significant  number  of Medicare
hospital  stays  that  previously  had  no  SSI  days
associated  with  them  now,  upon  recalculation,  did
have  associated  SSI  days.  The  Board  credited
Southwest’s  estimate  that,  using this  internal  agency
summary,  and  based  on  average  figures  for  the

7  Although  it  does  not  acknowledge  that  CMS  had  already
known  about  the  problem  for  years,  Baystate,  545  F.  Supp.  2d
at  27,  this  internal  agency  email  (AR2111)  succinctly  describes
the  issue:

RECALCULATION  OF  THE  SSI  PERCENTAGE  FOR
REQUESTING  HOSPITALS  PRODUCED  RESULTS
NOT  CONSIDERED  LOGICAL.  AFTER  ANALYSIS
AND  DISCUSSIONS  WITH  THE  SSA  PROGRAMMER
(PAT  CRIBBS),  THE  PROBLEM  WAS  IDENTIFIED.
AND
BENEFICIARIES
IDENTIFIED  AS  ‘stale’  (ie.  inactive  after  about  a  year)
ARE  NOT  INCLUDED  IN  THE  DATA  THAT  WE
RECEIVE  FROM  SSA.

THAT

HAVE

DIED

14

length  of patient  stays  and  the  dollar  value  per  SSI
day,  the  aggregate  impact  on  ali  hospitals  could  be
about  $4 billion  for  fiscal  years  1989  through  1996.

In sum,  CMS  confirmed  for  itself  that  it had  been
significantly  understating  the  SSI  fraction  numera-
tor,  thus  underpaying  DSH  hospitals,  and  appeared
ready  to  correct  the  problem.  But  then,  for  reasons
CMS  witnesses  claimed  not  to  recall,  CMS  staff
received  an  unspecified  “different  direction”  from  the
agencys  “Policy”  personnel,  t.e.,  Tayloe  and  her
group.  Board  Dec.  34-35;  AR  5254.
No  corrections
were  made.  The  corrected  SSI  tapes  and  the  results
of the  CMS  recalculation  still  existed  as  late  as  April
1997  (according  to  another  email  produced  in
Baystate),  and  they  would  have  allowed  every  hospi-
tal  to  identify  each  patient  stay  and  the  precise
number  of  days  that  were  incorrectly  omitted  from
CMS’s  prior  calculations  because  of the  omission  of
“stale”  SSI  records.  But  those  key  documents  disap-
peared  sometime  later—including  all  the  nrior  year
tapes  that  a  CMS  programmer  had  been  keeping
under  his  desk—leaving  just  the  summary  document
described  above.

No  one  told  the  hospitals  any  of  this,  nor  disa-
bused  them  of  the  agency’s  false  assertions  that  it
did  not  have  the  SSI  records,  that  it was  not  respon-
sible  for  the  SSI  calculation,  and  that  the  SSI  data
was  “verified  and  correct.”  Instead,  CMS  left  stand-
ing  the  misimpression  that  any  problems  with  sub-
sequent  recalculations  were  the  hospitals’  fault.

4.  “Stale”  records  were  by  no  means  the  only
problem.
In  1986,  for  example,  the  Secretary  in-
formed  providers,  in  the  Federal  Register,  that  the
agency  would  be  using  Social  Security  numbers  to

15

perform  the  match  of  SSA’s  records  against  CMS’s
Medicare  part  A records,  recognizing  that  this  widely
used  identifier  is unique  to  each  patient.  In  fact,  the
agency  never  did  any  such  thing,  instead  attempting
to  match  something  called  a  “HICAN”  (“Health
Insurance  Claim  Account  Number”)  against  SSA’s
“Title  IT’  numbers,  which  are  used,  not  for  SSI,  but
to  track  benefits  under  the  Social  Security  retire-
ment  program.  That  produced  data  mismatches  that
should  not  have  occurred,  and  would  not  have  oc-
curred  had  CMS  used  SSNs  as  it  led  everyone  to
believe  it was.  The  error  must  have  dawned  on  the
agency  during  the  Board  proceedings,  because  a  year
after  he  first  testified  that  he  had  never  used  SSNs
in  the  match,  an  agency  witness  tried  to  recant,
telling  a  skeptical  Board  that  CMS  in  fact  did  use
SSNs  as  part  of the  match.

This  systemic  error  and  several  others  are  de-
tailed  in  the  district  court’s  Baystate  decision.  What
the  district  court  had  no  occasion  to  address,  howev-
er—and  what  may  prove  relevant  to  the  equitable
tolling  question—is  how  those  errors  happened.
According  to  the  Congressional  Budget  Offfice’s
“March  2012  Medicare  Baseline”  (available  on  the
CBO  website),  DSH  now  represents  an  $11  billion
annual  payment  adjustment  for all hospitals.  Yet,  as
the  Board  found,  the  CMS’s  match  process  was
devised  “on  the  fly,”  based  on  a  few  phone  calls  and
letters  between  CMS  and  SSA,  without  the  testing  or
validation  that  usually  accompanies  a process  of this

8  CMS  implemented  a  change  in  1996  that  marginally
improved  the  match  by  utilizing  additional  HICANs,  but  that
change  was  made  prospectively  —  no  prior  years  were  corrected,
and  providers  were  not  told  about  the  change  or  the  reasons  for
1t.

16

importance  and  potential  complexity,  and  without
any  significant  reassessment  as  the  years  passed.
AR5054.

In  sharp  contrast,  when  the  same  two  agencies
got  together  to  perform  a  very  similar  match  to
determine  how  much  cash  assistance  SSA  would  pay
out  to  SSI  recipients  who  are  in nursing  homes,  there
were  detailed  discussions  about  the  functional  re-
quirements  of  the  match,  there  was  testing  and
validation,  and  there  was  a  determination  to  do  the
match  using  the  individuals’  own  unique  SSNs.
Notably,  and  unlike  DSH,  more  matches  in  this
nursing  home  context  actually  reduces  the  govern-
ment’s  expenditures,  so  there  was  every  incentive  to
get  it  right.  But  with  no  one  able  to  see  inside  its
“black  box,”  and  no  one  but  providers  to  bear  the
impact  of  an  undercounting  mistake  in  the  SSI
fraction  numerator,  CMS  took  a  comparatively
slapdash  approach,  and  stayed  with  it  even  after
problems  came  to the  agency’s  notice.

5.  Making  matters  worse,  CMS  went  to  disturb-
ing  lengths  in  Baystate  to  conceal  the  impact  of  all
these  mistakes.
The  Board  issued  subpoenas  in
2000  that  required  CMS  to  produce  specific  data
elements  relating  to  each  patient  and  patient  day
that  was  included  in  the  SSI  fractions  for  the  fiscal
years  at  issue.  CMS  did  not  comply,  so  Baystate
brought  a  civil  action  in  federal  district  court  to
compel  a  response  for  the  1995  fiscal  year.
The
Secretary  successfully  moved  to  dismiss  in  2001,
submitting  a  declaration  from  a  CMS  employee  to
show  that,  after  an  “exhaustive  search,”  the  request-
ed data  could  not  be found.  AR688.

17

About  two  years  after  that  declaration,  though,  in
a  2003  hearing  before  the  Board,  another  agency
employee  revealed  that  the  SSI  and  Medicare  days
data  that  CMS  had  used  to  compute  the  SSI  fraction
for  the  hospital’s  1995  fiscal  year  were  still  in  CMS’s
hands  when  the  agency  previously  had  declared
otherwise  under  penalty  of perjury.  Not  long  before
this  revised  testimony,  the  agency’s  counsel  sent  the
witness  an  email,  attaching  a  list  of  files  he  had
located—responsive  to  the  Board’s  subpoena—and
asking  whether  the  agency’s  prior  federal  court
declaration  was  “literal/l]/y  true  (I hope)  in light  of the
files  listed  below.”  AR1631  (emphasis  added).

CMS  ultimately  produced  this  data,  but  not  be-
fore  issuing  an  extraordinary  warning  to  the  Board
that  it  “should  proceed  very  cautiously  with  respect
to  any  further  discovery  orders  in  this  case.”
AR5791.
The  Board  Chair  responded  by  making
clear  that  her  Board  had  a  responsibility  to  find  the
truth:
“[{I]n  analyzing  your  client’s  caution  that  we
tread  very  carefully  here  on  other  discovery  orders
from  what  I’ve  seen  it’s  very  clear  that  we  just  need
to  get  to  the  bottom  of  this  and  get  the  facts  out,”
adding  that  “it  became  very  clear  after  a  lengthy
examination”  that  “[y]ou  had  [responsive  records]  all
along.  It was  right  there.”  AR5799-5801,  5803.

By this  time,  the  Board  was  deeply  enmeshed  in
an  unsuccessful  effort  to  get  its  own  agency  to  en-
force  the  Board’s  subpoenas.
In  2002,  the  Board
issued  a  subpoena  directing  SSA  to  produce  its  SSI
records  for  a  sample  of Medicare  beneficiaries  treat-
ed  by  Baystate  in  the  relevant  years.  When  SSA
refused  on  privacy  grounds,  the  Board  asked  the
Secretary  to  enforce  the  subpoena  in  court  pursuant
to  42  U.S.C.  § 205(e).  The  Secretary  refused.

18

Baystate  then  went  to  its  patients  directly,  and
for  a  small  population  of  still-living  Medicare  pa-
tients  who  consented  to  release  of their  SSI  records,
was  able  to  confirm  that  some  of these  patients  had
not  had  their  inpatient  stays  counted  as  SSI  days  in
the  CMS  match.  Armed  with  that  proof,  Baystate
went  back  to  the  Board,  which  issued  another  sub-
poena  in June  2003,  directing  SSA  to produce  its  SSI
records  for  all  the  hospital's  Medicare  patients  in the
years  at  issue,  which  the  Board  later  said  was  “fun-
damental  data  that’s  needed  to  get  the  answers  in
this  case.”
AR2617-18.  When  SSA  furnished  only
part  of that  data  (for deceased  persons  who  had  been
patients  in  one  of  the  years  requested),  the  Board
wrote  directly  to  the  Secretary,  asking  for  assistance
in  getting  the  necessary  information  from  SSA.  See
42  U.S.C.  139500(i)  (requiring  Secretary  to  “make
available  to  the  Board  such  ***  assistance  as  the
Board  may  require  to  carry  out  its  functions”).

When  that  produced  no  results,  either,  in  2004,
the  Board  made  a  forma!  request  to  HHS  counsel  for
enforcement  of its  subpoenas.  “There  is no  authority
in  the  statute  or  regulations,”  the  Board  wrote,  “for
the  CMS  Administrator  to  block  evidence  from  being
sought  or  presented  that  the  Board  has  determined
is  essential  to  a  matter  in  dispute.”  AR6705.  The
CMS  Deputy  Administrator  nonetheless  refused  to
direct  the  agency's  Office  of  General  Counsel  to
enforce  the  Board’s  subpoenas.

While  all  this  was  going  on,  Baystate  was  looking
for  creative  solutions.  The  hospital  proposed  to  have
SSA  release  its  data  to  a CMS  third-party  contractor
to  verify—at  Baystate’s  expense—which  of  the
hospital’s  Medicare  patients  were  entitled  to  SSI
benefits  during  their  inpatient  stay.  SSA  ultimately

19

agreed  to  a  version  of this  proposal,  but  CMS  did  not,
telling  Baystate  that  its  Office  of  Information  Ser-
vices  (headed  by  the  employee  who  had  signed  the
agency's  declaration  described  above),  was  not  will-
ing  to  participate  in  Baystate’s  proposed  alternative
data  match.

6.  Ultimately,  Baystate  was  able  to  prove  to  the
Board  that  the  agency’s  calculation  of the  SSI  frac-
tion  was  deeply  flawed  in  several  key  respects  that
tended  to  deflate  the  fraction  and  underpay  DSH
hospitals,  potentially  by  billions  of  dollars.
But  it
took  years  of what  amounted  to  trench  warfare,  at  a
cost  to  Southwest  of  $2.5  million  by  the  time  the
Board  issued  its  decision  in  2006  (and  with  further
review  proceedings  at  the  agency  and  in court  still  to
come,  bringing  the  total  outlay  to  $3.9  million).
Along  the  way,  the  hospital  had  the  good  fortune  to
find  a  former  career  SSA  employee  who  was  willing
to  speak  openly  about  her  interactions  with  CMS  and
the  known  limitations  in  the  SSI  records  her  agency
provided;  to  have  access  to  witnesses  from  Southwest
who  just  happened  to  be  in  a  position  to  glimpse  the
agency's  mistakes  years  earlier;  and  an  agency  Board
that  was  exceptionally  persistent  in  demanding  the
truth  from  a  recalcitrant  agency  and  its witnesses.®

Virtually  no  providers,  regardless  of  size  or  so-
phistication,  would  have  had  the  resources  and  good

his  testimony  from  one  evidentiary  hearing  to  the  next,  first
testifying  that  he  never  used  Social  Security  numbers  for  the

20

luck  necessary  to  reach  the  truth  of  this  situation
even  if they  were  given  a  decade  to  do  it,  let  alone  to
take  action  within  180  days  after  the  reimbursement
determinations  were  issued  for  all  the  fiscal  years
that  could  be  affected  by these  errors.  The  Board’s
2006  decision  in Baystate  really  was  the  earliest  clue
to  the  regulated  community  that  they  might  have
been  underpaid  as  a  result  of flaws  in CMS’s  calcula-
tion  of the  SSI  fraction.

Even  then,  big  questions  remained,  largely  be-
cause  the  agency’s  many  roadblocks  and  conceal-
ments  in  Baystate  had  succeeded  in  creating  an
evidentiary  challenge  that  the  Secretary  made  every
effort  to  exploit—providers’  inability  to  show  the
dollar  impact  of errors  that  the  agency  largely  con-
ceded.  In  the  Baystate  district  court  case,  for  exam-
ple,  in 2008,  the  Secretary’s  counsel  initially  met  the
court’s  questions  on  that  point  by  saying  “[w]ell,  it
depends  what  you  mean  by billions  of dollars.”  Tr.  62
[Dkt.  68].!°

Fortunately,  Baystate  had  gotten  enough  of  a
peek  behind  the  agency’s  curtain  to  make  some
educated  guesses  about  impact  and  persuade  the
court  that  “the  systemic  monetary  impacts  of  the
agency’s  errors  are  potentially  enormous,”  545  F.
Supp.  2d  at  54  n.35,  and  eventually  learned  enough
through  post-judgment  discussions  with  the  agency
to  understand  the  extent  to  which  the  hospital  had
been  underpaid  in  the  years  at  issue.  But  Baystate

10  The  agency's  key  policy  employee  similarly  testified  before

hundred  million  dollars”  would  “[njot  necessarily”  have  left  a
lasting  impression  on  her,  “given  the  amount  of  money
involved.”  Board  Dec.  35.

21

only  required  the  agency  to  correct  that  hospital’s
SSI  percentages  for  the  years  at  issue  in  that  law-
suit.
It would  have  been  impossible  for  most  hospi-
tals  to  similarly  quantify  the  impact  of this  issue  on
them  and,  indeed,
to  the  best  of  Southwest’s
knowledge,  no  hospital  but  Baystate  with  a  pending

nished  the  details  necessary  for  them  to  determine
whether  and  to  what  extent  they  were  underpaid.
This  despite  the  fact  that  the  Secretary  successfully
objected  to  district  court  supervision  of the  remand
in  Baystate  on  grounds  that  her  agency  well  under-
stood  what  needed  to  be  done  to  fix  the  match  pro-
cess.  Def.  Partial  Opp.  to  Pl.  Mot.  to Amend  Opinion
and  Order,  at  4-8  [Dkt.  44].

In  fact,  it  took  CMS  until  2010  to  announce  its
“fixes”  for the  errors  identified  in Baystate,'!  plus  two
more  years  to  recalculate  the  SSI  fractions  it  had
already  issued  for  2006  and  2007  and  issue  first-time
calculations  for  2008  through  2010.  This  leaves  prior
years  unaddressed  and,  even  as  to  the  later  years
that  were  addressed,  leaves  hospitals  still  in the  dark
about  whether  the  numbers  are  right.  That  is  be-
cause  the  agency  released  none  of  the  underlying
data  necessary  to  verify  the  percentages  and  has
confused  the  issue  still  further  by not  separating  the
Baystate-related  impact  from  the  impact  of  other
controversial  changes  the  agency  implemented  at  the
same  time.!?

exactly  the  ways  identified  in  that  case.
Baystate  had  the  time  it  needed  to  identify  the
potential  underpayment  and  properly  include  it  as

changes  to make  sure  it would  never  happen  again.

8. Here  then,  is a  situation  where  CMS:  concealed
a  truly  massive  set  of  mistakes;  made  affirmative
misstatements  (including  under  penalty  of  perjury)
that  put  providers,  the  Board,  and  the  courts  off the
track  about  what  happened;  still,  to  this  day,  has  not
fully  disclosed  the  payment  impact  to  hospitals;  and
has  taken  active  steps  to put  the  truth  out  of reach  in
Given  those  facts,  it  takes  a  certain
future  cases.
amount  of temerity  to  suggest,  as  the  Secretary  does,
that  there  is  no  basis  for  any  court  in  any  case  ever
to  consider  equitable  extension  of the  180-day  appeal
period,  and  more,  to  tout  the  fact  that  neither  the
Board  nor  the  district  court  in  Baystate  made  a
finding  that  the  agency  committed  the  sort  of willful
misconduct  that  often  justifies  equitable  tolling  in
other  contexts.

The  reality  is  that,  to  prevail,  Baystate  only  had
to  show  that  the  agency  acted  arbitrarily  and  capri-
ciously  by  failing  to  use  the  best  available  data  to

perform  the  relevant  calculations.
necessary  to  go  further  and  prove  fraud  or  similar

submits  that  this  record  would  have  been  sufficient
to  do  it.
That  thought  evidently  occurred  to  the
Board  Chair,  who  commented  as  follows  on  the
agency’s  potential  culpability  (AR5800-01):

As  I say,  I'm  not  ***  trying  to  form  an  opinion
on  whether  or  not  anything  was  done  by  the
client,  CMS,  to  willfully  keep  information  out
of the  hands  of the  Provider  and  the  Board  or
whether  it  was  purely  just  a  lack  of  under-
standing  of  who  was  doing  what,  but  I  do
know  that  the  government  has  been  very  keen
in  at  least  the  last  decade  with  regard  to  the
Federal  False  Claims  Act  ****  And  as I recall,
one  of the  standards  of culpability  in the  False
Claims  Act  is  willful  ignorance.  And  I think
that’s  very  reasonable  and  it  should  be  im-
posed,  but  I also  think  that  we  tofo,]  all  of us
that  deal  with  federal  health  care  funds
should  be  held  to  the  same  standard.  None  of
us  should  be excluded  from  that  standard{.]

Moreover,  if there  has  been  no  fraud  finding  (yet),
one  can  also  blame  the  Secretary’s  self-serving
interpretation  of  a  regulation,  formerly  42  C.F.R.
405.1885(d),  that  was  promulgated  to  extend  the
three-year  limit  for  reopening  reimbursement  deter-
minations  if they  were  “procured  by fraud  or  similar
fault  of any  party  to  the  determination.”  The  Secre-
tary  now  claims  that  her  agency  is  not  a  “party”
(echoing  the  post-Baystate  changes  to  her  agency's

25

hearing  rules)  and  that,  therefore,  only  fraud  by  the
provider  will  excuse  late  reopening.  Pet.  Br.  7  n.5.
As  Auburn  points  out,  Resp.  Br.  10  n.4,  50  n.20,  the
agency  said  otherwise  when  the  rule  was  first  issued.
(The  district  court  in  this  case  refused  to  credit  the
Secretary's  revised  view.  Petn.  46a  n.15.)

Having  tried  to  make  it  impossible  for  providers
to  hold  her  agency  accountable  even  for  fraud,  it  is
awkward  to  say  the  least  for  the  Secretary  to  try  to
foreclose  equitable  tolling  by arguing  that  no  one  yet
has  found  that  the  agency  engaged  in  the  type  of
intentional  dishonesty,  deliberate  indifference,  or
willful  ignorance  that  most  of us  would  call  “fraud  or
sunilar  fault.”

II.  CASE  STUDY  2:

CAPE  COD  AND  THE

“RURAL  FLOOR”!5

1.  Most  Medicare  hospitals  are  paid  based  on  a
prospectively  determined  rate  per  discharge,  which
starts  with  a  “standardized  amount”  that  is  periodi-
cally  adjusted  for  inflation  and  other  factors  and
then  for  the  most  part  carried  forward  from  year  to
year  as  adjusied,  rather  than  recalculated  from
scratch.
that  standardized
amount  is  multiplied  by other  factors  that  are  calcu-
lated  anew  each  year,  including  the  “wage  index,”
which  reflects  the  difference  between  the  average
wage  levels  faced  by each  hospital  in  its  local  labor
market  versus  the  national  average  of  hospital
wages.

For  each  hospital,

13  Unless  otherwise  indicated,  these  facts  are  drawn  from  the
D.C.  Circuit’s  opinion  in  Cape  Cod,  630  F.3d  203,  and  the
Administrative  Record  (“AR”)  and  2007  and  2008  rulemaking
records  (“RO7”  and  “RO8”)  filed  by the  Secretary  in  that  case  at
the  district  court  level  (D.D.C  No.  08-1751).

26

In  1997,  Congress  determined  that  the  wage  index
was  being  applied  in  a  manner  that  resulted  in some
urban  hospitals  being  paid  less  than  the  average
rural  hospital  in  their  state,  and  set  out  to  correct
that  anomaly  by enacting  what  is  known  as  a  “rural
floor”——requiring  that  the  wage  index  applied  to  an
urban  hospital  be  at  least  as  great  as  the  wage  index
assigned  to rural  hospitals  in the  same  state.

Because  this  adjustment  could  tend  to  increase
overall  Medicare  payments,  however  (by  requiring
upward  adjustments  to  the  wage  indices  for  urban
hospitals  not  otherwise  offset  by  decreases  else-
where),  Congress  also  required  that  the  rural  floor  be
made  budget-neutral.  That  is,  to  the  extent  that  the
rural  floor  would  increase  aggregate  Medicare  pay-
ments  as  it  altered  the  distribution  of  Medicare
payments  between  urban  and  rural  hospitals,  those
payments  would  have  to  be  adjusted  downward  to
offset  that  impact—to  make  sure  Medicare  paid  no
more  in  the  aggregate  to  all  hospitals  nationwide
than  it  would  have  without  the  rural  floor.
See
Balanced  Budget  Act  of  1997,  Pub.  L.  No.  105-33,  §
4410,  42  U.S.C.  1395ww  note.  Rural  floor  is only  one
of  several  adjustments  that  require  the  agency  to
make  a  further  adjustment  to  assure  budget  neutral-
ity.

2.  For  years,  the  Secretary  said  in  the  Federal
Register  that  she  performed  the  budget  neutrality
adjustment  in  a  “cumulative”  manner.  This  meant
that  the  aggregate  payments  resulting  from  applying
the  agency's  published,  rural-floor  adjusted  wage
indices  in one  year  would  be compared  to  the  project-
ed  aggregate  payments  resulting  from  the  rural-
floor-adjusted  wage  indices  for  the  current  year,  and
the  budget  neutrality  adjustment  would  be  applied

27

for  the  current  year  only  to  the  extent  necessary  to
address  any  incremental  increase
in  aggregate
payments  versus  the  prior  year  that  were  attributa-
ble  to the  rural  floor.

This  incremental  increase,  if any,  would  be  re-
moved  from  the  standardized  amount  to  accomplish
the  budget  neutrality  adjustment,  and  the  standard-
ized  amount,  thus  adjusted,  would  carry  forward  to
the  next  year  for  possible  further  incremental  ad-
CMS’s  Federal  Register  notices  that
justment.
accompanied  publication  of  its  wage  indices  each
year  were  not  specific  about  the  data  variables  the
agency  used  in  its  calculation,  but  they  did  state
explicitly  that  “budget  neutrality  adjustment  factors
are  applied  to  the  standardized  amounts  without
removing  the  effects  of the  [prior  year]  budget  neu-
trality  adjustments.”  See,  e.g.,  71  Fed.  Reg.  47,870,
48,147  (Aug.  18,  2006).

In  2006,  however,  a  hospital  consultant  named
Theodore  Giovanis  sent  an  email  to  the  Deputy
Director  of  CMS’s  Division  of Acute  Care,  asking  a
number  of  technical  questions  about  the  agency’s
fiscal  2007  budget  neutrality  calculations  that  were
not  directed  to  the  rural  floor  per  se.  The  Deputy
Director  referred  the  questions  to  a  staff  member,
who  told  Giovanis  by email  that  the  agency’s  practice
was  to  compare  projected  aggregate  payments  in  the
current  year  with  the  rural  floor  applied  against  the
prior  year  aggregate  payments  without  any  rural
floor  adjustment,  and  then  use  that  difference  to
reduce  the  current  year’s  standardized  amount  for
budget  neutrality  purposes.  AR58-60.

This  was  180  degrees  the  opposite  of  what  the
Secretary  had  always  told  the  regulated  community.

a |

28

Instead  of adjusting  each  year  for  incremental  gains
attributable  to  the  rural  floor  in  that  year  only,
recognizing  that  all  prior  year  increments  had  al-
ready  been  captured  in  the  standardized  amount
that  the  agency  started  with  each  year,  CMS  actually
had  been  taking  the  combined  budget  impact  of the
rural  floor  for  all  years  out  of  the  standardized
amount  each  year—which  would  mean,  for  example,
that  the  rural  floor  budget  neutrality  adjustment  for
fiscal  2006  would  reflect  a  fiscal  1998  incremental
adjustment  that  was  taken  nine  times,  a  1999  ad-
justment  taken  eight  times,  and  so  on.'4

Giovanis  responded  by email  the  same  day,  ask-
ing  “[W]hy  doesn’t  this  [method]  duplicate  in  some
respect  the  carve  out  for  the  application  of  the
The  agency  went  silent.  Giovanis
floor{?]”  AR59.
then  prepared  timely  formal]  comments  on  this  issue
for consideration  by the  agency  as  it was  formulating
its  rules  for  fiscal  2007.  The  comments  made  very
clear  his  concern  that  the  agency  had  not  used  the
so-called  “cumulative”  approach  it  had  advertised,
but  instead  an  illogical  formula  that  had  a  duplica-
tive  and  compounding  impact  on  the  standardized
amount,  eroding  it each  year  without  any  regard  to
what  extent,  or  even  whether,  there  was a rural  floor
impact  anticipated  for  the  coming  year.  See  Giovanis
Decl.  (Pl.  Mem.  in  Opp.  to  Def.  Cross-Motion  for
Summ.  J., Ex.  2 [Dkt.  30]).

14  “Specifically,  the  hospitals  argue  that  CMS  has  duplicated
prior  adjustments  by each  year  calculating  the  full  amount  of
the  adjustment  necessary  to  counteract  the  effect  of the  rural
floor  and  then  applying  that  adjustment  to  a  figure  that  incudes
adjustments  carried  over  from  previous  years.”  Cape  Cod,  630
F.3d  at  207.

29

Indeed,  because  the  full  running  total  of all  prior
budget  neutrality  adjustment  was  coming  out  of the
standardized  amount  anew  every  year,  without  prior
year  reductions  being  put  back  into  the  standardized
amount  first  to  clean  the  slate,  the  standardized
amount  inevitably  would  decline  every  year  even  if
there  were  no  year-on-year  changes  in the  rural  floor
impact.  And  without  the  clue  provided  by the  agen-
cy’s  email,  there  would  have  been  no  way  to  tell  that
the  Secretary  was  doing  anything  other  than  what
she  said  she  was  doing,  let  alone  that  hospitals
might  be  underpaid  as  a  result.

Giovanis  hand-delivered  his  comments,  which
were  clearly  marked  as  such,  to  an  agency  employee
that  he  knew  and  had  delivered  materials  to  before.
She,  too,  worked  in  the  Division  of  Acute  Care,
reporting  to  the  Deputy  Director  to  whom  Giovanis
had  sent  his  first  email.
Giovanis  gave  her  three
copies,  one  of  them  for  the  rulemaking  record  and
one  each,  separately  addressed,  to  the  Deputy  Direc-
tor  and  the  employee  who  had  answered  Giovanis’
email.  He  would  later  swear  out  a  declaration  re-
counting  all these  facts.

But  the  agency’s  2007  rules  were  issued  without
any  acknowledgement  of  these  comments,  and  just
stated  per  usual  that  CMS  would  use  the  “same
methodology”  as  in  past  years,  then  provided  the
same  inaccurate  explanation  of that  methodology  as
it  had  in  those  years.
71  Fed.  Reg  at  48,147.  Ad-
vised  by Giovanis,  a  number  of hospitals  brought  suit
challenging  the  agency’s  rules  for  2007  and  2008.
For  the  2008  rulemaking,  the  Secretary  acknowl-
edged  receiving  comments  directed  to  the  “inappro-
priate  duplicating  effect”  of her  calculation,  but  she

pened  and  neither  admitted  nor  denied  that  the  prior

or  commenters’

In  the  Cape  Cod  litigation,  which  was  eventually

formal  comments.
The  Secretary  argued  that  the
absence  of Giovanis’s  comments  indicated  that  they
were  not  submitted  or  were  not  submitted  properly,

number  it  had  set  up  to  arrange  hand-delivery  of
comments  to  agency  staff at  CMS’s  Baltimore  office.

The  agency  then  submitted  a  declaration  from

had  understood  it contained  public  comments  or  if it
required  her  to  accept  anything  on  behalf  of the  two
other  employees  that  Giovanis  copied  on  his  com-
Cerne  Decl.  (Reply  Mem.  in  Supp.  of  Def.
ments.
Cross-Motion  for  S.J.,  Ex.  1 [Dkt.  33]).  This  despite

Letter  to  [CMS]  on  the  Proposed  FY  2007  IPPS

Moreover,  as  the  hospitals  showed,  the  2007
rulemaking  record  did  contain  several  other  com-
ments  that  were  either  late  or  misaddressed  and,
most  importantly,  a  partial  copy  of Giovanis’s  email
exchange  eventually  showed  up  in  the  record  for  the
next  rulemaking  year,  2008.  RO08:492-93.  The  agen-
cy  neither  admitted  nor  denied  receiving  the  email  or
the  comments,  and  furnished  no  explanation  for  how
the  emails  could  go  missing  in  2007  but  wind  up  in
the  2008  record,  except  to  say  that  they  “did  play  a
role”  in  the  change  the  agency  made  for  2008.  Def.
Mem.  in  Support  of Cross-Motion  for  S.J.  at  13  n.7
{[Dkt.  26].

3.  The  D.C.  Circuit  invalidated  the  2007  as  well
as  2008  rules,  rejecting  the  Secretary's  argument
that  the  hospitals  had  waived  their  2007  challenge
when  Giovanis  failed  to  call  the  designated  phone
number.  Ultimately,  the  errors  he  identified  result-
ed  in  a  reported  multi-billion  dollar  settlement  for
thousands  of hospitals  for  fiscal  years  going  back  to
1999.
Chad  Terhune,  Medicare  to  Settle  Hospital
Reimbursement  Dispute,  Los  Angeles  Times  (Apr.  12,
2012).

Here  again,  though,  as  with  Baystate,  it  is  not
credible  for  the  Secretary  to  make  blithe  assertions
about  a  properly  functioning  system  of administra-
tive  and  judicial  review,  triggered  by  sophisticated,
well-advised  providers  who  had  the  time  necessary  to
determine  that  they  had  been  underpaid  and  act
Instead,  as  in  Baystate,  the  agency  in
accordingly.
Cape  Cod  did  an  important  calculation  wrong  from
the  beginning,  but  was  insulated  for  years  from  any
In  both  cases,  the  truth  only  came  out
challenge.
later,  and  then  only  because  of  a  rare,  fortuitous
lapse  by  someone  at  the  agency,  revealing  a  bit  too

32

much  to  someone  savvy  enough  to  suspect  the  possi-
ble  implications  and,  in  that  way,  giving  some  pro-
viders  the  clue  they  needed  to  pursue  the  issue
further—that  is,  if they  proved  to  have  the  resources
and  persistence  to  litigate  their  way  through  the
agency's  wall  of secrecy  and  denial.  Most  others  in
the  regulated  community  just  had  to  accept  at  face
value  the  inaccurate  things  the  agency  had  said
publicly.

If that  is  truly  the  way  the  Secretary  can  be  ex-
pected  to  operate,  she  should  not  be  surprised  when
a  provider  such  as  Auburn  argues  that  the  equities—
her  agency’s  concealment—justify  tolling  the  appli-
cable  review  deadlines  until  the  time  when  a  reason-
ably  diligent  provider  knows  or  should  know  that  it
has  a  solid  basis  to contest  the  agency’s  calculation  of
the  Medicare  payment  due  that  provider.

33

CONCLUSION

Baystate  and  Cape  Cod  show  that  it can  take  years
and  the  occasional  breakthrough  litigation
out-
come—potentially  long  after  the  Secretary's  appeal
and  reopening  deadlines  expire  for  a  given  fiscal  year
determination—to  reveal  the  serious  errors  that  the
agency  has  proven  capable  of making,  particularly  in
the  work  that  it  performs  behind  closed  doors.  The
availability  of equitable  tolling  would  provide  modest
assurance,  in  appropriate  cases,  that  the  time  Medi-
care  providers  need  to  discover  these  mistakes  does
not  automatically  insulate  the  errors  from  review.
Southwest  therefore  urges  the  Court  to  affirm  the
D.C.  Circuit’s  decision.

Respectfully  submitted,
